# Thread: Is El Salvador the Modern Day “American Dream”?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-el-salvador-the-modern-day-%E2%80%9Camerican-dream%E2%80%9D.30897/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adolphinsbrain — 2025-05-13T17:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-el-salvador-the-modern-day-%E2%80%9Camerican-dream%E2%80%9D.30897/post-352434>

The America Gov is one slick bastard I’ll tell you that for sure. There was this show on Netflix I used to watch when I was a low dopamine fuck , it was called “Blacklist” and there was this character named “Raymond Redington”. Long story short, the way he made his needed relationships to survive being the #1 most wanted criminal world is , he would make up problems for people and then offer them a solution - knowing the entire time it’s his master mind plan that he had created.
Now.. how does this tie to our reality. I realized that this show telling us that our US Gov is the real “Ramond Redington” , I think so ( I know so). This Government is so fucked that it’ll be dumb for you to not want to get out of here... the fact that 5g towers are on top of buildings where people live and sleep is absolutely sickening , metaphorically and quite literally. They don’t really care about you nor your health. they don’t and it’s the truth. Uncle Jack as been saying this in between what he says ( or at least that’s what I hear when he talks) They might care a sliver about us bcus of the voting stuff but other than that once they’re in office, what they once said turns into our hands holding sand or they would half ass what they said. It took me 22 years of my life to really comprehend just at a surface level what the government really doing and their goal is. I don’t really understand it fully but I do know that what they do care about is money & power. Always has been. Uncle Jack is so right during or after COVID WE THE PEOPLE had a duty to fucking put our forks down and get together and get this country back, for the reasons our founding fathers did . It’s a fucking shame how Americans let it get this far ( obv thru Jabs , blue light, non emf, etc) but I can’t buy that ya , I cannot. ME.. I used to be the lowest DOPAMINE FUCK YOU CAN IMGAINE, i used to vape (first and last thing i did in bed) my circadian biology was straight fucked woke up 12-1 pm , gamed untill like 3/4 am. I wanted to be a profession call of duty player, I would stay in front of a blue screen monitor , right next to verizon router and still manage to understand there’s a way bigger game being played here ( us gov well more the Royal families and pentagon) trying to gain control of its citizens and country. I heard uncle Jack and I understood him crystal fucking clear. It’s time to get the fuck out of here and if you don’t. You will pay the price (we already are). Uncle jacks quotes/ wisdom are and is priceless. I can’t quote him bcus I don’t remember on the top of my head so I’m paraphrasing but nothing about us is mediocre so why would you settle for an Enviroment that’s an c and or d in most places of America.. Dr Jack telling us it’s possible (or that it is) we make light stronger than the sun and how we have a 30 million volt charge in our imm, like what the fuck. I almost choked on my own saliva. Our environment (if you live in a geo engineering n 5g state ) is literally containing our a potential and not only that but making us sick and dumb as fuck.
I am planning to leave america for good untill there’s absolute change.. if anyone could drop locations where a black swan mitochondrial could thrive on a quantum level please comment them. A jungle , island anything. I will make the move not for my human flesh and bones being but for my quantum computers. Not half assing this life and I need to be at full potential for not only myself but humanity. One day I’ll be needed and I need to be ready, I won’t be ready if I stay in New York.

# Thread: Feeling Totally Conflicted About My Career
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 55
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fouadfo1 — 2025-05-14T05:03:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-352467>

Hello everyone, I am currently a practicing pharmacist in the traditional sense. This past year I have come across Jack Kruse’s work and it’s been quite eye opening. This is in addition to all the revelations that have come out during the past few years that have really changed my perspective on disease and science and the many nefarious actors that work within medicine.

I went to pharmacy school because I really love biochemistry and pharmacology. I was under the impression that the human body is a system of levers and pulleys that should/ need to be manipulated to elicit a desired outcome for the benefit of the patient.

Obviously Dr Kruse has flipped my world upside down and I’ve learned that biochemistry and pharmacology are only a tiny part of the equation and the real story is within biophysics. I’ve missed the mark totally. And suddenly, the thousands of drugs I come across daily all seem to be useless at best and dangerous at worst. This is not including the handful of drugs that are actually beneficial (certain antibiotics, drugs used in surgery, pain killers for trauma etc).

But I’m left in a very peculiar position. I have a young family, a mortgage, loans and debts to pay off like everyone else. I honestly do not know what to do going forward. I am not a physician so I cannot just establish a clinic where I treat patients from a quantum biological perspective or even a functional medicine perspective. The thought of going to medical school almost makes me want to barf considering what the institution has become, in addition to the fact that I would need to rack up hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt to put myself through the medical school ringer.

I have no idea what to do going forward. I do not want to be part of this paradigm or model of treatment anymore, but I do not know where to go or what to do to earn a living.

Would love to hear your suggestions or maybe from someone in a similar position.

Thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-14T05:55:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-352469>

Bitcoin solves this.

### AlbertD — 2025-05-14T06:44:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-352471>

PharmD here. It is a tough pill to swallow.

Do what you gotta do to pay the bills. But know the long term plan.
I have a funny feeling that the whole world will have no choice, but to learn Jack's science. It's inevitable. Because no one is getting healthier. Everyone is getting sicker. Given autism rates, forget replacement rates. Society won't even be able to be run in several generations.

I wonder if RFK Jr. will create some sort of program similar to how \[insert tech company\] incentives hackers to hack their server to identify vulnerabilities. But create a similar incentive available to the public to find cures.

In the meantime, I do believe reading the blogs and learning this science has the potential to change every single industry. One cannot imagine the opportunities available because they as of yet, they do not exist. We are early.

I also find dating even harder now. It's inconvenient, but that's life.

### Adamo — 2025-05-14T13:33:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-352475>

> [AlbertD said:](/goto/post?id=352471)

>
> I wonder if RFK Jr. will create some sort of program similar to how \[insert tech company\] incentives hackers to hack their server to identify vulnerabilities. But create a similar incentive available to the public to find cures.
>
> In the meantime, I do believe reading the blogs and learning this science has the potential to change every single industry. One cannot imagine the opportunities available because they as of yet, they do not exist. We are early.
>
> I also find dating even harder now. It's inconvenient, but that's life.

I felt the same thing years ago coming from a "tech" background and working in IT.
Being around server rooms and computers 24/7 is NOT ideal in anyway, nor "healthy." When wireless and mobiles starting popping up everywhere, instinctually I was very uneasy.

Dating? Never mind the political/social bent people are in/on, but how many people are obsessed with the wrong things, jabbed up, on pills, addicted to escapism etc.... not to mention how many people are social-media-only-fans types... it's a strange place out there!

For the original OP, start moving in the direction that you want. Week after week, move closer and closer and it will begin to shape up more in the light that you want it to be.
Nothing happens overnight... so try not to pressure yourself so much.

### Steve Z — 2025-05-14T14:19:42+0300

Different industry, same story here. I supervise a large regional 911 center in my state. We use a lot of tech. Add in shift work and constant irradiation with blue light and nnEMF. All the trees that have died around my worksite are where RF readings are highest.

I recognize I'm on a different wavelength than most, am fine with that, and just keep working my plan.

### S Hudson — 2025-05-14T16:49:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-352481>

### harezlak19 — 2025-05-14T18:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-352486>

The way I understand the post is that you are not worried about your own "health implications" (ie, nnemf, RF, etc.) of working as a pharmacist, but rather the moral dilemna of filling out and providing drugs to people that could or will negatively impact their health without them being aware--

Not in the same situation, but I have worked and currently working in a field that I am not passionate about and feel that there is something else out there. In terms of finding something else, echoing Albert's post above---you need to do what you need to do in the short term to pay the bills and provide for your family. Long term, figure out something that your passionate about that will pay the bills. Unfortunately, this is only something that you can figure out for yourself. No one can tell you what will fulfill you as a human being but yourself. As Jack says "if youre not good for yourself, then who are good for."

> [Adamo said:](/goto/post?id=352475)
>
> Nothing happens overnight... so try not to pressure yourself so much.

Totally agreed---work on thinking about different options, trying them out as part time gigs, creating your own business, etc. Work the day to day with the intentions of finding something else.

Could not agree more in terms of dating, its a crazy world out there! Especially for those looking to start families.

### Wild Side Blue — 2025-05-26T18:36:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353373>

> [fouadfo1 said:](/goto/post?id=352467)
>
> Hello everyone, I am currently a practicing pharmacist in the traditional sense. This past year I have come across Jack Kruse’s work and it’s been quite eye opening. This is in addition to all the revelations that have come out during the past few years that have really changed my perspective on disease and science and the many nefarious actors that work within medicine.
>
> I went to pharmacy school because I really love biochemistry and pharmacology. I was under the impression that the human body is a system of levers and pulleys that should/ need to be manipulated to elicit a desired outcome for the benefit of the patient.
>
> Obviously Dr Kruse has flipped my world upside down and I’ve learned that biochemistry and pharmacology are only a tiny part of the equation and the real story is within biophysics. I’ve missed the mark totally. And suddenly, the thousands of drugs I come across daily all seem to be useless at best and dangerous at worst. This is not including the handful of drugs that are actually beneficial (certain antibiotics, drugs used in surgery, pain killers for trauma etc).
>
> But I’m left in a very peculiar position. I have a young family, a mortgage, loans and debts to pay off like everyone else. I honestly do not know what to do going forward. I am not a physician so I cannot just establish a clinic where I treat patients from a quantum biological perspective or even a functional medicine perspective. The thought of going to medical school almost makes me want to barf considering what the institution has become, in addition to the fact that I would need to rack up hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt to put myself through the medical school ringer.
>
> I have no idea what to do going forward. I do not want to be part of this paradigm or model of treatment anymore, but I do not know where to go or what to do to earn a living.
>
> Would love to hear your suggestions or maybe from someone in a similar position.
>
> Thanks

New here. I work as a contracts admin assistant and currently looking for another job. I have the same issue of hating my work.

I was a business owner as a wedding videographer and actually loved the work. However EDS hypermobility did bring me down. I couldn’t do the heavy lifting anymore and had to walk away. One of the saddest things to go through knowing my body couldn’t do it.

Also, one MD, who owns her own private practice, and put a lot of time into me. For the past 8 years, I have had a shit ton of Prolotherapy done. This MD is in her 60s and study under Dr. Hemwall himself in the early 90s. She’s a hero and save my life. She took a big risk on me for having 3 major accidents on top of EDS. Which she diagnosed me with it. I owe her my life and we both work for each other. We barter services. And to this day she has saved me over $88,000 at least.

Present time. I lost close to 20 years of my life with this on going issue. Although I still think I one area needs to be addressed due to left pelvic floor dysfunction of the adductor muscle pulling out of the pelvic floor. I’m hoping to see her this week for it.

I lost a lot of time from EDS and not being able to do things that I loved to do. I was really good at surfing, owning a business, and learning to read music and playing drums. At my age of 43 starting over sucks. Actually starting over is not the bad part it’s the pay of a job. I life a simple life with no kids or husband. And to get ahead of financial gain seems impossible. I’m a fighter and will beat this generational trauma or something.

I’m glad to learn more about Bitcoin. Hoping to catch a break at some point. And taking the next step after Prolotherapy. I would love to travel worldwide with my MD to tell the world there is hope for us. But there is a mother step with this and I need to do my part once again. Which is sun and understanding Uncle Jack.

Either way. You are not alone. And be thankful you had a good run of health and made a great living. Some had to struggle a bit. Not complaining but we are all people wanting something better in life. That’s time.

Cheers!
Crystal

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-30T05:43:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353564>

People often feel conflicted about their careers in today’s technocracy due to a mix of systemic pressures and personal dilemmas shaped by rapid technological and societal shifts that are coming quickly.

1.  Automation and Job Insecurity: Advanced technologies like AI and robotics are reshaping industries, automating tasks, and displacing jobs. Workers in fields from manufacturing to white-collar roles fear obsolescence, creating anxiety about relevance and stability. For instance, studies suggest 20-30% of current jobs could be automated by 2030, pushing people to constantly upskill or pivot careers.
2.  Mismatch Between Skills and Demands: The pace of technological change outstrips traditional education and training. Many feel stuck in roles misaligned with emerging needs or pressured to learn complex, ever-evolving tech skills (e.g., coding, data analysis) while competing with younger, tech-native generations.
3.  Work-Life Imbalance: Technocracy glorifies productivity and constant connectivity. Remote work, enabled by tech, blurs boundaries, leading to burnout. People struggle with the expectation to be “always on” while craving meaningful personal time, especially when tools like Slack or email tether them to work.
4.  Purpose vs. Profit: Modern careers often prioritize corporate goals or tech-driven efficiency over personal fulfillment. Many feel their work lacks meaning, especially in roles serving algorithms or profit-driven systems, clashing with a desire for purpose-driven impact. For example, gig economy workers often report feeling like cogs in a machine.
5.  Cultural Narratives and Pressure: Social media and tech culture amplify success stories (e.g., startup unicorns, tech moguls), creating unrealistic expectations. People feel conflicted when their career trajectory doesn’t match the “hustle culture” ideal or when they’re pushed into high-paying but soul-crushing tech roles.
6.  Economic Inequality and Reward Systems: Technocracy concentrates wealth in certain sectors (e.g., Big Tech and Centralized Healthcare), leaving others underpaid and undervalued. Teachers, caregivers, or creative professionals often feel stuck in less lucrative fields while tech elites thrive, breeding resentment and self-doubt about career choices.
7.  Choice Overload and FOMO: The digital age offers endless career paths (e.g., freelancing, tech startups, remote gigs), but this abundance leads to decision paralysis and fear of missing out. People question if they’re in the “right” career when bombarded with options online.
8.  Erosion of Human Connection: Tech-heavy workplaces can feel isolating, with algorithms or metrics dictating performance. This dehumanization clashes with the human need for collaboration and belonging, leaving workers conflicted about staying in sterile, tech-driven environments.

In short, the modern technocracy creates a tug-of-war between adapting to relentless change, chasing financial security, and seeking personal meaning. People feel trapped in this centralized system that demands constant reinvention while offering little stability or deeper purpose. Decentralized money will reduce the economic pressures that force people into misaligned careers. Most disaffected people still do not understand how money fixes this career issue. Being an entrepreneur is the path of the savage. Workers will feel less conflicted about pursuing meaningful work, as they’d have greater control over their financial destiny, reducing dependence on technocratic institutions.

### A Case for Hope in Challenging Times​

Ben Lytle

**{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Widespread fear and pessimism are more far-reaching than ever before in my lifetime.** You see it in people’s eyes as they go about their business with greater urgency. Or when they are more impatient with minor inconveniences. Hypersensitivity and intolerance make more topics off-limits. Optimistic people are a minority, even if they concede that things may worsen before they get better. It may surprise you that pervasive pessimism is not new, nor is the pandemic solely responsible. Despite record prosperity, developed countries have reported increased pessimism about the future for over a decade. Is pessimism justified, or could it be a form of mass hysteria? Either way, what can each of us do about it?

### Facts Support Optimism​

**_Living Conditions_**
The first Industrial Revolution, in the 1760s, led to widespread improvement in living conditions, with the majority of the population living in extreme poverty dropping from 80 percent to 20 percent by 1800. Recent progress has been even more staggering, with one billion people lifted from extreme poverty since 1990. Innovations in the pipeline promise continued improvement.

**_Working Conditions_**
Work was historically dangerous, tedious, repetitious, and physically demanding. Today’s workplaces can be stressful but are safer than ever. In three decades, most physically demanding, dangerous, and repetitive jobs will be automated. **“New Work” will be oriented toward creativity, problem-solving, and complex human relationships that are difficult to automate and better suited to tap human potential. There will be job losses during the transition, though likely not extreme, due to population declines in developed countries.**

_**Life Expectancy**_
**Life expectancy has more than doubled since 1900 and will likely double again in the next one hundred years.** Scientific progress using the fusion of medical research with automation in the diseases of aging and the aging process could lead to more than linear improvement in life expectancy. Innovations like driverless vehicles and remote health monitoring will also reduce premature deaths. Innovations will support healthier lifestyles through more effective diet, exercise, sleep- and stress-management programs, pharmaceuticals, and home and personal health monitoring innovations. As a result of these innovations, most people under 50 years of age today are likely to see their 100th birthday. Among today’s infants are likely the first people to reach 150 years of age.

**_Uncivilized Behavior_**
A common complaint today is that people are becoming uncivilized and more violent. Evidence supports the opposite conclusion. Brutal executions were public celebrations with children present only a couple of hundred years ago. Duels, to the death, settled many disputes. Bearbaiting and cockfighting were popular local “sports.” Roads were unsafe without private security. Slavery and indentured servitude were accepted worldwide. Wars routinely plagued much of the world until as late as the 1990s.

Large-scale infantry warfare is increasingly ineffective, as Russia has learned from its invasion of a much smaller Ukraine. War will continue to evolve into regional conflicts, proxy wars, and targeted battles between drones or special forces and terrorist cells. Bloodless but efficient and cost-effective cyber warfare is the likely long-term future.

Violent crime has fallen steadily for the last two decades. Most violent crime is highly concentrated in a handful of US cities, neighborhoods within those cities, hot spots within the areas, and among a few extremely violent career criminals. Recent spikes in violent crime have followed the same pattern, with violence largely spilling outward from hot spots.

Today’s geopolitical threats, rancorous political and cultural divide, and coarse, uncivil language are unnerving, but historically have been a prelude to better times. **We can take some comfort that socially accepted brutality and widespread lawlessness are unacceptable in most of the world following a decades-old and likely irreversible trend.

_End of Days_**
Some pessimists accept the arguments for optimism above but nevertheless envision end-of-the-world scenarios from a deteriorating environment, a superpower nuclear war, another pandemic, or some other catastrophe. Impending doom has been a popular fantasy since humankind’s earliest days. Perhaps the end-of-days scenarios are inventions of overactive egos struggling to fathom a world without them in it. History and rational thinking suggest that a humanity-ending event (if one ever arises) will be a threat we do not anticipate instead of one we recognize. **As resourceful creatures, we innovate to avoid or mitigate known threats.

_Possible Causes of Widespread Pessimism_**
Many of us have become irrationally fearful and hopeless. Worse, many people need to spread their gloom to others. **Let’s examine some possible causes of widespread pessimism.

_Projection?_**
Does our personal opinion of the future tell us accurately what the future will be? Or does it more accurately tell us about ourselves? Do we project inner anxiety and fears outward to create a distorted worldview? Are we so accustomed to instant gratification and the good life that we become fearful and angry when something disturbs it? Do we expect perfection, stability, and security that has never existed in nature and never will? Everyone is entitled to form their own judgments, but do you observe others or yourself defaulting to the negative instead of a balanced view of probabilities about what the future might hold?

**_Age-Related Pessimism?_**
Older people tend to be less optimistic. The world population is aging and declining everywhere except Africa and the Middle East as life expectancy improves and birth rates fall. Population decline and aging will become worldwide unless birth rates increase. **Age-related pessimism reduces the quality of life and increases the risk of disease and death in older persons.** Pessimistic older adults may influence younger people through their negativity.

**_Hunter-Gatherer Brains in a Digital World?_
Humans are hard-wired to be anxious and fearful—some of us more than others.** Death came early to our ancestors from animals and other humans, with minor injuries and diseases until the last hundred years. They survived and dominated the earth partly because their ego and shadow functions alerted them to danger and urged them to control their environment. The accelerating change of the last three centuries has altered our risks faster than our psychological anatomy can adapt. Our ego is unnecessarily aggressive, as demonstrated in board rooms, legislatures, checkout lines, and driving. Defensiveness and fear cause us to see danger where little exists and miss real emerging risks. Examples include pandemic responses and obsolete fears of overpopulation when underpopulation is the New Reality.

**_A Toxic Environment of Fear_
Fear sells and enables a culture of manipulation.** Far too many politicians use fear to gain power and demonize opposition instead of seeking wise compromise. Interest groups treat every minor setback as a catastrophe. Some religions warn that heaven is attainable only through their dogma. Many educational institutions foster elitism and narrow-mindedness instead of critical thinking, expanded perspective, and wisdom. News media unashamedly substitutes divisive propaganda for unbiased journalism. Social media promotes popularity over meaningful relationships while using algorithmic messaging to promote discord. Entertainment media often serves up coarseness, violence, and comic book fare instead of content that inspires or enlightens. Many advertisers use fear of disease and inadequacy to sell products. It takes a strong, wise person to remain positive despite unrelenting manipulation through fear.

**_Loss of Perspective_**
Many of us have tragically lost loved ones to COVID-19. Life today is challenging, with many unknowns. **But we forget that life has always been challenging and always will be, and yesterday’s challenges were much more severe.**

Compare today to 1914 through 1963. World War I killed 20 million people. As it ended, the Spanish Flu pandemic spread worldwide, causing a higher percentage of deaths in the population than COVID-19 today. The Spanish Flu killed young and healthy people like my twenty-eight-year-old grandfather. People were defenseless against the Spanish Flu, unlike the COVID-19 pandemic’s vaccines, therapeutics, and modern healthcare systems. Spanish Flu survivors faced the Great Depression less than a decade later with historic unemployment, shortages, suffering, death, and loss of hope. World War II and 70 to 85 million deaths immediately followed the Great Depression. The Cold War followed WWII when human extinction from nuclear war came very close from 1949 until the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1963.

Would any of us trade our twenty-first-century lives for those of the twentieth-century? Shouldn’t we find comfort in humanity’s three million years of unrelenting upward progress, even with its ups and downs? Shouldn’t probability alone suggest a 50-50 chance of a better future? Doesn’t the pace of today’s innovation (such as a COVID-19 vaccine delivered in months) increase the odds of a better future well past 50 percent?

**Is Pessimism Healthy?**
Does it matter if people are pessimistic? I think it matters a great deal. **Irrational fear and pessimism do not soothe, enlighten, or improve a single life.** Suicide is the second leading cause of death today among people aged 25 to 34. Does widespread pessimism and fear contribute to that tragic and unnecessary statistic? If so, the young deserve better from us. Irrational optimism is also unhelpful but extremely rare. Why not replace fear, pessimism, and negatively biased egos with critical thinking and an enlightened, balanced perspective of the past and future?

**_I’m only one person—what can I do?_
The world changes one person at a time, and that is true today more than ever.** Each of us has a vast megaphone through the Cloud, social media, and our traditional social circles. Speaking one’s mind has never been more socially acceptable. We can use our voices to encourage wisdom, optimism, and commitment to create a better world within our reach.

**_Elevate conversations to the bigger picture and the longer term._**
Humans are an ancient, resourceful, resilient species. An estimated 122 billion before us marched upward for three million years through valleys of despair to emerge each time higher and better. Our bottomless capacity for innovation overcomes seemingly impossible adversity and strengthens us each time for what lies ahead.

**_Become a beacon of hope for a more civil, wise society._
Speak to the positive when others generalize, demonize, or polarize.** Encourage healing over dividing. Remind others how negativity and fear play into manipulators’ hands. Defend differing points of view. Ask others to refrain from harsh judgments of those with whom they disagree. Those who disagree are not evil or stupid; they are only trying to find their way through a confusing, rapidly changing time. Speak to the innate good in most people. Describe heroes as the wisest among us instead of the nastiest advocates. The unwise browbeat to dominate. The wise respectfully disagree and communicate to achieve understanding.

**_Move beyond fear._**
One day, when I was in my thirties, I fretted with a wise, older friend during lunch. He said, “Ben, you are a strong man. Can’t you handle whatever life serves up?” The question rocked me. An instant flash of consciousness (an “Aha!” moment) changed me forever. I understood that I could indeed handle whatever life presented. Fear has never owned me since. If it can happen to me, it can happen to you. **Our unevolved ego keeps us fearful. We are stronger than we imagine, and our hearts are brave.

_Tell fear merchants—no more!_
Fear merchants only exist because we allow them to.** Change will come when a significant number of us turn them off, stop voting for them, or stop buying what they sell. Demand better. Write reviews and letters criticizing fear to manipulate. Say you expect better and will not purchase products or vote for anyone using fear to sell. If you work for such an organization, try to change their approach.

### dforsstrom — 2025-06-01T19:12:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353695>

> [fouadfo1 said:](/goto/post?id=352467)
>
> Hello everyone, I am currently a practicing pharmacist in the traditional sense. This past year I have come across Jack Kruse’s work and it’s been quite eye opening. This is in addition to all the revelations that have come out during the past few years that have really changed my perspective on disease and science and the many nefarious actors that work within medicine.
>
> I went to pharmacy school because I really love biochemistry and pharmacology. I was under the impression that the human body is a system of levers and pulleys that should/ need to be manipulated to elicit a desired outcome for the benefit of the patient.
>
> Obviously Dr Kruse has flipped my world upside down and I’ve learned that biochemistry and pharmacology are only a tiny part of the equation and the real story is within biophysics. I’ve missed the mark totally. And suddenly, the thousands of drugs I come across daily all seem to be useless at best and dangerous at worst. This is not including the handful of drugs that are actually beneficial (certain antibiotics, drugs used in surgery, pain killers for trauma etc).
>
> But I’m left in a very peculiar position. I have a young family, a mortgage, loans and debts to pay off like everyone else. I honestly do not know what to do going forward. I am not a physician so I cannot just establish a clinic where I treat patients from a quantum biological perspective or even a functional medicine perspective. The thought of going to medical school almost makes me want to barf considering what the institution has become, in addition to the fact that I would need to rack up hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt to put myself through the medical school ringer.
>
> I have no idea what to do going forward. I do not want to be part of this paradigm or model of treatment anymore, but I do not know where to go or what to do to earn a living.
>
> Would love to hear your suggestions or maybe from someone in a similar position.
>
> Thanks

There are other options! Find a way to get into functional medicine, naturopathy, and nutritional science. As a retired chiropractor of 43 years' experience, I wish I had added the functional medicine aspect to my practice, and I could still be working with people. Another program out there is with Global Healing, [www.GlobalHealing.com](http://www.GlobalHealing.com). He also has a certification program that you go through to become certified to use detox programs, and his product line is really pure to help people, much like a functional medicine approach but much cheaper and quicker then the other ways. [https://connect.xfinity.com/appsuite/#!!&app=io.ox/mail&folder=default0//xa}q0Fv&Rfurq)\]qtf](https://connect.xfinity.com/appsuite/#!!&app=io.ox/mail&folder=default0//xa%7Dq0Fv%26Rfurq\)%5Dqtf). Dr Group, the chiropractor behind this company, was on President Trump's medical advisory panel when COVID first hit in 2019 or 2020 and made functional medicine recommendations for covid, but was overridden by all the MD's. ### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-01T20:10:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353700>

### AlbertD — 2025-06-01T21:00:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353705>

Pursuing a career in healthcare is subjecting oneself anywhere from 1-20 years of hazing. Healthcare is a rule following profession. You are rewarded for following the rules and penalized for breaking them.

For that reason, creativity is rarely incentivized. Would you want your doctor to be creative with how they perform a surgery or write a prescription? But that assumes the current consensus is absolutely correct, which is far from the current status quo.

Degrees/Diplomas/certifications are all the same. I'm only interested in investing in the S&ME 500, not the S&P 500. Skills & beliefs are the only thing that will unlock the future waiting for me. I just need to subject myself to an unknown number of years in my "Rocky" cut scene. (It's hard and I don't know how long it will take.)

Regardless of what world order replaces the current one, I "feel" like skills will still be valued in the new world. I wonder if bitcoin will be valued more than skills in that new world. But it certainly doesn't hurt to have both.

### JulieBlueSky — 2025-06-02T09:03:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353734>

my son loves guitar, self taught, but hit a limit to his skills and so had some lessons with a classical spanish guitarist up the road from us just for a different angle and 18 months later is now teaching private guitar lessons in primary schools near us. Never would have thought or considered it possible, it just happened. Right place right time, and a cosmic reward to following passion.

### Moonbird71 — 2025-06-04T03:21:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-353857>

I'm loving this thread! It's very inspiring...I'm a simple restaurant manager of meager means, yet I see no matter what field or path your on career wise or financially, there is always something...I'm still learning, working on a plan to get out of California...I'm also an anomaly in my family... even estranged from longtime friends as my lifestyle evolves....very grateful for this forum...

### Carly18 — 2025-06-24T13:06:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-355142>

I work in an adjunct field to healthcare which, thankfully, in my current location is not regulated by the provincial government, yet. It is however, full of rule following sycophants that have built thriving businesses on completely unhealthy practices.
I’m trying to make money over the next 3 months but am definitely conflicted about it every single day I’m there.
Firstly, the clinics open during peak UV hours only and the hours never shift with the seasons. All the rooms are horribly lit and the natural light blocked out to keep the space cool and dark. The fact that there are even windows in clinics is a bonus because most owners I’ve talked to don’t believe windows are necessary in treatment rooms. There are half of the rooms in my current workplace without natural light though and most of them are taken first by therapists thinking working in the dark all day long is part of their routine. Plus, the nnEMF exposure is off the charts. Every room has a BT speaker, wifi throughout the space accessible to every client and their devices, online booking/billing/charting/scheduling/bookkeeping and a friendly neighborhood 5G tower nearby.
Secondly, the majority of the clients don’t understand what a proper therapeutic treatment should be. They prefer to be comfortable, pampered and asleep. They’re what I call “lotion nappers”. Just like most in the world, they want their aches and pains to disappear while someone else does the work and they sleep, naked and pampered on the table. Heaven forbid the treatment is not pleasurable or only slightly uncomfortable and they do nothing to contribute to their health improvements either during the massage or afterwards.This is how you build a thriving business though, catering to the masses delusional expectations of receiving health benefits without their own participation. They stay on the treadmill of using their health insurance while never truly being treated. Gotta use up all those benefits every year.
Thirdly, the use of oils/lotions on the skin is horrendous. If I keep this pace with “lotion nap” massages in the clinic I am in currently, I will easily go through a litre of coconut oil in a month. It’s all over my hands and forearms all day long and I stink of it when I get home. Completely unhealthy to clients and therapists. I haven’t even scratched the surface on the subject of vaccine shedding which I’ve now discovered I might as well have taken all the jabs anyway because of how much skin contact I’ve had with the compliant over the past few years.
Lastly, the medi spa business model has entered the chat within my industry to the detriment of all involved. When I see how indoctrinated people have become thinking a machine will solve weight problems and smooth away wrinkles, I cringe.
I niched my practice when I first got out of school specifically to take clients willing to endure a bit of pain in their treatments for much faster results. I couldn’t stand “lotion nappers” even back then and still can’t. I treat people who don’t want to waste their time sleeping away their life while being covered in oil and locked in a dark room for 1-2 hours in the middle of the day. Everyone receives their “medicine” while fully clothed and without “lube”.
Unfortunately for me, not everyone likes it that way.

### MayaB — 2025-06-25T02:25:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-totally-conflicted-about-my-career.30900/post-355207>

> [eve001 said:](/goto/post?id=353679)
>
> Same situation here. This is a forum full of skilled people with ethics. How about we use that to find potential synergies to create new jobs? What does that require? That we know ourselves and our skills; that we engage with eachother to know eachother and feel who may be a match and that we inspire eachother. What skills do you have and want to share in your next chapter? Do you people think you can solve this alone or with zombies? Some may survive alone. But will you thrive and feel fullfilled alone? For the person who started the thread: an idea should you have your own farmacy: could you use a small spot in it to introduce slowly new things. Like an indoor fountain for instance, or an artwork of a seascape? -- check some of Dr K's blogs on this for the vagal nerve -- I made plenty of great artful seascape photos myself b4 I had to ran away from 5G. I am still struggling to create a suitable PC workplace since I got so heavily EMF'd. How about we skilled people here network? How about we organize videocalls for that? I can moderate the call if in decent CEST time frame = away from bedtime and AM sun exposure time. I have experience w/ hosting/moderating/facilitating. But sure more people here can, and different time zones ideally would have their own videocall anyhow. So many wants and needs can be turned into projects or deeds if people decide to collaborate. I believe that anyhow. What would be a good group videocall app? I haven't done group calls for some years to heal/redox.

This really resonated with me! I'm feeling open to exploring new business opportunities that align with the principals discussed here. I am in a place where I have been running my own business for nearly 15 years (Archaeology consulting company) and have come to dislike it more and more as the years go on (mostly due to the gov legislation we have to work under rather than the archaeology itself - although I don't find Australian archaeology very riviting but being able to often work outdoors has its benefits). However, the business is profitable and allows me to work very flexible hours around my kids needs and allows us to travel frequently which is something we really enjoy. So it is a case of disliking the work but propping up an enjoyable lifestyle with potential for future proofimg/ability to purchase bitcoin etc. At some point I would like to switch to a more enjoyable career/business and am often wondering how I can use my current skillset/centralised pieces of paper (degrees/doctoral degree) to make a new move.

If I recall from skimming through _The Fiat Standard_, my takeaway is: just about all of us are screwing one another over, one way or the other, in some way, due to the time preference increasing effects of fiat money. On a fiat standard we all have to cannibalize upon each other to some extent. I recommend that book for its creative treatments of whole swaths of the debased society. So I wouldn't be too hard on myself. Conversely, on a sound money, maybe we would be screwing each other over less... though human finitude will always linger, perhaps we can better cohere.

I have been wondering the scale of civilization possible with a Bitcoin circular economy that Max Keiser keeps mentioning on X. I am partial to how great Byzantium seemed before the currency debasement - the longest lasting empire in recorded history. Would Bitcoin circular economies ever become like that?

# Thread: Success Story - Sleeping better
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-story-sleeping-better.30901/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mightygumshoe — 2025-05-14T16:16:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-story-sleeping-better.30901/post-352480>

Last year, mid-summer (before I knew who Dr. Kruse was -- sorry Dr. Kruse), I started using Red Light Therapy - I have a soft pad (weaker) and a box. Anyhow, after using the "box" by laying on the floor with it at the back of my skull and on my face for 20 minutes a day at least 5 times a week, I started sleeping 4 to 5 hours a night continuous within 1 week of use ( I have cats that disturb the peace and after waking me up by breaking something or being on my face I cannot get back to sleep). Those red light boxes (good brands) are awesome. In 8 minutes, my mind is quiet, and I can meditate, pray, or whatever. Then in December someone introduced me to Dr. Kruse, Dec 2024. I heard the podcast with Danny Jones etc. Then dug for more info. So, I did measurements (electric field, with voltmeter etc.) around my bedroom, and rest of house, and pulled all tech out of my room. Unplugged extension cords with extra jacks. No wifi, turnws odd lights near the bed, electric blanket (Michigan winter) removed, etc. Moved the needle a bit ...5 hours to maybe 6. Still awakened by cats, but went back to sleep. Added earthing last Fall but not regularly. In December put the mat on the bed. It helped too. Somewhere between 6 and 7 hours most nights, of sleep. From Dr. Kruse learned about getting up with the sun (I do my best to be out there within half hour of sunrise) and blue light blocking glasses. These last two were a gamechanger. I am sleeping 8 to 8.5 hours a night, sound, hard sleep!!!! On occasion a cat still gets me up, but rarer. Because I am sleeping so soundly. What a difference. Not since I was in my 20s have I slept this well. God bless you all.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-15T04:47:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-story-sleeping-better.30901/post-352520>

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/)

No wifi, turnws odd lights near the bed, electric blanket (Michigan winter) removed, etc. Moved the needle a bit ...5 hours to maybe 6. Still awakened by cats, but went back to sleep. Added earthing last Fall but not regularly. In December put the mat on the bed. It helped too. Somewhere between 6 and 7 hours most nights, of sleep. I enjoy having my cat around but that little demon goes in the garage at night. Best biohack to date.

### Mightygumshoe — 2025-05-15T18:44:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-story-sleeping-better.30901/post-352550>

> [MTA321 said:](/goto/post?id=352537)
>
> I enjoy having my cat around but that little demon goes in the garage at night. Best biohack to date.

I had a demon, that I lost to squamous cell in upper jaw just 6 weeks ago. He was a terror, threw ponytails he found on floor on my head to wake me up, put his whiskers up my nose, pulled my hair, destroyed 9 sets of wired earbuds, opened cupboards, pushed my book stack off the dresser...a nightly routine, and if I refused to get up, would start going for the tiffany style lamp. I had to prop pillows in front of my bedroom door to keep it open because he destroyed the "twangy" doorstop behind...and as I write this, I would allow it all again...to have my fluff ball back. 12 years old. Way too young and an indoor cat. Cancer sucks. Lost my husband years ago to the same type of cancer, but in the kidney (caused by stones, that is another story, was early 40s), incredibly rare in the USA, very common in parts of Africa (bad water) and sadly was misdiagnosed; idiot docs put a cancerous kidney back in his body saying was infection (squamous makes a layer, it is different, not hard tumor)...and could not get a lawyer to touch it because was a judgement call...very rare in USA....very hard to sue, at least in Michigan it is. They opened my husband 8 times in 10 months; those docs made hundreds of thousands on a man who had wounds all over. That was step one of me walking away from traditional medicine. Step two was when docs ripped a hole in my daughter's stomach, with meds and we walked away. Anyhow I digress, but I miss my cat; his sis misses him too. God bless. Cats are the coolest creatures on the planet. But a good night sleep is important. I am just grateful that with no tech in room, and the glasses I wear if reading online before bed...I am sleeping better than EVER before. And it's not because I lost demon fur; his sis has taken his role upon herself, as if he channeled her from beyond....Enjoy.

# Thread: Sleep quality impaired at high altitudes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/>

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Asael Enriquez — 2025-05-15T03:43:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/post-352512>

Hi,

Would living in a home that is at 7,200ft above sea level reduce sleep quality? After reducing electric fields in the bedroom close to .100mV and RF to under 5 micro watts per meter squared, it seems that I still have trouble sleeping at night and feel fatigued during the day. I find myself waking up at around 3:00am most nights and I'm unable to fall back asleep. I currently suffer from dysbiosis in the gut caused by yeast overgrowth, not sure if that would be related to what I'm experiencing. It seems that hiking and exercising is more difficult here when compared to lower elevation locations. When I drive down to West Texas, I stay at my parents house and sleep in a room there, no sleep issues whatsoever. The room at that house in Texas is painted with YShieldhsf54 EMF blocking paint.

### Mat.I — 2025-05-15T04:14:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/post-352513>

RF is on the low end and would still be in the "slight" concern range.. It could be a combination of things. The classic 3am wakeups are usually tied to stress axis (HPA) activation. Things to bare in mind at your elevation oxygen pressure is ~ 75% of sea level, this alone (for people who aren't built for this environment) would reduce oxygen delivery to tissues and cause issues that are tied to hypoxia e.g. stress axis activation could be hypoxia driven.. This will overall increase sympathetic tone. Also, there are variations in the earths mag field ~ 1% decrease at your elevation. This can also have affects on redox, vagal tone, and your brain clock genes.. Due to underlying gut issues you could not have much buffer for more stress as the yeast overgrowth produces acetaldehyde and ethanol which can shift you towards a FE3+ state (pseudo hypoxic) your baseline. In summary your baseline could already be redox compromised and the elevation stress pushes past your buffering capacity - triggering brainstem stress responses, poor vagal tone and interrupted sleep..

### Mat.I — 2025-05-15T04:17:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/post-352514>

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-15T04:25:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-quality-impaired-at-high-altitudes.30906/post-352515>

High-Altitude Effects on Oxygen and Physiology Mimics the GOE.

At 7,200 feet above sea level, atmospheric pressure is lower, leading to reduced oxygen partial pressure (PO₂). At sea level, PO₂ is about 159 mmHg (21% of 760 mmHg), but at 7,200 feet, it drops to around 120 mmHg (due to a pressure of ~570 mmHg). This causes:

Hypoxia: Lower oxygen saturation in the blood (SpO₂ typically 90-94% at 7,200 feet vs. 98-100% at sea level), reducing oxygen delivery to tissues, including the brain.

Increased Respiratory Rate: The body compensates by increasing breathing rate (hyperventilation), which can lower CO₂ levels (hypocapnia), leading to periodic breathing during sleep.

Oxidative Stress: Hypoxia can initially reduce ROS production due to less oxygen for the ETC, but compensatory mechanisms (e.g., upregulation of hypoxia-inducible factors) may later increase ROS, altering mitochondrial function.

My thesis hinges on oxygen-dependent UPEs sleep. At 7,200 feet, hypoxia could disrupt this process:

Reduced UPE Emission: With less oxygen available, mitochondrial ETC activity decreases, reducing electron leakage and ROS production. Since ROS are the primary source of UPEs (per Roeland Van Wijk’s mechanism), biophoton emission may weaken or shift in spectrum. UPEs in complex life are in the UV range (200-400 nm) with high information content (per Shannon’s theory). A reduction in UPE intensity or a broadening of the spectrum (less specificity) could lead to less precise wave function collapses in microtubules, impairing the neural patterns of sleep.

Altered Melanin-Sunlight-mtDNA Loop: Melanin’s role in absorbing sunlight and modulating mitochondrial metabolism relies on oxygen to produce UPEs. At high altitudes, lower oxygen levels could weaken this photon loop, reducing UPE-mediated signaling. This would likely disrupt circadian rhythms, as sunlight-driven UPEs are key to aligning sleep-wake cycles with environmental light.

CSF and Microtubule Effects: CSF distributes UPEs to microtubules, ensuring synchronized wave function collapses during sleep. If UPE emission is reduced due to hypoxia, the coherence of these collapses may decrease, **_leading to fragmented sleep states._** For example, the vividness of REM sleep (where UPE emission is higher) could be diminished, reducing dream quality and restorative effects.

Sleep Quality at High Altitude

High-altitude hypoxia is known to affect sleep quality in the literature, and this aligns with my thesis:

Periodic Breathing: At 7,200 feet, many individuals experience periodic breathing during sleep (Cheyne-Stokes respiration), where cycles of deep breathing alternate with pauses (apneas). This is due to hypoxia-induced hyperventilation lowering CO₂, which suppresses the drive to breathe until CO₂ builds up again. These apneas disrupt sleep continuity, reducing deep sleep (slow-wave sleep) and REM sleep, both critical for restoration.

Reduced Slow-Wave Sleep: Studies (e.g., Weil, 2005) show that at altitudes above 5,000 feet, slow-wave sleep decreases due to hypoxia and periodic breathing. In your model, slow-wave sleep may correspond to lower UPE emission with a specific spectrum, collapsing wave functions in a pattern that promotes restorative processes. Reduced UPEs at 7,200 feet would impair this, leading to less restorative sleep.

Fragmented REM Sleep: REM sleep, where UPE emission is higher in your theory, is also disrupted at high altitudes due to frequent arousals from apneas. This could reduce the vividness of dreams (linked to UPE-mediated wave function collapses) and impair memory consolidation, a key function of REM sleep.

Circadian Disruption: Lower oxygen levels may weaken the melanin-sunlight-mtDNA loop, disrupting circadian alignment. This could delay sleep onset or cause early awakenings, further reducing sleep quality.

Acclimatization and Individual Variability

The impact on sleep quality at 7,200 feet depends on acclimatization and individual factors:

Short-Term Effects: For someone newly arriving at 7,200 feet, sleep quality is likely to be significantly reduced for the first few days to weeks. Studies (e.g., Bloch et al., 2010) show that sleep fragmentation peaks within the first 1-2 weeks at altitude due to periodic breathing and hypoxia.

Acclimatization: Over weeks, the body adapts by increasing red blood cell production (via erythropoietin) and improving oxygen delivery. This may partially restore mitochondrial function and UPE production, improving sleep quality. However, periodic breathing often persists to some degree at 7,200 feet, especially during deep sleep.

Individual Factors: People with lower hypoxic ventilatory response (sensitivity to low oxygen) or pre-existing conditions (e.g., sleep apnea) may experience worse sleep quality. Conversely, those who are well-adapted to altitude (e.g., lifelong residents) may have mitigated effects, as their mitochondria may have adapted to produce UPEs more efficiently under hypoxic conditions. Likely related to Bohr effect and 2, 3 DPG changes.

Clinical Implications

Reduced Sleep Quality: Living at 7,200 feet is likely to reduce sleep quality, especially initially, due to hypoxia weakening the melanin-sunlight-mtDNA-UPE loop. This disrupts the spectral specificity and intensity of UPEs, impairing wave function collapses in microtubules and fragmenting sleep states (less slow-wave and REM sleep).

Long-Term Effects: Chronic hypoxia at 7,200 feet could lead to persistent mtDNA stress, similar to the nnEMF-induced mtDNA hypoxia you’ve described. This might alter UPE spectra over time, potentially reducing the vividness of conscious experience during sleep and wakefulness, and affecting overall brain health.

Mitigation Strategies: Enhancing oxygen availability during daytime via solar exposure should restore UPE production and improve sleep quality. Additionally, maximizing sunlight exposure to strengthen the melanin-sunlight loop may help, though the lower PO₂ at altitude will still limit mitochondrial efficiency.

# Thread: Hi from Sweden.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-sweden.30909/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### GazeofGzon — 2025-05-15T19:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-sweden.30909/post-352555>

Hi Guys.

My name is Åsa, this is a old viking name meaning godess, but im no godess, Im just a creative from Sweden the land of extremes, the land with dark winters and little day light and light summers where the sun hardly sets.
I have always been into "health" and my book shelf is filled with all the fad books from food gurus on diets and lifestyle but they never really sat right with me, they just seamed to miss something. now i know better now i know what was missing.
About a year ago i got quite disturbing sensory issues in my body i kinda figured out what that could be directly and instinctivly tossed my shoes off in middle of mid winter February, and upped my saunas and cold plunges. Even before getting a confirmed diagnosis 6 months later i had already got a nice tan lost 15kg and found Dr Jack and read his book and listened to many many podcasts.
Im splitting my weeks between Stockholm and my familys farm. I follow the epi paleo RX prescription and i watched the sunrise every day until now when the sun in freaking Sweden goes up at 4.00 and sets at 9.30 its really the toughest time so far. ( I gladly take advice on that )
One morning not long ago when the sunrise was at 6.00 i had a way to close encounter being chased by a big ass wolf, im not kidding you, it followed me all they way home to the door, things you get to se and experience at sunrise. As a spirit animal crossing your path its supposed to mean to trust your instincts. as I try to do every day in this work as i aiming to heal from this "blipp" with nature and questioning everything everything everything.
So far so good, now tanned like a little gingerbread man and actually feel that Im thriving, Im trying to learn and understand the work and navigating this site.

Appreciate you Dr Jack Kruse- with gratitude Åsa

# Thread: Hello from Butler PA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-butler-pa.30912/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lindgrentodd8 — 2025-05-15T21:18:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-butler-pa.30912/post-352561>

Hello everyone! My name is Todd Lindgren and I am here in a desperate grasp to reconnect with nature and a community of people in the same pursuit. It’s very sad that this is the case because when I was a little boy I felt very connected(when I was in nature) and completely fascinated by it when I wasn’t. Any time we were given in school to draw I spent that time drawing pictures of nature and when it came time to learn about the Native Americans I was obsessed(something about their lifestyle). My 3rd grade teacher saw that this was the case and decided to give the role of the chief in the thanksgiving day play we put on. I wore that big fancy feathered head dress with so much pride.

Around 5th grade I was decidedly labeled an unruly child by my parents and given adhd medication despite the fact(I clearly remember) that the psychiatrist that we seen said I was a completely normal child…at 13YO when my father took his own life(still taking medication) around this same time I got caught snorting in and giving my adhd drugs to friends….my mom took it away and a year later I found crystal methamphetamines. Needless to say my life took a turn for the worse for quite a long time(until 28 years old).

In 2016 I caught a 4 year prison term in the california state prison system and I decided to spend that time sober(for the first time since 14YO). It was like waking up from a horrible dream except the nightmare was the life I chose to live for so long.

The story is not all doom and gloom though. Before I went to prison I had already had my first child. In 2013 I made a desperate attempt to get away from the drug scene in California and went up to Alaska to work. I met my now 3 children’s mother on the plane up to Alaska(where she was from). My first child was a huge motivation(by far the biggest) for me to make the choices I did while incarcerated.

Almost 10 years later I’m still sober and have an insatiable passion and curiosity for life and a deep desire for connection to others with a similar mindset.

I started a nonprofit early 2024 that would serve individuals like myself(in recovery)…I’m happy to talk more about it but to keep a long story short and relevant to the story of how I ended up here I tried to get everything with the nonprofit up and running by fundraising(which I have no experience with), got discouraged and decided to take a break and focus on my current full time job as a commercial driver in the oil and gas industry.

Well being a commercial driver I have a lot of time to listen to podcasts. This where I run into jacks tetragrammaton podcast with Andrew and Rick and I was consumed immediately. I have spent the last six months looking up terms like cytochrome, bandgap, inverse square law, NADH and paramagnetic. Boy when I started investing 7 years ago I thought economics and monetary policy was the most interesting thing in the world but that couldn’t be farther from my truth today. Time for money is the dumbest trade I could make…especially with the beautiful family I have.

So I pulled the small amount of money I had invested out and bought a truck and am going to get a camper today and will be doing everything I can to reconnect me and my family back to nature….i think I may have a melanoma and figured that would a great start if this is the case. I remember Jack saying somewhere that a biopsy is the dumbest thing you can do so does anybody know what papers I should be reading if I’m looking to try and figure this out on my own without going too far into the meat grinder?

I suppose I will have to go get some tests done by a doctor but I am more terrified of them speeding up the process and me dying in a hospital than dying happy out in nature while having conversations with my 10-year-old daughter about evolution, mitochondria and entropy.

I’m already trying to keep up with Jacks new posts on Patreon and going back and starting to dig into tensegrity and cold thermogenesis but was wondering if anyone could give me a little nudge in the right direction.

Happy to be here and I’m not just looking for answers to my possible problem so anyone that just wants to say hi feel free considering this IS a meet and greet! Thanks and looking forward to meeting some like minded folks!

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-15T23:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-butler-pa.30912/post-352568>

welcome Make some paragraphs.

### lindgrentodd8 — 2025-05-15T23:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-butler-pa.30912/post-352573>

Yeah, I guess that would improve readability. I will work on that.

How dangerous?

How do we really work?
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Cells control light interactions via their surfaces (e.g., eyes,skin,gut), chromophores, cytochromes, opsins, water state, and oxidation states, delivering light to mtDNA for processing. This light-driven process, not genes, sculpts phenotype, genes act as “lenses” modulating light’s effects. In my decentralized thesis, UPEs collapse wave functions in mtDNA, predicting cellular outcomes with precision when light is controlled (e.g., narrow UPE spectra in health).

Disruptions like blue light or nnEMF (as in Studgardt Disease or blue light toxicity) broaden UPE spectra, impairing this control and leading to dysfunction (e.g., lipofuscin, demyelination, altered consciousness). The 2024 Nature book review lsupports this light-centric view, emphasizing biophotons’ role in life, aligning with my paradigm that light, not genes, is the primary sculptor of life.

Everything youre made of is a lens for light.

LIDAR IS DEADLY, as result.

# Thread: Gauss Reading in El Salvador
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauss-reading-in-el-salvador.30919/>

**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Alain Dwight — 2025-05-16T17:35:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauss-reading-in-el-salvador.30919/post-352621>

Anyone taken a gauss reading down here, what were the results?

I tried with my Cornet and only got 0.2 µT which I think comes out to 0.002 Gauss, less than the Earth's magnetic field, I'm assuming it's designed to pick up magnetic fields off AC rather than the Earth's DC field

### Anthony Smith — 2025-05-16T19:14:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauss-reading-in-el-salvador.30919/post-352634>

> [Alain Dwight said:](/goto/post?id=352621)
>
> Anyone taken a gauss reading down here, what were the results?
>
> I tried with my Cornet and only got 0.2 µT which I think comes out to 0.002 Gauss, less than the Earth's magnetic field, I'm assuming it's designed to pick up magnetic fields off AC rather than the Earth's DC field

“Down here” is a big place.
Every location, street, beach, house, room will be different.
I read 8mG+/- walking, even driving down the street under power lines.
Earths magnetic field is natural DC, power lines in ES put out an 60Hz AC magnetic field.
If the wiring is good in a house the magnetic field should be below 0.2 mG (0.02µT).
A continuous prolonged 4+ mG field is associated with Leukemia in children.

### Jacks — 2025-05-16T20:48:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauss-reading-in-el-salvador.30919/post-352652>

> [Alain Dwight said:](/goto/post?id=352635)
>
> Interesting! What I'm trying to do is get a reading of how much the volcanic properties of El Salvador increase the DC magnetic field of the Earth, if at all, like Earth is normally 0.5 gauss at the surface, but is it more in El Salvador?

This is all I found on magnetic anomaly maps of north and central america but does not cover El Salvador, but you might be able to infer from areas to the north that are similar geologically. Red is a positive anomaly - so greater than "average" magnetic field. As you can see the magnetic field varies significantly from place to place.

### [North American Magnetic Anomaly Map](https://mrdata.usgs.gov/magnetic/map-us.html)

mrdata.usgs.gov

However, looks like a magnetic survey was done for the southeast side of ES ([https://getech.maps.arcgis.com/apps...deb775a74d03ab9992a9d410032d&find=El-Salvador](https://getech.maps.arcgis.com/apps/webappviewer/index.html?id=85abdeb775a74d03ab9992a9d410032d&find=El-Salvador) - site shows the extent of the survey) but I could not find the actual map of the magnetic anomalies for this area.

### Mat.I — 2025-05-17T11:16:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauss-reading-in-el-salvador.30919/post-352706>

You need a low range high resolution "DC" gauss meter to measure the earth mag field

# Thread: Getting out of Dodge Club

### BiohackersTO — 2025-05-16T20:30:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-out-of-dodge-club.30922/post-352646>

Hello: I'm writing from Toronto, Canada, and for obvious reasons, I am planning to leave here soon.

For the lady who was on the February member call, who didn't understand what was so terrible about Canada, if you are interested, I would be happy to meet with you over coffee/tea to explain it to you (someplace in the Greater Toronto area).

For everyone else, I would like to collaborate on escape plan strategies. People from other areas are welcome to contribute.
I am currently researching the following and would like to connect with folks and share notes:
\- Physical destination: El Salvador
\- Virtual destination (for registering an online business) Prospera, Honduras

Does anyone have any obvious red flags about registering a business in Prospera?

So far, the advantages I see, include operating a business away from Canada, lower taxes and reduced reporting requirements. So far, it looks like a great place from which to operate an online, Bitcoin only business. So for all you health practitioners and influencers out there, this could be a good place to register an online business and receive bitcoin payments for your online products. I am still reviewing the information I have received from Prospera and will consult with an accountant, but so far, it looks like it would drastically reduce reporting requirements and taxation on such a business.

To be clear, I am only interested in registering a business in Prospera. As a physical destination, it sounds like an EMF hell-hole, so I have no plans to live there. But it sounds promising for registering a business.

Please respond if you know something I don't know and/or if you would like to participate in a get-out-of-Dodge strategy planning group.

### BiohackersTO — 2025-05-21T22:26:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-out-of-dodge-club.30922/post-353081>

This message goes out to **Nathalie in Quebec from the April members pow-wow call**: Uncle Jack suggested Strike app but I don't think that is available to us in Canada.

Some good services for buying Bitcoin in Canada include Bitcoin Well and Bull Bitcoin. For both of these, you need to set up your own wallet first and when you purchase through either of these services, they send your Bitcoin to your non-custodial wallet. BTC Sessions YouTube channel covers wallets, Bitcoin Well, and Bull Bitcoin.

This is KYC (Know Your Customer) Bitcoin and sometimes the banks are jerks about payments made to Bitcoin Well or Bull Bitcoin. But you call the bank's customer service and get it worked out, for the most part. Some people say it works better through a credit union.

Some of Ben (BTC Sessions) videos say you can get non-KYC from these sources. That might still be possible with Bitcoin Well ATMs (but the fees are higher). In the past, Bull Bitcoin offered this service through making a payment through the post-office. I think they now ask for full KYC info at the Post-Office, but if this option is still available, at least it de-centralizes that personal information a bit.

That said, if you have a viable non-KYC option, that is always better. Just don't do anything risky like trust strangers to obtain non-KYC bitcoin.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-13T04:38:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-out-of-dodge-club.30922/post-360949>

Strongly believe our parents are the last humans who will ever retire. They know it. You know it. And it's because the math is fundamentally broken. Life expectancy up. Birth rates down. Pension funds gambling on 8% returns while bonds pay 2%. Every government running the same Ponzi hoping they're not in charge when the music stops. But also, and here's where it gets darker, we don't want retirement anymore. IMO when people say "I want to retire early" I think what they really mean is that they want optionality. The "dream" isn't to stop working at 65 it's to stop taking orders at 35. So when Bitcoiners say "Bitcoin is my retirement plan" I don't think they really mean a literal retirement plan because retirement isn't a thing anymore. It's an escape velocity plan. You don't need enough to never work. You need enough to never need to work. The 401k is a museum piece. The pension is a fairy tale. We're all going to work until we die OR build something that works while we sleep.

### S Hudson — 2025-10-13T23:50:42+0300

You don't need enough to never work. You need enough to never need to work. The 401k is a museum piece. The pension is a fairy tale. We're all going to work until we die OR build something that works while we sleep.

Every time I tell people I quit dentistry at 45 and retired from paid work they give me a blank look and always ask "but what do you do?" There are so many who can't grasp the fact that life is filled with much more than a drive to and from an office 5 days a week. Suffer 5 to enjoy 2 is a ludicrous concept, especially when most people's idea of enjoyment is toxic to them

and you mentioned in a post recently on becoming a beach bum... and something in my head clicked. I've suddenly become obsessed with the idea of sitting on a beach and learning to draw (which is something i was obsessed with until the university system chewed me up and spat me out).

So, that's what i'm going to do
Looking forward to hearing when your new clinic is up and running
