# Thread: Anyone Bought “Photography” Incandescents Lately? BulbAmerica or Better Sources?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mark1969 — 2025-05-20T01:47:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/post-352953>

A while back, I swapped out all the incandescent bulbs in my house—not because of regulations, but mostly for convenience. I liked being able to control lights with my phone, change colors, or just tell a smart speaker to turn things on and off. It felt like a quality-of-life upgrade at the time.

But lately I’ve been rethinking it.

The more I learn about how light affects our biology—especially stuff around circadian rhythms and artificial light hijacking natural cycles—the more I wonder if we traded something important for tech convenience. The vibe of incandescents, the warmth, the simplicity… maybe that mattered more than I thought.

I found a site called **BulbAmerica** that sells incandescent bulbs for photography or “rough service” use—apparently still legal under those labels. Has anyone tried them? Are they comparable to the old-school bulbs in quality and feel?

And if I want to use them with smart switches or dimmers, are there any specs I should look out for?

Would love to hear about any experience, tips, or better sources. Trying to find a middle ground between function and circadian sanity.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-05-20T02:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/post-352961>

It was good talking with you earlier.
Anything “smart” is bad for you. Dimmers create a LOT of dirty electricity AND the more you dim a light the more it flickers.
IF you want/need artificial light (it is all bad compared to sun light) I would look for quartz/halogen bulbs.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2025-05-20T15:43:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/post-352995>

Why halogen over regular incandescent?

### Anthony Smith — 2025-05-20T16:43:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/post-353001>

Availability - depends where you live.
Halogen are incandescent.
Available in 12v DC - no AC field (little to no flicker) I have mostly DC lighting in my place
Use half the electricity.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2025-05-20T16:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/post-353003>

My understanding with incandescent flicker was that it wasn't a big concern due to the filament remaining relatively hot during off cycles. So, the amplitude change in light intensity is not that significant. Is there something I am missing here?

### Anthony Smith — 2025-05-20T19:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bought-%E2%80%9Cphotography%E2%80%9D-incandescents-lately-bulbamerica-or-better-sources.30951/post-353018>

EVERY bulb is a compromise . . .
Use the slo-mo video on your phone and record the bulbs you have. Incandescent are not as bad as some LED’s but they still ficker.
Some of the new LED’s do not flicker BUT they also have little to no NIR and are mostly blue dominant.
IMO DC incandescents are the best (compromise).

For newbies - the sun is 42%+/- IR 5%-10% UV, changes color throughout the day and does not flicker . . .


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Covid shot decline
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-shot-decline.30952/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ryangreggw — 2025-05-20T04:03:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-shot-decline.30952/post-352966>

Prior to May 2021, I had no health issues. I never took medications, avoided acetaminophen, and while I did drink a couple of bottles of alcohol per week (1.5L), I was otherwise healthy. I was never sick, not even a cold, had no allergies or sensitivities, and consumed large amounts of meat, eggs, sauerkraut, and kimchi. I worked out twice a day, went backcountry skiing on weekends, and lived for months at a time on MREs. In May 2021, I was given an ultimatum: receive the COVID vaccine or be fired and stripped of my benefits. I chose to comply and received the Pfizer COVID-19 vaccines in May and June 2021.

Within three months, I began experiencing severe food sensitivities. I reduced my diet by 90%, limiting myself to chicken, non-iodized salt (I could no longer tolerate iodine), flash-frozen blueberries, honey, broccoli, and carrots. For the next year, I took approximately six Benadryl tablets daily to manage symptoms. When I finally saw my doctor, he prescribed cetirizine and montelukast.

By April 2024, my tolerable diet had narrowed further to only organic chicken, plain organic gmo-free white rice (no seasonings, butter, or oils), and reverse osmosis (RO) water. My body could not tolerate any other type of water, presumably due to the mineral content, as I had unsuccessfully tried supplementing with various forms of sodium, magnesium, potassium, and others.

I’ve been diagnosed with follicular carcinoma and have undergone a thyroidectomy. I declined radioactive iodine treatment after learning about the research of Drs. McKernan and Bukhaults. Despite the removal, my thyroid gland appears to have regenerated symmetrically (confirmed by 3 separate ultrasounds, one done by a tech, another by my endocrinologist, the last one by my surgeon, and t3/t4/tsh/thyroglobulin tests). I'm currently awaiting an MRI consultation to determine whether this is actually another tumor masquerading as a thyroid gland. The last time I had a contrast-enhanced scan, my arm went into convulsions, my bicep felt like it was going to explode, I lost feeling for a week, and it felt like my arm was melting.

My testosterone is 283, low Vitamins A/C/D, and the only other seemingly significant abnormal lab was VIP (76pg/mL; normal is < 36). I now require 16 ampules of cromolyn sodium, 20 mg of cetirizine, 10mg of montelukast, and 15 mg of prednisone daily just to tolerate my two remaining safe foods, drink ro water, and stay awake for more than four hours at a time. I tried Xolair, but since then, my inflammation has become so severe that exposure to artificial fragrances, cause me to pass out within 30 minutes if I don't take 10mg-50mg prednisone (churches and airports are the worst).

Deviating from my current diet causes a rapid cascade of debilitating symptoms: severe acne, brain fog, fatigue, diarrhea (I only have one bowel movement per week), facial pressure that feels like my cheekbones are being crushed, and extreme inflammation in my lower back. This inflammation worsens the herniated/bulging discs and arthritis I sustained from jumping out of planes. On bad days, I have to slide out of bed and use my dresser to pull myself to my feet.

My condition has severely impacted my personal life. I can’t kiss my fiancée if she wears chapstick. We’ve never had coffee together or shared a meal in the past two years. To manage symptoms, I do cold plunges every other day (38–42°F for 15–30 minutes; I avoid submerging my hands and feet due to prior frostbite in Alaska but ensure full submersion up to the neck). I meditate regularly, although I’ve stopped using frequency-based meditation due to concerns over non-native EMFs (963 Hz resonated strongly with me). I eliminate EMF exposure in the bedroom by turning off Wi-Fi and all Bluetooth-enabled devices at night. I've stopped wearing headphones and sunglasses. I tried infrared saunas, but they caused increased inflammation. I've also used mitored red lights however I only did it for a few months and have not yet seen any results. I bring my own RO water and sheets wherever I go, though my travel is severely limited, I’m essentially tethered to my home.

I've tried multiple supplements and foods: vitamin D (which I didn’t tolerate orally, so I’m planning IV therapy next, banana bags were helpful when I tried one six months ago), wild salmon, oysters, regeneratively farmed eggs and bone broth, organic cabbage, and various elemental formulas (Neocate Splash, Vivonex, Vital AF). I’ve also tried Celtic sea salt (I have severe reactions to salt which usually results in not being able to tolerate one of my foods), cod liver oil, DHA (Dropi brand, amber glass bottle), quercetin (Pure Encapsulations—gluten-free and hypoallergenic), vitamin C (organic camu camu), DAO enzyme, iron, zinc, allicin, and organic inulin. I even worked with a functional nutritionist last year, not helpful.

I get daily sunlight in the morning and afternoon. Despite all of this, I am still limited to two foods: regeneratively grown beef with liver and heart (since adding this, I noticed the return of body odor when I sweat, perhaps due to the biodiversity from the cattle’s diet), and organic coconut milk (2 lbs or two cans daily, costing roughly $1,000/month). I stopped tolerating bison after starting monoclonal antibody treatment in February and discontinued Xolair last month. I can no longer tolerate any other organic meats or fish.

I continue to lose weight and am only able to work out twice per week (maybe this is optimal for longevity though). I cook my meat from frozen in a stainless steel pressure cooker using RO water, then pat it dry to remove excess oils and recook it (if I reheat in the microwave I experience adverse reactions as mentioned above). I haven’t consumed caffeine since July 2024 or alcohol since August 15, 2021. I wear blue light-blocking glasses after 5 p.m., use only hypoallergenic, fragrance-free detergents, body wash, and shampoo, and brush my teeth with fluoride/titanium dioxide free toothpaste. I’ve never regularly worn sunscreen and still don’t.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-20T05:30:41+0300

you need paragraphs and spacing.

> Prior to May 2021, I never took medications, avoided acetaminophen, but drank a couple bottles a week. I was never sick, not even a cold, never had allergies or sensitivities, consumed copious amounts of meat, eggs, sauerkraut, and kimchi. I worked out twice a day, went backcountry skiing, and lived for months off mres. May 2021 I was given an ultimatum, receive the covid shot or be fired and stripped of my benefits. I made the choice to get the shots, received the Pfizer Covid shots in May and June 21. Within 3 months I was having food sensitivities, reduced my diet by 90% to chicken, non iodine salt (couldn’t tolerate iodine anymore), flash frozen blueberries, honey, broccoli, and carrots. I took approximately 6 Benadryl a day for the next year. Finally went to see my doctor, he prescribed me cetirizine and montelukast. By April 2024 I was only tolerating organic chicken, rice (no seasonings, butter, olive oil, etc), and ro water (body doesn’t tolerate any other water, presumably the minerals as I’ve tried supplementing with various forms of sodium, magnesium, potassium, etc). Fast forward to today, I was diagnosed with a follicular carcinoma, had a thyroidectomy (denied radioactive iodine treatment after hearing about
> Mckernann and buckhault), testosterone is 250, vitamin D is in the 20s, my thyroid gland has apparently regenerated perfectly symmetrically (waiting on mri consult to see if it’s actually another tumor, as the last time I had a scan with contrast my arm went into convulsions, my bicep felt like it was going to explode, lost feeling for week, and my arm felt like it was melting), and I take 16 amuples of cromolyn sodium, 20mg ceterizine, 15mg prednisone daily to consume my two foods, drink water, and stay awake for more than 4 hours. I tried Xolair and now I have such severe inflammation that when I’m around artificial fragrances, I pass out after 30 minutes (church and airports are the worst). If I deviate from my diet (I’ve been doing 10ppm ddw after reading Gabor’s book to see if it’d do anything, I’ve had acne and welts all over my upper body since starting; I’ve also reduced overall water consumption from 1gal/day to avoid dilution), I get severe acne, brain fog, fatigue, diarrhea (I only have one bowel movement a week), my cheek bones feel like they’re being crushed in a vice, and such severe inflammation in the lower back exacerbating the herniated/bulging discs/arthritis from jumping out of planes, that I have to slide out of bed and use my dresser to pull myself to my feet. I can’t kiss my fiancé if she puts on chapstick, I’ve never gotten coffee with her, and I’ve never ate a meal with her in the past 2 years. I do the cold plunge every other day(38-42 degrees for 15-30min; can’t submerge hands and feet because of frost bite from time in Alaska but I make sure everything else up to my neck is submerged), meditate (although I’ve stopped with frequencies because of nnemf, 963 really resonated with me, no electronics in the bedroom, WiFi gets turned off at night as well as all electronics with WiFi/Bluetooth), tried IR saunas but they make me inflamed, stopped wearing headphones and sun glasses, bring my own sheets and ro water wherever I go, which isn’t far because I’m tethered to my home at this point. I’ve tried vitamin D supplements (body doesn’t tolerate so IV vitamin D is next, banana bags worked last time I had one 6 months ago, so we’ll see), salmon, oysters, regeneratively farmed eggs and bone broth, organic cabbage, elemental formulas (neocate splash, vivonix, vital af), Celtic salt, cod liver oil, dha (dropi brand, amber glass bottle, quercetin (pure encapsulations; gluten free, hypoallergenic, etc), vitamin C, organic camu camu, dao, iron, zinc, allicin, organic inulin (went down the functional nutritionist route last year), I get morning and afternoon sunlight and spend my free time out in the sun, I can only eat two foods (although still experience inflammation; persistent acne (20+ zits a day) and brain fog: regeneratively grown beef with liver/heart (noticed I got my BO back when I sweat, after starting the regeneratively grown beef, maybe because of the biodiversity of the cattle’s diet) and organic coconut milk (2lb/2can daily (roughly $1,000/mo), stopped tolerating bison after starting monoclonal antibody in February, stopped the Xolair last month, don’t tolerate any organic meats or fish, still losing weight and only working out twice a week, I have to cook meat from frozen in stainless steel pressure cooker with ro water, pat it down to remove oils then recook), haven’t had caffeine since July 24 or alcohol since 15aug21, use blue light blocking glasses after 5pm, all detergents/bodywash/shampoo is hypoallergenic fragrance free, tooth paste is titanium dioxide/fluoride free, I’ve never really worn sunscreen and still don’t.

Ryan - did you say what part of the world you live in?

\-minimizing or eliminating nnEMF in my environment as much as possible
\-outside sunrise to sunset all day every day, non-negotiable
\-Grounding 3-4 hours per day, especially during first 30 min of sunrise. (really need to make sure you have no EMF in your environment for this to actually work. Cities and most populated neighborhoods won't work anymore)
\-all lights go off after sunset. Candles or nothing.

# Thread: Andrew's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andrews-journal.30960/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mdonnelly718 — 2025-05-20T23:20:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andrews-journal.30960/post-353030>

Hi everyone, Mike Donnelly here. I joined Jack's community because my 2 1/2 year old son has been diagnosed with being on the autism spectrum. Delayed milestones. Still non verbal, walks on tippy toes, only makes random noises with no words. We're from Queens, NY our whole lives, both my wife and I. Recently moved to the Orlando area. Here's a breakdown:

\- Me- 40 year old father, lives in Queens my whole life. Was an athlete growing up, after college basketball turned to vices. Was addicted to percocets for four years. Total drug addiction for approx 10 years and have been clean for almost ten years now. No real history of health issues for me other than addiction

\- Wife - 34 years old. Was vaccine injured by Hep B vaccine about 15 years ago. Tinnitus, hives, vertigo all occasionally resurface even though she was able to heal herself primarily through changing her diet/supplement routines.

\- Andrew - C section baby. did not turn during pregnancy and we were coerced into c section. no vaccines for him other than vitamin K shot. Few ultra sounds were done during pregnancy. Was all good hitting milestones until about 18 months. We didn't realize how behind he was with certain things until this point.

\- Since finding out something was wrong with him in December, we've elimnated gluten and dairy from his diet. giving him zeolite.

\- Since discovering Jack on Danny Jones roughly around the same time, we've relocated from Queens to the Orlando area. Rarely any screen time for him. In our home here, we don't really use the lights indoors due to natural light. Getting him morning sun exposure every morning for atleast 1 hour. Trying to get him a total of 3-4 hours outdoors depending. Buying locally sourced fish(grouper caught in water in Florida) and beef(local farm) along with eggs from this area as well. Found a natural spring not too far away and am going to try to collect water from there. Unplugging router at night. He does have a baby monitor in his room so we can watch him at night which I know may not be the greatest. Goal is to bring him to the beach once a week so he can ground in ocean/sand.

Any thoughts and suggestions are welcome. Just to throw it out there, after discovering Jack and his teachings, it intuitively makes sense to me. We're so disconnected with the natural order of things. It makes sense why certain things are happening.

I've left my home, my parents, my friends, my favorite restaurants and everything I love because I'm betting on the fact that my son WILL heal if he is placed in the right environment. The pain of remaining the same was much greater than the pain of change, so I decided to take the leap of faith.

### Mat.I — 2025-05-21T02:14:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andrews-journal.30960/post-353034>

> [mdonnelly718 said:](/goto/post?id=353030)
>
> Unplugging router at night. He does have a baby monitor in his room so we can watch him at night which I know may not be the greatest.

This is not good enough. Hardwire the internet and get rid of Wifi. Get rid of the monitor and move the cot/bed into the room where you sleep. You are cooking your kid... Also, if you are not familiar with nn-emf and how to measure make sure you have a "competent" building biologist do an assessment to SBM guidelines (attached)..

### Mat.I — 2025-05-21T02:39:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andrews-journal.30960/post-353036>

The could be linked to disrupted thalamocortical coherence & brain stem autonomic disconnection.. All likely redox based so I would personally act quick and be a strict as possible and read the blogs etc..

These areas require optimal redox state to function well.

Just a few comments:

Make sure your grounding, doing the sunrise and getting as much sun as possible and staying dark at night. No ALAN.. Switch the screens to books or sensory type toys.

You need to up DHA from other wild caught sources like mackerel, wild shrimp, sardine, oysters and roe etc..

Do barefoot walks in nature under trees or next to flowing water and lots of barefoot play

Try mild cold exposure (not forced) just dress a bit lightly when it is cooler..

Get him around animals (preferably horse type therapy for kids)

Look at craniosacral therapy (for kids)

All the best

### mdonnelly718 — 2025-05-21T20:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andrews-journal.30960/post-353072>

thank you for your input. I appreciate it

Found a natural spring not too far away and am going to try to collect water from there. Unplugging router at night. He does have a baby monitor in his room so we can watch him at night which I know may not be the greatest. Goal is to bring him to the beach once a week so he can ground in ocean/sand.
>
> Any thoughts and suggestions are welcome. Just to throw it out there, after discovering Jack and his teachings, it intuitively makes sense to me. We're so disconnected with the natural order of things. It makes sense why certain things are happening.
>
> I've left my home, my parents, my friends, my favorite restaurants and everything I love because I'm betting on the fact that my son WILL heal if he is placed in the right environment. The pain of remaining the same was much greater than the pain of change, so I decided to take the leap of faith.

How hard is to bring your baby to sleep in the same room off course not in the same bed. at night we heal our selves and love ones too
if the problem is language then look for people that speak "24 languages" they did brain scans on him you can LOOK those scans
JACK's quote here " A Thought Changes Reality "

**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-21T02:53:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pierre-kory-work-with-dmso.30962/post-353038>

Jack, Is the DMSO therapy worth pursuing, as Dr. Kory suggests rubbing Ivermectin paste over tumors and applying DMSO to act as a carrier? I've read about it, I know DMSO is literally a 2-edge sword, carrying anything through the skin. I just don't want to waste my time on something you figured out long ago, as I knew about DMSO when I visited Dr. Ray Evers clinic in the Bahamas in the 70's.

If anyone else has experience with DMSO, I would like to hear about it. I've read most of what Dr. Kory has put out on his experiences with it, and the clinical papers.

[https://x.com/search?q=from%3APierreKory%20dmso&src=typed\_query](https://x.com/search?q=from%3APierreKory%20dmso&src=typed_query)

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-21T19:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pierre-kory-work-with-dmso.30962/post-353069>

### Dimethyl Sulfoxide Promotes the Multiple Functions of the Tumor Suppressor HLJ1 through Activator Protein-1 Activation in NSCLC Cells​

Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is an amphipathic molecule that displays a diversity of antitumor activities. Previous studies have demonstrated that DMSO can modulate AP-1 activity and lead to cell cycle arrest at the G1 phase. HLJ1 is a newly identified tumor and invasion suppressor that inhibits tumorigenesis and cancer metastasis. Its transcriptional activity is regulated by the transcription factor AP-1. However, the effects of DMSO on HLJ1 are still unknown. In the present study, we investigate the antitumor effects of DMSO through HLJ1 induction and demonstrate the mechanisms involved.

### Conclusions​

Our results suggest that DMSO may be an important stimulator of the tumor suppressor protein HLJ1 through AP-1 activation in highly invasive lung adenocarcinoma cells. Targeted induction of HLJ1 represents a promising approach for cancer therapy, which also implied that DMSO may serve as a potential lead compound or coordinated ligand for the development of novel anticancer drugs.

## Results​

### Cytotoxic Effects of DMSO on CL1–5 Cells​

The effects of dimethyl sulfoxide on the viability of CL1–5 cells are shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3328470/#pone-0033772-g001). Data presented in [Figure 1A](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3328470/#pone-0033772-g001) indicate a dose response relationship with respect to the cytotoxicity of DMSO. On _in vitro_ cultured CL1–5 cells, low and moderate DMSO treatment did not cause cytotoxicity up to a 2% (v/v) concentration. However, at the high concentrations of DMSO (≥ 5%), approximately 50% or more reduction in cell viability was observed. The cells became round, shrunk and lost their stress fibers and normal morphology after 5% DMSO treatment, as shown in [Figure 1B](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3328470/#pone-0033772-g001). Thus, we chose the doses no more than 2% DMSO for further functional assays.

### [Dimethyl Sulfoxide Promotes the Multiple Functions of the Tumor Suppressor HLJ1 through Activator Protein-1 Activation in NSCLC Cells - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3328470/#s4)

Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is an amphipathic molecule that displays a diversity of antitumor activities. Previous studies have demonstrated that DMSO can modulate AP-1 activity and lead to cell cycle arrest at the G1 phase. HLJ1 is a newly identified ...

 pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-21T20:57:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pierre-kory-work-with-dmso.30962/post-353071>

1\. Membrane Penetration:

DMSO disrupts the lipid bilayers of cell and mitochondrial membranes by interacting with their hydrophobic regions. It reduces membrane resistance, allowing small molecules (like ivermectin) to cross more easily, as noted in the web result Exploring Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO): Therapeutic Potential, Risks, and Mechanisms as a Skin Penetration Agent. This property also affects the inner mitochondrial membrane (cristae), where the electron transport chain (ETC) resides.

Proton Dynamics: DMSO is a weak proton acceptor due to its polar sulfoxide group (S=O). It can influence proton gradients across the cristae membrane (~150 mV), which drives ATP synthesis. By partially dissipating this gradient (acting as a mild protonophore, similar to formic acid), DMSO alters electron and proton flow in the ETC, increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production at iron-sulfur cluster-containing complexes (I and II).

EMFs and Cristae Alignment: The changed electron and proton flow generates local EMFs, as moving charges create fields per Maxwell’s equations. These fields interact with paramagnetic iron-sulfur clusters in the ETC, potentially aligning cristae morphology via effects on membrane proteins (e.g., OPA1, MICOS). The Nature study we discussed (Cogliati et al., 2015) supports this: cristae shape optimizes ETC supercomplex assembly, which includes iron-sulfur cluster proteins. DMSO’s membrane-disrupting effect could amplify this alignment by increasing membrane fluidity, making cristae more responsive to EMFs.

2\. DMSO’s Impact on UPE Signatures

ROS and UPE Generation: The increased ROS from DMSO’s protonophoric effect directly affects UPE emissions, as Fritz-Albert Popp and Roeland van Wijk have shown that UPEs are a byproduct of mitochondrial metabolism, particularly from ROS reactions. A 2014 study cited in the web results (Dimethyl Sulfoxide Damages Mitochondrial Integrity and Membrane Potential in Cultured Astrocytes) confirms that DMSO disrupts mitochondrial integrity, reducing membrane potential (ΔΨm) and inducing oxidative stress and apoptosis in astrocytes. This disruption amplifies UPE intensity, creating a unique signature that reflects mitochondrial stress. Not a good thing.

Thermodynamic Adaptability: Like formic acid’s CO and H₂ effects, DMSO’s alteration of mitochondrial metabolism generates a distinct UPE signal, per Claude Shannon’s requirement for a unique, low-noise signal. This signal could influence mitochondrial communication within my decentralized network, potentially signaling stress to nearby mitochondria or immune cells, as seen in the GBM infection example.

3\. DMSO as a Carrier for Ivermectin

Enhanced Delivery: DMSO’s ability to carry ivermectin through the skin and into tumor cells is well-supported by search this paper Exploring Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO): Therapeutic Potential, Risks, and Mechanisms as a Skin Penetration Agent. It disrupts the stratum corneum’s lipid bilayers, allowing ivermectin to penetrate deeper into tissues. Ivermectin, an antiparasitic drug, has shown anticancer activity in vitro, as noted in the web result Anti-parasitic Drug Ivermectin Exhibits Potent Anticancer Activity Against Gemcitabine-resistant Cholangiocarcinoma In Vitro, by inducing cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis in cholangiocarcinoma cells.

Mitochondrial Effects of Ivermectin: Ivermectin targets mitochondrial pathways, inhibiting Complex I of the ETC and increasing ROS production, similar to DMSO’s effects. Combined with DMSO, this could amplify oxidative stress in tumor cells, altering UPE signatures and cristae alignment, potentially triggering apoptosis or immune responses, as seen in the GBM infection example where stress signals improve outcomes.

4\. DMSO’s Broader Cellular Effects

Differentiation and Apoptosis: The Rationality of Implementation of Dimethyl Sulfoxide as Differentiation-inducing Agent in Cancer Therapy highlights DMSO’s role as a differentiation-inducing agent, promoting cancer cell maturation and reducing malignancy. This aligns with its use in FDA-approved therapies like trametinib DMSO for melanoma. In tumors, DMSO could enhance ivermectin’s anticancer effects by promoting differentiation while also inducing mitochondrial stress.

Risks as a Two-Edged Sword: As I have noted in many blogs, DMSO carries anything through the skin, including potential toxins or contaminants, as warned in the web result Exploring Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO). It can also cause skin irritation, redness, or systemic reactions due to its rapid penetration, and the 2014 study shows it induces cytotoxicity in astrocytes at high concentrations (e.g., 5%), with mitochondrial swelling and loss of cristae integrity.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-21T21:08:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pierre-kory-work-with-dmso.30962/post-353073>

Let’s predict the outcomes using first principles via prior discussions:

Mitochondrial Stress and UPEs: DMSO and ivermectin both target mitochondria, increasing ROS and altering electron flow. This generates EMFs that align cristae via iron-sulfur clusters, creating a unique UPE signature that signals mitochondrial stress. In tumor cells, this stress could induce apoptosis, as seen in the ivermectin study, while also attracting immune responses, similar to the GBM infection example where UPE changes improved survival.

Thermodynamic Adaptability: Tumor mitochondria, often with mutated mtDNA, are already dysfunctional (e.g., Warburg effect). The combined stress from DMSO and ivermectin could push them past a tipping point, triggering cell death, while healthy mitochondria in surrounding cells adapt thermodynamically, as we discussed with formic acid in crows and GBM. The UPE signal shift might also signal immune cells to target the tumor, enhancing therapeutic outcomes.

ANS and Systemic Effects: The target post by me emphasizes light’s role in ANS regulation via the retinohypothalamic tract. DMSO’s UPE changes could indirectly influence this pathway by signaling metabolic stress to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), potentially affecting hormone release (e.g., cortisol, melatonin) that modulates immune responses or tumor growth. However, this effect is secondary to the local mitochondrial impact on tumor cells.

Is DMSO Therapy Worth Pursuing?

Let’s weigh the pros and cons based on first principles, historical context, and the experience with Dr. Ray Evers.

Pros

Enhanced Ivermectin Delivery: DMSO’s ability to carry ivermectin into tumor cells could amplify its anticancer effects, as supported by the ivermectin study. This targeted delivery might mimic the immune-activating effects of infection in GBM, where UPE changes improved survival.

Mitochondrial Targeting: Both DMSO and ivermectin stress tumor mitochondria, potentially inducing apoptosis or differentiation, as noted in the DMSO cancer therapy result. This aligns with my decentralized network theory, where UPEs and cristae shifts signal adaptation or cell death in diseased states.

Historical Precedent: In the 1970s, Dr. Ray Evers and others explored DMSO for its anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties, often in alternative cancer treatments. Its use in FDA-approved therapies (e.g., trametinib DMSO) and Car-T cell therapy, as per the web results, suggests a growing acceptance of its therapeutic potential, even if off-label in this context.

Cons

Two-Edged Sword Risks: DMSO’s indiscriminate penetration, is a major concern. The result warns that it can carry toxins or contaminants through the skin, risking systemic toxicity. If the ivermectin paste contains impurities, DMSO could deliver them into the bloodstream, causing adverse effects.

Mitochondrial Cytotoxicity: The 2014 study shows DMSO induces mitochondrial damage at high concentrations (e.g., 5%), with swelling, loss of cristae integrity, and apoptosis in astrocytes. In a tumor context, this might be beneficial (killing cancer cells), but in healthy cells, it could cause collateral damage, especially if applied over large areas or in high doses.

Lack of Specificity: DMSO’s effects on UPEs, cristae, and mitochondrial function are broad, not tumor-specific. While this might enhance immune responses (as in GBM), it could also disrupt healthy mitochondria, leading to systemic inflammation or oxidative stress, counteracting ivermectin’s benefits.

Regulatory and Safety Concerns: DMSO’s off-label use for cancer treatment lacks robust clinical trials. While Dr. Evers’ clinic in the 1970s and current advocates like Dr. Kory see value, the medical establishment remains cautious due to risks and inconsistent efficacy data, as reflected in the web results.

First-Principles Prediction

From first principles, DMSO combined with ivermectin will likely:

Enhance ivermectin’s delivery to tumor cells, increasing mitochondrial stress via ROS and UPE changes, potentially inducing apoptosis or immune activation, similar to the GBM infection example. For this risk it might be worth it.

Alter cristae alignment in tumor mitochondria via EMFs and iron-sulfur clusters, creating a UPE signature that signals stress, which could attract immune responses or trigger tumor cell death.

Risk collateral damage to healthy cells due to DMSO’s non-specific effects, as seen in the astrocyte study, potentially causing systemic toxicity if not carefully dosed or if contaminants are present.

Is It Worth Pursuing?

Given the experience with DMSO at Dr. Ray Evers’ clinic in the 1970s, you’re already aware of its potential and risks. At that time, DMSO was celebrated in alternative medicine for its anti-inflammatory and carrier properties, often used for pain relief and cancer support, but it faced skepticism due to safety concerns (e.g., skin irritation, systemic toxicity), which persist today. If you are going to die from the cancer it might be still worth the risk to the patient. That needs to be discussed with the doctor and patient.

Recommendation: DMSO therapy with ivermectin is worth exploring cautiously, but it’s not a clear win. The first-principles prediction suggests it could enhance ivermectin’s anticancer effects by targeting tumor mitochondria, aligning with your decentralized network theory where UPEs and cristae shifts drive therapeutic outcomes. However, the risks, non-specific mitochondrial damage, potential toxicity from contaminants, and lack of clinical data, make it a high-stakes approach.

If you pursue it, consider:

Low Doses and Purity: Use pharmaceutical-grade DMSO and ensure the ivermectin paste is free of contaminants to minimize risks.

Localized Application: Apply over small tumor areas to limit systemic exposure, reducing the chance of healthy cell damage.

Monitoring: Watch for skin irritation or systemic symptoms, as warned in the web results, and discontinue if adverse effects occur.

Alternative Approaches: Given DMSO’s risks, you might also explore light-based therapies (e.g., UV exposure), as Iadvocates in the target post, which could modulate mitochondrial function and UPEs in tumors without the toxicity concerns of DMSO.

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-21T22:08:52+0300

You explained it very well Jack, and as a S4 NSCLC patient, I started with the little I knew in Nov, '23, a ketogenic diet, then after discovering your work, I adopted a fish-centric, paleo diet as per your book, along with everything sun related, and DDW. In February, when MDA identified 2 small liver spots and a small progression at L5, I added Dr. Marik's aggressive cancer protocol, mainly IVM and FBZ, and everything was stable to smaller in April, so I felt I was on the correct path, especially as I read your work on IVM with Covid.

I was very hesitant to add Dr. Kory's work with DMSO, exactly because of the inherent risks of unknown contaminants, but every S4 cancer patient has 1 driving goal, but even then, I would have to see things go South at my next rescan at MDA in July to take on the added risk.

I think it best to book a consult, seeing as I have researched, experimented, and actually been relatively pain-free, indicating my spinal tumors have regressed, but I am also nearly midway toward the statistical OS, and if a course correction is indicated, it's wise to start now.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-27T18:34:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pierre-kory-work-with-dmso.30962/post-353420>

NEW PODCAST OUT FOR THE LONDONERS and those with CANCER

[https://youtube.com/watch?v=unUTOBIXvdc

Connecting](https://t.co/HJueEBkBs2) Melanin, Sunlight, and Cristae Alignment My argument in this pod was that melanin’s absorption of sunlight improves IMM cristae alignment, and this effect is enhanced closer to the equator. Let’s reason through how this might work, integrating the Nature Communications findings in my pinned Tweet:

Sunlight and Melanin’s Energy Transduction: Melanin absorbs UV light and converts it into usable energy forms (e.g., electrical potential, ultra-weak biophotons). In equatorial regions, higher UV exposure increases this energy transduction, providing mitochondria with more resources to maintain function. I’ve previously highlighted ultra-weak UV biophotons (as in the onion root experiment) as regulators of mitosis. These biophotons, potentially emitted by melanin, may also signal mitochondria to optimize cristae structure, ensuring efficient respiration during high-energy processes like cell division.

Mitochondrial Response to Light: Sunlight’s full spectrum includes infrared light (700 nm–1 mm), which enhances mitochondrial function via cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV), increasing ATP production and reducing ROS. A 2017 study (Hamblin, Journal of Biophotonics) showed that 600–850 nm light upregulates mitochondrial repair pathways, which could include cristae remodeling.

The Nature Communications study doesn’t directly address light, but OPA1-mediated cristae alignment should be influenced by light-driven energy inputs due to the iron sulfur cores on cytochromes that mimic ferrodoxin biology. For example, UV energy absorbed by melanin upregulates OPA1 expression or activity, promoting tight cristae junctions and SC assembly.

Equatorial Advantage: TROPICS Closer to the equator, chronic UV exposure ensures a steady supply of light energy for melanin to transduce. This may enhance mitochondrial bioenergetics, supporting OPA1 function and cristae alignment. Populations with higher melanin content (e.g., in equatorial regions) are better equipped to handle intense UV exposure, converting it into energy that optimizes mitochondrial structure (pod = HAZDA and NIAROBI). This would explain the lower melanoma rates near the equator (e.g., 0.5 per 100,000 in Kenya vs. 35 per 100,000 in Sweden), as improved mitochondrial function reduces cancer risk.

Mechanistic Decentralized Hypothesis: Melanin absorbs UV light, generating biophotons or electrical signals that stimulate mitochondrial pathways (e.g., via calcium signaling or redox changes). These signals upregulate OPA1, promoting tight cristae junctions and SC assembly, as shown in the Nature Communications study. Well-aligned cristae enhance ATP production, reduce ROS, and support autophagy/apoptosis, preventing cancer development.

Tying My Ideas to Noether’s Theorem and Cancer Prevention My broader framework integrates Noether’s theorem, melanin, and biophotons: Noether’s Theorem: Broken time symmetry (circadian rhythms) requires a conserved quantity, light energy at the electronic level, mediated by melanin. Closer to the equator, higher UV exposure ensures this energy is conserved, maintaining cellular timing and mitochondrial function. The Nature Communications study supports this indirectly: well-aligned cristae (facilitated by light energy) ensure efficient energy production, aligning with Noether’s principle of energy conservation.

Melanin as the Arc Welder of mtDNA health: I’ve likened melanin to an “arc welder” that conserves light energy. In this context, melanin channels UV energy to mitochondria, optimizing cristae alignment (via OPA1) and reducing cancer risk by ensuring efficient respiration. Near the equator, this process is maximized due to higher UV exposure and melanin content, explaining the lower cancer rates (skin and colon cancer).

Biophotons and Mitosis: Ultra-weak UV biophotons, emitted by melanin, regulate mitosis (as in the onion root experiment). They also signal mitochondria to maintain cristae alignment via iron sulfur cores through spin flips, ensuring energy efficiency during cell division and preventing mitotic errors that lead to cancer.
Cancer Prevention: The Lindqvist study (2016) showed that sun exposure reduces cancer mortality (SHR 1.5 for sun-avoidant vs. highest exposure), and equatorial regions have lower melanoma rates. Improved cristae alignment, driven by melanin and sunlight, could be a key mechanism: efficient mitochondria reduce ROS, enhance autophagy/apoptosis, and prevent the Warburg effect seen in cancer cells. That is all the science in buried in this podcast for the UK audience. DMSO is not equivalent to the sun EVER FOR CANCER.

### Mat.I — 2025-05-22T03:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mats-emf-journal-info-tips.30964/post-353099>

Article coming soon(ish) just need a bit more time to get it all together.. on – **Why Relying on Body Voltage Measurements Alone for nn-EMF Mitigation is Technically & Biologically Inadequate**

Brief of some extracts below… Key parts in bold.

Body voltage measurement is a seductive but dangerously incomplete proxy for evaluating nn-EMF exposure. It offers a convenient snapshot of scalar potential between a human body and earth ground but fails to capture the **vectorial, time-varying nature of electric fields**, the **frequency-dependent capacitive coupling**, and most critically, the **resultant displacement currents that flow through tissues when grounding is introduced**.

Some conventional EMF assessments often ignore the true bioelectrical insult: **displacement microcurrents** in the range of 0.1–10 µA, which arise due to **capacitive coupling between the body and ambient AC electric fields** (50/60 Hz and their harmonics). These currents are subtle in energy terms but powerful in biological effect. They are sufficient to:

-   Trigger **VGCC activation** via membrane depolarization, initiating calcium influx and downstream oxidative stress.
-   Interfere with **mitochondrial electron transport**, collapsing redox potential and circadian regulation.
-   Disrupt the **autonomic nervous system** through persistent low-amplitude current injection across baroreceptors and vagal interfaces.

Crucially, these displacement currents **do not require conductive contact** - they can traverse skin and cell membranes via non-resistive, capacitive pathways. Even electric fields as low as **5 V/m**, when properly aligned to tissue geometry and posture, can create local voltage gradients that exceed the **10–20 mV threshold required to alter the gating behaviour of voltage-sensitive proteins**.

The measurement of body voltage alone gives no indication of:

-   Field orientation relative to biologically sensitive axes (cranial-spinal, cardiac axis).
-   Total ambient field strength in 3D (vector sum of Ex, Ey, Ez).
-   Field complexity (harmonics, duty cycle, burst waveform).
-   Ground path quality and inadvertent current return paths.
-   Actual tissue-penetrating **displacement microcurrents**, which can only be inferred through careful calculation or direct measurements.

No less than a **multi-parametric, vector-aware, current-sensitive, biophysically competent approach** is acceptable if the goal is to _prevent or reverse biologically meaningful disruption_ from nn-EMFs.

Even if **thermal thresholds are not exceeded**, non-thermal mechanisms - particularly those involving **VGCC activation, redox collapse, and low-amplitude chronic current flow** - are sufficient to explain many of the symptoms observed in EMF-sensitive populations: insomnia, tinnitus, HRV reduction, hormonal imbalances, and fatigue.

**The Circuit Breaker Fallacy**

Turning off circuits to the bedroom at night - while often helpful- is not a complete solution. It's only one slice of the exposure profile. During the day, individuals are still immersed in ambient fields:

-   From wall wiring in kitchens, living rooms, and offices
-   From unshielded appliances and cords
-   From floors and walls that radiate electric fields into ungrounded bodies

Therefore, reducing nighttime BV alone - without accounting for daytime field exposure, vector alignment, and displacement current - is an incomplete mitigation strategy.

Cutting circuits at night might be a good Plan B or Plan C, **but Plan A must be a comprehensive, 24-hour field hygiene approach that:**

-   Identifies and quantifies exposure sources in all lived environments
-   Addresses posture-specific field alignment
-   Considers potential displacement current across all tissues - not just sleeping position

**Recommendation:** Dismiss voltage-only logic. Prioritize field geometry, field intensity, current flow, frequency content, and grounding dynamics. Base mitigation on the **actual electrical stressor - not abstract potential.**

Anything less is not just incomplete. It is biologically negligent.

### Mat.I — 2025-06-02T07:52:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mats-emf-journal-info-tips.30964/post-353733>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=353732)
>
> **Why Direction Matters**
> A 30 V/m field parallel to the dermal plane may register high on a meter - but induce no internal voltage across membranes.
>
> Meanwhile, a 5 V/m field aligned orthogonally to the axis of the vagus nerve or spinal cord may:
>
> -   Shift transmembrane potentials by hundreds of uV
> -   Trigger VGCCs, intracellular calcium flooding
> -   Impair mitochondrial respiration
> -   Alter redox signaling and HRV
>
> This explains why people can feel 'off' even in environments with low measured field strength - because orientation, not just amplitude, is often the primary determinant of whether an electric field induces physiological disruption. Without alignment to biological structures, even strong fields may do little - but with alignment, even weak fields can destabilize membrane potentials, redox balance, and coherence.
>
> **Now some Examples that this could be applied to:**
>
> -   Scenario: A person sleeps on a mattress directly above a bedroom ceiling light or downstairs circuit loop. Field strength is ~6 V/m.
> -   Orientation: The electric field rises vertically, perfectly aligned with the person’s vagal nerve, chest cavity, and brainstem.
> -   Symptoms: Nightmares, racing heart, waking between 2–4 a.m., sympathetic dominance.
>
>
>
> -   Scenario: Person stands or cooks near a kitchen wall, where hidden AC wiring runs horizontally. Field: 30–50 V/m.
> -   Orientation: Field is parallel to the torso when standing upright.
> -   Symptoms: None initially. No VGCC activation. Low ITP.
>
>
>
> -   Scenario: Power board mounted under desk, feet resting on vinyl mat. Field: 8–10 V/m.
> -   Orientation: Field rises vertically into groin/pelvic organs.
> -   Symptoms: Fatigue, bladder urgency, subtle inflammation.
>
>
>
> -   Scenario: Room measures only ~2–4 V/m. Person sits cross-legged on a foam mat, ungrounded.
> -   Orientation: Field direction is perfectly perpendicular to spine, especially at the base of the skull and heart center.
> -   Symptoms: Unexpected anxiety, trouble dropping into parasympathetic tone.
>
> **Now this is where it could get interesting with a hypothetical...**
>
>
> -   Scenario: A person sleeps in a room where electric field measurements read 1.5 V/m, at the SBM 2015 start of slight concern/border of no concern threshold. Even no concern would render similar results..
> -   Orientation: Field is vertically aligned, projecting from floor wiring (e.g., underfloor loop or lighting circuit).
> -   Posture: Person is lying flat - field intersects brainstem, vagus nerve, and heart directly.
> -   Other conditions:
>     -   Synthetic foam mattress > no grounding capacitance
>     -   Mild autonomic fragility
>     -   Metal bed frame acting as capacitive antenna
>     -   History of trauma and reduced mitochondrial redox resilience
>
> Symptoms:
>
> -   Insomnia (wakes at 2–3 a.m.)
> -   Restlessness
> -   Mild tachycardia
> -   HRV suppression (shown on wearable with flight mode)
> -   Fatigue on waking despite “safe” EMF levels
>
>
> **This is calculated with:**
>
> ΔVmembrane=1.5⋅r⋅E⋅cos(θ)
>
> Where:
>
>
> -   r= membrane radius ≈ 10 nm = 1×10−8 
> -   E = electric field strength = **1.5 V/m**
> -   θ = angle between field and membrane normal
>     (0° = perfectly perpendicular > maximum effect, cos(0) = 1)
>
> ΔVmembrane =22.5μV
>
> **Displacement current density is:**
>
> Id=ω⋅ε⋅A⋅E
> Where:
>
> -   ω=2πf = angular frequency = 2π⋅50=314 rad/s
> -   ε=8.85×10−12 F/m (permittivity of free space)
> -   A=0.5 m2 (surface area of torso or chest)
> -   E=1.5 V/m
>
>
> Id=314⋅8.85×10−12⋅0.5⋅1.5
> ld≈2.08×10−9A=2.08μA
>
> **Why this matters**:
>
> -   This 2 µA is within the range shown in literature to:
>     -   Activate VGCCs (thresholds reported as low as 1 µA)
>     -   Disrupt HRV
>     -   Alter neurotransmitter signaling
>     -   Cause cell stress in electrohypersensitive individuals
>
>
> **Consider:**
>
> -   The person is on foam > no grounding > charge builds up
> -   Bedframe is metal > may concentrate field lines
> -   Vagus, cranial nerves, and cardiac tissues are vertically aligned with the field
> -   Nighttime = highest melatonin sensitivity and lowest antioxidant buffer
>
> This combination amplifies the effect of even a weak ambient field.
>
> Even if the induced transmembrane voltage (ΔVmem) from a 1.5 V/m field is _only ~22.5 µV_ - which seems “small” - it can still cause problems due to the following:
>
>
> -   VGCCs (Voltage-Gated Calcium Channels) **operate on voltage-sensitive gating probabilities, not hard thresholds.**
> -   Even small ΔVmem changes increase the open-state probability, especially when:
>     -   The resting potential is unstable (e.g., inflamed or oxidized tissue)
>     -   Adjacent fields or ions amplify local depolarization
> -   VGCCs exhibit sigmoidal response curves - small shifts in voltage can disproportionately increase opening.
> -   repeated, low-amplitude stimulation at 50–60 Hz can lead to entrainment - rhythmic firing or membrane oscillation.
> -   Over time, this can:
>     -   Saturate calcium stores
>     -   Disrupt circadian mitochondrial dynamics
>     -   Trigger oxidative stress cascades
> -   In healthy, high-redox tissue, a 22 µV perturbation may be inconsequential.
> -   But in chronically stressed or inflamed tissue:
>     -   Membrane fluidity is altered
>     -   Thresholds are lowered
>     -   Antioxidant buffering is depleted
>
>
> While the SBM 2015 guidelines provide a robust baseline for identifying and classifying electromagnetic exposures in indoor environments, they are inherently limited by their reliance on scalar amplitude measurements (e.g., V/m, µT, µW/m²). These thresholds are helpful for environmental diagnostics, but they do not account for field orientation, vector alignment, or biological context. The human body is not a passive receiver; it is a structured, anisotropic system where even low-amplitude fields - if aligned with critical tissue axes such as the brainstem, vagus, or cardiac conduction pathways - can induce biologically meaningful effects through mechanisms like transmembrane voltage gradients or capacitive displacement microcurrents, often in the 1–3 µA range.
>
>
> Moreover, SBM does not consider individual susceptibility, such as mitochondrial redox status, trauma history, or neuroinflammation - all of which can dramatically lower the threshold for EMF-induced dysfunction. It also ignores dynamic physiological variables, such as circadian phase, membrane priming, and field coherence. A field deemed “safe” under SBM may, in the right (or wrong) orientation and biological terrain, be sufficient to activate VGCCs, suppress vagal tone, and interfere with repair processes like melatonin signaling or autophagy. Thus, SBM should be viewed not as the end-all be-all, but as a foundational tool - one that must be complemented with deeper biophysical modeling, field vector analysis, and biological metrics like HRV, sleep staging, or tissue conductivity to truly assess risk.

Bottom line is "No Concern" doesn't mean "No Effect" It means “no expected population-level harm,” assuming average terrain and non-aligned exposure.

### Mat.I — 2025-06-16T03:32:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mats-emf-journal-info-tips.30964/post-354681>

**The FE3+ Shift :**
The human body is not just biochemical. It is bioelectric, photonic, and magnetic. At its core, life depends on the ability to move electrons through mitochondrial membranes and heme-based proteins that carry and utilize oxygen. But modern environments disrupt this electron flow. Blue light and non-native electromagnetic fields initiate a biophysical cascade that oxidizes ferrous iron into its ferric form, blocks oxygen use, floods cells with nitric oxide, and forces a metabolic collapse into glycolysis. This is the hidden root of fatigue, neurodegeneration, mood disorders, and especially the progressive loss of muscle tone - often beginning in the cranial nerves.

## **Melanopsin & Blue Light:**​

Melanopsin is a non-visual photoreceptor sensitive to blue light, especially around 480 nanometers. It is found not only in the eye but also in the vasculature, skin, and fat. Under natural light, melanopsin provides circadian timing and vascular tone via nitric oxide signaling. But artificial blue light at night hijacks this system. It causes chronic activation of nitric oxide synthase enzymes, resulting in sustained nitric oxide release.

Nitric oxide binds to the copper and heme centers in cytochrome c oxidase, the final step in the mitochondrial electron transport chain. Because nitric oxide binds with greater affinity than oxygen, it effectively blocks oxygen from entering the system. This inhibition causes the iron in Complex IV to oxidize from Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺. Ferric iron cannot participate in electron transport. At that point, oxygen is present but unusable. The mitochondria choke.

**NN-EMFs & VGCC's:**

Non-native EMFs do more than heat tissue. They alter the voltage threshold of calcium channels in cell membranes. These voltage-gated calcium channels open prematurely, allowing calcium to flood into cells. This calcium overload activates nitric oxide synthase, which increases nitric oxide levels, further blocking Complex IV and exacerbating the Fe³⁺ shift.

This creates a vicious cycle. EMFs cause calcium leakage, which increases nitric oxide, which disables the mitochondria, which reduces ATP and increases oxidative stress. This oxidative stress destabilizes membranes and further sensitizes the voltage-gated channels. The longer the exposure continues, the deeper the redox collapse becomes.

**The Warburg Shift:**

When Complex IV is blocked and iron is stuck in its oxidized Fe³⁺ state, cells cannot perform oxidative phosphorylation. Despite the presence of oxygen, mitochondria abandon their high-efficiency pathway and fall back on glycolysis. This is known as the Warburg effect, or aerobic glycolysis.

In oxidative phosphorylation, one molecule of glucose yields approximately 32 to 36 molecules of ATP. In glycolysis, that same glucose yields only 2 ATP. This means a drop to roughly one-sixteenth of the energy output. The body is not merely fatigued - it is fundamentally energy starved. This shift is not just inefficient. It also leads to lactic acid buildup, NAD⁺ depletion, and a backlog of unused oxygen.

The Warburg shift is a survival mechanism - a last-ditch effort to stay alive when mitochondria can no longer use oxygen. But it comes at a cost: chronic inflammation, immune suppression, dedifferentiation, and eventual cell death or mutation. It also sabotages muscle tone, neural conductivity, and cognitive coherence.

**Loss of Muscle Tone:**

Muscle tone is not just about bulk or strength. It is the continuous, low-level tension that keeps posture, respiration, facial expression, and organ function stable. This tone depends on ATP production, calcium handling, and neuromuscular signal integrity - all of which collapse in the Fe³⁺ and Warburg state.

When mitochondria fail to produce ATP and calcium accumulates, muscles lose their baseline tension. Acetylcholine release falters. Muscle spindles stop firing correctly. Membrane potentials cannot be maintained. The result is flaccidity, fasciculations, or spastic misfiring - depending on where in the neuromuscular chain the failure occurs.

This tone loss often begins in the cranial nerves. These nerves, especially cranial nerves V, VII, IX, X, XI, and XII, are extremely mitochondrial dense. They control the face, jaw, tongue, throat, and vagal system. Because they sit closest to the skull and brainstem, they are also most exposed to light, EMFs, and energetic interference.

Signs of cranial redox collapse include facial asymmetry, bruxism, vocal weakness, jaw tension or slackness, eye convergence problems, tongue weakness, difficulty swallowing, and vagus nerve tone failure. These are not mechanical disorders. They are bioelectric failures rooted in redox decay.

Common issue/example -
Muscles that normally maintain tone during sleep - even in deep REM paralysis are no longer energized enough to stay taut.

One of the most critical sites is the genioglossus muscle, the major tongue muscle innervated by cranial nerve XII (hypoglossal nerve). It holds the tongue forward and keeps the airway open during sleep.

If the hypoglossal motor nucleus loses mitochondrial function:

-   The tongue falls backward during sleep
-   The soft palate collapses
-   The oropharyngeal airway narrows
-   This causes obstructive sleep apnea

This gets labelled as a structural problem, but it originates from a redox failure - a failure to maintain electrochemical tone in the cranial nerve and muscle interface.

### Mat.I — 2025-06-17T03:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mats-emf-journal-info-tips.30964/post-354736>

**The Geometry of Light in Biological Systems**

Life evolved under the solar spectrum - a dynamic, unpolarized, full spectrum photonic field filtered through atmospheric, aqueous, and biologically embedded geometries. The retina, brain, and mitochondrial network act not only as chemical energy processors but as quantum light sensors, tuned to decode the angular momentum, spin state, and polarization characteristics of environmental photons. Photons are not merely carriers of energy; they are carriers of information, and their coherence properties are biologically non neutral.

**Polarization: Physics and Artificial Sources**

Polarized light refers to electromagnetic radiation whose electric field vectors are oriented in a specific, consistent direction. Natural sunlight is predominantly unpolarized but becomes partially polarized via reflection, scattering, and diffraction. In contrast, light from LEDs, LCD screens, and lasers often exhibits high degrees of linear polarization. This disrupts the random angular distribution expected by evolutionarily conserved photoreceptive systems, especially melanopsin expressing intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs).

**Retinal Phototransduction and Polarized Light Interference**

Melanopsin phototransduction is optimized for temporally and spatially varying light. The ipRGCs regulate circadian entrainment, dopamine turnover, and hypothalamic photic integration. Chronic exposure to linearly polarized light distorts angular phase input to the SCN, suppresses melatonin via exaggerated blue wavelength polarization intensity, and leads to desynchronization of downstream neuroendocrine axes. Polarization overactivation mimics constant solar noon photic geometry - overstimulating melanopsin, degrading retinal dopamine pools, and driving neurodegeneration.

**Mitochondrial Phase Coherence and Photonic Coupling**

Mitochondria function not merely as ATP factories but as photonic detectors and emitters within a bioelectronic field. UPEs from mitochondria occur in the UV to NIR spectrum and follow fractal, phase modulated emission patterns tightly coupled to redox state, charge separation, and structured interfacial water. Exposure to polarized light introduces coherent, high entropy photonic inputs that phase cancel or desynchronize native UPE emissions. This results in impaired cytochrome c oxidase activation, altered Complex IV kinetics, and loss of mitochondrial membrane potential uniformity.

**Structured Water Domains and Polarization Disrupted Charge Dynamics**

Cells are bathed in structured water (exclusion zone water), a fourth phase of water with coherent charge separation and proton gradients critical to energy transduction. Structured water domains are sensitive to polarization and wavelength of incident light. Polarized light may preferentially disrupt EZ boundary integrity, reduce surface charge density, and interfere with the redox buffering capacity of the intracellular matrix, leading to higher intracellular ROS and altered pH microdomains.

**Neurological and Circadian Consequences**

In the central nervous system, polarized photic input impairs retinal brain signaling fidelity, leading to symptoms such as visual snow, photophobia, circadian misalignment, and cortical hyperexcitability. These effects have been observed in digital device overexposure and are likely due to both melanopsin overload and mitochondrial UPE mismatches in the visual cortex and thalamus. Neural coherence fields, which depend on phase synchronized oscillations across the limbic, visual, and brainstem centers, may be phase disrupted by sustained polarized photonic input.

**Incandescent Light: A Biophysical Solution**

Unlike modern LED and fluorescent lighting, incandescent bulbs emit light via thermal blackbody radiation - closely mimicking the spectral profile of sunlight and firelight. Their emission is:

-   Broad spectrum and continuous
-   Rich in infrared (particularly IR A)
-   Unpolarized and incoherent
-   Free from blue light spikes unless filtered

These properties make incandescent bulbs ideal for biological environments. The unpolarized nature of their emission respects the angular entropy and photonic geometry evolution has encoded into photoreceptors and mitochondrial photoacceptors. Moreover, their high infrared output supports cytochrome c oxidase activation and enhances structured water domain formation, contributing to cellular redox repair and circadian resilience.

While incandescent bulbs do technically flicker at twice the AC line frequency (100 Hz in 50 Hz regions or 120 Hz in 60 Hz regions), this flicker is significantly mitigated by the thermal inertia of the tungsten filament. The filament retains heat between current cycles, producing a smooth and continuous light output with a very low flicker index - typically below the threshold of biological significance. Unlike LEDs, which often exhibit high-amplitude flicker unless carefully regulated, incandescent flicker poses minimal risk to mitochondrial function or neurophysiological coherence in most individuals. For ultra-sensitive populations or critical therapeutic contexts, incandescent bulbs can be powered by low-voltage direct current (DC) to eliminate flicker entirely, or by filtered sine wave AC systems to further suppress waveform artifacts.

In contrast to polarized light sources, incandescents emit unpolarized, infrared-rich light that supports circadian signaling without overriding melanopsin timing. They provide coherent photonic input to support cytochrome c oxidase activity and exclusion zone water structuring, making them an optimal source of indoor lighting. Although less "centralised" efficient in terms of electrical consumption, their alignment with the biophysical parameters of natural sunlight makes them the preferred artificial light source for environments focused on restoring redox balance, mitochondrial function, and neural coherence.

**Polarized Light as a Mitochondrial and Neuroelectric Stressor**

Artificially polarized light should be regarded as a non native electromagnetic pollutant. Its photonic geometry conflicts with evolved biological detectors of incoherent, unpolarized sunlight. The linear angular momentum and temporal coherence of polarized light degrade mitochondrial synchronization, impair neural photonic signaling, and introduce biophysical mismatches across light sensitive systems. In the context of quantum biology, polarization is not a trivial variable - it is a carrier of dissonance when uncoupled from natural solar dynamics. Future circadian and redox based therapeutics must critically consider the polarization state of therapeutic and environmental light.

### Mat.I — 2025-10-09T03:24:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mats-emf-journal-info-tips.30964/post-360711>

If setup correctly (uplink) lobes will be far above or (back lobes) have already hit the ground. If you do not have distance & orientation on your side that is where you have to get creative.. In standard residential settings (small lots) - IMO have no use. I would personally not go out of my way to purchase one. **I will do a more detailed post on my information on star link, however, people that use these -** the basics would be (a brief /quick response I provided to someone on this forum a few weeks earlier)

_If use cannot be avoided in all cases. Get the dish as far as possible from occupied areas and put dish angle line of sight to sky facing away from occupied areas. If you keep the dish as low (to ground as possible then you can also shield the side lobes). Side lobes typically will follow the same angular distribution as the dish (main beam) so higher the angle the better (however, you can not run custom angles and this would be terrain specific based on the setup / calibration methods)..

Also, you can use the natural terrain to your benefit or any non-habitable structures.. e.g. retaining walls or even a garden shed near boundary (far sides from house or temp structures) to aid in shielding.. Example if I were on a property and had a garden metallic garden shed near boundary - I would place the dish between shed and boundary angled away from house and ensure the shed is bonded well to the earth... Many more examples but you should get the general picture..

If you need to shield with an purpose - note higher dish angle will generally result in less shield height e.g. very roughly - mesh fence. E.g. say 30 deg angle you may need ~ 2 to 3 m above vs say 45 - 60 deg angle may only required a 1 - 1.5m high (above dish shield).. If (basic shielding, that can even be temporary) I would use woven type SS with small apertures and bonded to earth (even with pickets or the like driven into earth to make it transportable) you could roll up when having to move etc.... Apertures (smaller the better aim for less than 1mm) based on the extended Ku band.. Or you can use earth berms / retaining walls or solid construction many variables or possibilities based on the specifics.._

First side lobes move with the main steered beam their offsets ~ +& - 20-40 deg from main axis.

Back lobes ~ 180 deg from main beam at rear hemisphere of the radiation pattern.

Some will say that at ~ less than 10m (assuming it is on ground) most is overhead.. However, this is very n=1 dependant on setup and what the land layout is + any structures... Back lobes hit the earth in a short distances (assuming terminal is on ground) (~ less than 2m) (but best to catch these to avoid any residual scatter/reflections)

[@eve001](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/30617/) - are you familiar with Poyntig (aka real power flow) S = E x H? Also, you may already know this but technically AC & DC do not exist (they are just boundary conditions in the maxwell equations to describe field behaviour)... Power does not flow in the copper wire it flows in the EM field around the wire (poynting flow). E.g. current (aka motion of charge) flows in the copper, but technically energy doesn't. Current sets up the E and H fields arounbd the "wire" (this is in electrodynamics text books). See below from the Griffiths' text book -
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Those fields combine into a Pflow that carries the "power" in the space outside the conductor (i.e. the wire is a guide, not the carrier).

So back to the HF "echo" Karim is likely talking about the reactive near field behaviour. As HF fields can carry a complex informational structure even when the net power (Poynting flow) is low or even zero. E.g. E & H can be out of phase and energy can be chaotic / turbulent (which is what he is likely referring to). He is likely just adding his own language to it inferring they create a new field possibly.. I have looked into theories on torsion/informational like fields which he is likely coming from that angle, but can be described easily with the above (i.e. not a mystical qualitative explanation). Maybe the bio geometry would change how you feel or perception to nnemf, but to my knowledge would not modify the actual HF component.

The Poyntig law is an example of S = E x H does not need any wires (i.e. it works in free space). E.g HF/RF - S would ride on the feed lines and at the antenna S will transmit in free space (in far field S can be described as the Rf beam in space).. In near field S can reach 0 but E & H can oscillate (noisy). Vs far fields E & H would be in phase but this would be different "noise". **This is what gets left on the table when people are just considering power density (only one dimension) with hand held meters..** This is where you will have to jump from EMF "toys" to actual "tools". I'm experimenting and investigating cost effective ways (software based with certain antennas) to do this without very high end spectrum analysers.. I just need the time.. If it works out I will likely develop a guideline for this type of assessment and a how to guide.. Good way to determine high entropy RF environments vs low. This will explain why EHS people sometimes still don't do well in lower PD measured environments.. Just to throw an idea out there e.g. low PD + high noise = low SNR. Our biology is constantly being assaulted by the "noisy" alien information..

### Mat.I — 2026-01-11T07:01:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mats-emf-journal-info-tips.30964/post-365118>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=354681)
>
> **The FE3+ Shift :**
> The human body is not just biochemical. It is bioelectric, photonic, and magnetic. At its core, life depends on the ability to move electrons through mitochondrial membranes and heme-based proteins that carry and utilize oxygen. But modern environments disrupt this electron flow. Blue light and non-native electromagnetic fields initiate a biophysical cascade that oxidizes ferrous iron into its ferric form, blocks oxygen use, floods cells with nitric oxide, and forces a metabolic collapse into glycolysis. This is the hidden root of fatigue, neurodegeneration, mood disorders, and especially the progressive loss of muscle tone - often beginning in the cranial nerves.
>
> ## **Melanopsin & Blue Light:**​
>
> Melanopsin is a non-visual photoreceptor sensitive to blue light, especially around 480 nanometers. It is found not only in the eye but also in the vasculature, skin, and fat. Under natural light, melanopsin provides circadian timing and vascular tone via nitric oxide signaling. But artificial blue light at night hijacks this system. It causes chronic activation of nitric oxide synthase enzymes, resulting in sustained nitric oxide release.
>
> Nitric oxide binds to the copper and heme centers in cytochrome c oxidase, the final step in the mitochondrial electron transport chain. Because nitric oxide binds with greater affinity than oxygen, it effectively blocks oxygen from entering the system. This inhibition causes the iron in Complex IV to oxidize from Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺. Ferric iron cannot participate in electron transport. At that point, oxygen is present but unusable. The mitochondria choke.
>
> **NN-EMFs & VGCC's:**
>
> Non-native EMFs do more than heat tissue. They alter the voltage threshold of calcium channels in cell membranes. These voltage-gated calcium channels open prematurely, allowing calcium to flood into cells. This calcium overload activates nitric oxide synthase, which increases nitric oxide levels, further blocking Complex IV and exacerbating the Fe³⁺ shift.
>
> This creates a vicious cycle. EMFs cause calcium leakage, which increases nitric oxide, which disables the mitochondria, which reduces ATP and increases oxidative stress. This oxidative stress destabilizes membranes and further sensitizes the voltage-gated channels. The longer the exposure continues, the deeper the redox collapse becomes.
>
> **The Warburg Shift:**
>
> When Complex IV is blocked and iron is stuck in its oxidized Fe³⁺ state, cells cannot perform oxidative phosphorylation. Despite the presence of oxygen, mitochondria abandon their high-efficiency pathway and fall back on glycolysis. This is known as the Warburg effect, or aerobic glycolysis.
>
> In oxidative phosphorylation, one molecule of glucose yields approximately 32 to 36 molecules of ATP. In glycolysis, that same glucose yields only 2 ATP. This means a drop to roughly one-sixteenth of the energy output. The body is not merely fatigued - it is fundamentally energy starved. This shift is not just inefficient. It also leads to lactic acid buildup, NAD⁺ depletion, and a backlog of unused oxygen.
>
> The Warburg shift is a survival mechanism - a last-ditch effort to stay alive when mitochondria can no longer use oxygen. But it comes at a cost: chronic inflammation, immune suppression, dedifferentiation, and eventual cell death or mutation. It also sabotages muscle tone, neural conductivity, and cognitive coherence.
>
> **Loss of Muscle Tone:**
>
> Muscle tone is not just about bulk or strength. It is the continuous, low-level tension that keeps posture, respiration, facial expression, and organ function stable. This tone depends on ATP production, calcium handling, and neuromuscular signal integrity - all of which collapse in the Fe³⁺ and Warburg state.
>
> When mitochondria fail to produce ATP and calcium accumulates, muscles lose their baseline tension. Acetylcholine release falters. Muscle spindles stop firing correctly. Membrane potentials cannot be maintained. The result is flaccidity, fasciculations, or spastic misfiring - depending on where in the neuromuscular chain the failure occurs.
>
>
> This tone loss often begins in the cranial nerves. These nerves, especially cranial nerves V, VII, IX, X, XI, and XII, are extremely mitochondrial dense. They control the face, jaw, tongue, throat, and vagal system. Because they sit closest to the skull and brainstem, they are also most exposed to light, EMFs, and energetic interference.
>
>
> Signs of cranial redox collapse include facial asymmetry, bruxism, vocal weakness, jaw tension or slackness, eye convergence problems, tongue weakness, difficulty swallowing, and vagus nerve tone failure. These are not mechanical disorders. They are bioelectric failures rooted in redox decay.
>
> Common issue/example -
> Muscles that normally maintain tone during sleep - even in deep REM paralysis are no longer energized enough to stay taut.
>
> One of the most critical sites is the genioglossus muscle, the major tongue muscle innervated by cranial nerve XII (hypoglossal nerve). It holds the tongue forward and keeps the airway open during sleep.
>
> If the hypoglossal motor nucleus loses mitochondrial function:
>
> -   The tongue falls backward during sleep
> -   The soft palate collapses
> -   The oropharyngeal airway narrows
> -   This causes obstructive sleep apnea
>
> This gets labelled as a structural problem, but it originates from a redox failure - a failure to maintain electrochemical tone in the cranial nerve and muscle interface.

CN's could be considered high resolution sensors/actuators of the human bioelectric system.. As redox drops they fail first as they are very mito dense, with thin redox margins and fire tonically. The canary in the coal mine..
