### KFDags — 2025-09-26T00:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-360051>

Not many updates on me; still awaiting my actual neurosurgeon to review my CT/MRI images. I’m supposed to have an angiogram next, I believe. Both the CT & MRI came back to say - basically - all is good in the neighborhood, besides normal wear + tear. I have a sinus infection; that was essentially the worst of it. It’s amazing: built like Gumby & all diagnostic imaging comes back negative, nearly 100% of the time. I can’t even get mad anymore.

I did want to mention that my in-laws are now showing some benefits from a couple red light panels I’ve strategically placed in their living room. Believe it or not, my husband is the one who suggested that I help them with some of their ailments.

Look, they are both minimum 2x jabbed, my FIL has cancer now, my MIL is one of those Ozempic people, and they do all the wrong things at every possible turn. And in their current vegetative state, they’re never going to do what I suggest. Therefore, I just wanted to offset some of their bad habits. It’s been 6 days, and there is a noticeable cognitive improvement in both of them. I am actually shocked at the difference! My husband is shocked too. My FIL mentioned sleeping better, while my MIL seemed a lot calmer.

I fully understand those panels are crap; however, my kids & in-laws got to spend a few hours of genuine quality time together. A typical visit used to involve FIL falling asleep in place while snoring loudly, and MIL acting very frazzled, ADHD and high-strung. Next goal: lead them (fingers crossed) to the sunrise.

### KFDags — 2025-10-06T03:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-360462>

Too excited, so have to post this right now: my son, Logan, went this entire weekend using words + short phrases -instead of his usual yelling- to ask for help or request what he wanted! Nobody understands how much this means!! My entire weekend was filled with “Shower now!,” and “all done,” and “outside!,” “tickle!” or the most hilarious “Tip-sy Beansssss,” with a strong emphasis on the S, and all!! FYI, Tipsy Beans is Logan asking to be tossed into a giant pile of pillows; his nickname is _Beans_, as he ate beans once like 4 years ago.

Another miraculous thing: nearly all of his self-injurious behavior is gone. Gone!!! He had an awful history of smashing his head into the wall or floor; he’s put dents in the walls, our vehicles, etc. He would punch himself in the chin also, when he was extremely frustrated or having a meltdown. We almost never see those behaviors anymore! And the meltdowns are dwindling; can’t even remember the last one, frankly. The level of stress in our household has dropped so much. Feeling grateful to you, Dr. Kruse!

### Wild Side Blue — 2025-10-12T17:34:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-360920>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=354031)
>
> Hello there, I am a 39-year-old married mom of two. My son is 7 (he has ASD and is nonverbal,) daughter is 5; I’m a stay-at-home mom, and my husband is a police officer, and retired Army/National Guard. We live in the smallest US state, Rhode Island. I guess you could describe me as pleasantly weird? My mother-in-law called me “eclectic” but she’s just being polite. I have hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and so do my children, it appears. We also have a MTHFR polymorphism which, I’m certain, contributed to our health issues.
>
> I am 4’9” and ~95lbs; I stopped growing in fourth grade/age 12. My parents are decent people, with similar health issues, so they never recognized any of my problems, as ‘problems.’ I was often labeled lazy and selfish. I am an only child and had no cousins to play with growing up, so I was alone a lot. Friends come and go, still; I prefer solitude. Most of my childhood was spent being asleep or trying to sleep, playing video games, watching TV, but I also went outside a lot because I had (still have; we now live in my childhood home) a great backyard in the woods.
>
> My biggest struggle was/is being so exhausted that I simply couldn’t function, even prior to starting elementary school. Not narcolepsy at all, but it was a thirst for sleep that was never quenched. This seriously affected my academic ability (later, killed any career aspirations), and I was in classes through high school with lower-functioning kids with behavioral issues. During school breaks, I would sleep 15-18 hours straight. And after school, I’d come home and fall asleep in place, sometimes with my eyes open. I’d wake up to my mom screaming at me to wake up, after she got home from work at 6pm. Once I got my driver’s license, I used to skip school to sleep in my car; ended up with a dead battery once. I missed so much school that I showed up to the wrong class, because I didn’t know what day it was. Teachers would forget who I was.
>
> Right before high school graduation, at age 18, I unknowingly contracted mononucleosis, then walking pneumonia. But it felt no different than my usual daily baseline exhaustion so I went ahead with my life. Consequently, I wound up in critical condition in the ICU, intubated, and in a medically-induced coma for 19 days. I kept trying to pull tubes out and escape so I was given very high doses of drugs like Ativan, fentanyl, and paralytic drugs. They barely worked. Doctors didn’t understand why my body could tolerate such high doses of these drugs. They questioned my parents whether I was an alcoholic or intravenous drug user (I was neither, obviously). After nearly a month in the hospital, I recovered.
>
> I continued to have ongoing, strange medical issues to include kidney stones (spaced every 10 years beginning at age 21); shingles at age 24; POTS (beginning in early childhood,) along with MCAS-type symptoms; heart palpitations, unbearable social anxiety with facial flushing; severe hyperhidrosis, especially axillary; 2:30/3am random vomiting (until pre-teens) & vomiting while asleep (until age 7/8); a transient head-turn syncope (almost full blackouts with hearing loss followed by tinnitus, tunnel vision, loss of speech, unable to move for about 60-90 seconds) and, of course, hypermobility with subluxations.

Hi! I have EDS hypermobility too. Wrote a journal on it. You are not alone!

### KFDags — 2025-10-14T23:58:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361037>

> [Wild Side Blue said:](/goto/post?id=360920)
>
> Hi! I have EDS hypermobility too. Wrote a journal on it. You are not alone!

Greetings, fellow Bendy Person!

Nice to meet you; also, my sympathy goes out to you! I’ll have to check your blog out. Glad you also made it here, as I’m sure you know, there aren’t many non-shitty resources for those with hEDS out there. Technically, I’m self-diagnosed; found out that I (probably) had hEDS while doing research randomly one day, back in 2013.

Do you know anyone personally with hEDS? I actually do: this dude in my high school class, who has been on disability for years now; he’s had 15+ shoulder surgeries, thus far. I also know of a few friends (all in the same large family) who probably have hEDS; unfortunately, they have no clue at all. Just one person from that family was officially diagnosed. And even she knows nothing about it.

Have you always been hypermobile? Pretty sure I hit nearly all 9 points on the Beighton scale. I’ve always tried to have fun with it though, since most of my life I never realized it was a medical problem.

### Wild Side Blue — 2025-10-15T00:21:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361039>

I have met a few EDS people before from a severe range to mild. What’s wild, I wasn’t that bendy but very mild. But I have had major injuries that led up to it. I did get a diagnosis from my doctor and treatment of prolotherapy. Lot’s of prolotherapy! I done some film work for her at her own practice of regenerative medicine like PRP, prolotherapy, and stem cell so I have seen remarkable improvements. She’s awesome and thinks outside the box. Would love one day to get her and Uncle Jack in a Zoom meeting to talk about EDS. I used to be a really great short board surfer. And my goal is to do that again just know my limits of surfing waist to chest high waves not stomach to over head. lol. Lots of work I have to do but I know Uncle Jack is the next step to learning more. Between what my doctor and I know it’s a lot but it goes beyond what we know. That’s what I got to grasp and get with my doctor about it. We got to know more! lol.

Where can I learn more about how to cut off this switch and emf mitigate my son’s room? He’s almost three and nonverbal

Read the info, watch the videos, then call me.

### KFDags — 2025-10-22T20:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361425>

> [Wild Side Blue said:](/goto/post?id=361039)
>
> I have met a few EDS people before from a severe range to mild. What’s wild, I wasn’t that bendy but very mild. But I have had major injuries that led up to it. I did get a diagnosis from my doctor and treatment of prolotherapy. Lot’s of prolotherapy! I done some film work for her at her own practice of regenerative medicine like PRP, prolotherapy, and stem cell so I have seen remarkable improvements. She’s awesome and thinks outside the box. Would love one day to get her and Uncle Jack in a Zoom meeting to talk about EDS. I used to be a really great short board surfer. And my goal is to do that again just know my limits of surfing waist to chest high waves not stomach to over head. lol. Lots of work I have to do but I know Uncle Jack is the next step to learning more. Between what my doctor and I know it’s a lot but it goes beyond what we know. That’s what I got to grasp and get with my doctor about it. We got to know more! lol.

Must admit, I’m envious that you received an actual diagnosis! My neurosurgeon did indicate “stigmata of hypermobile EDS” in my progress notes; however, all other doctors I’ve seen across my lifetime want no part of this cornucopia of crazy that is my body. Best they could give me was “benign joint hypermobility.” I’ve had various genetic testing to rule out the other EDS types, plus some other diseases. All came back negative. Same with my two kids.

Yes, I’ve heard of prolotherapy but never tried it. It was something I was _just_ about to look into before finding Dr. Kruse. I am glad you are seeing such positive results. For a while, I tried EDS-specific physical therapy. Ever heard of the Muldowney PT Protocol for EDS? He’s based in Rhode Island. No clue if the protocol actually works but highly, highly **do not** recommend! The owner (Kevin Muldowney) spent most of our time trying to convince me to purchase his book, in order for the PT to truly work. Also declared he couldn’t find any evidence of tethered cord. Several months later, I had tethered cord release surgery.

### Wild Side Blue — 2025-10-23T20:44:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361497>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=361425)
>
> Must admit, I’m envious that you received an actual diagnosis! My neurosurgeon did indicate “stigmata of hypermobile EDS” in my progress notes; however, all other doctors I’ve seen across my lifetime want no part of this cornucopia of crazy that is my body. Best they could give me was “benign joint hypermobility.” I’ve had various genetic testing to rule out the other EDS types, plus some other diseases. All came back negative. Same with my two kids.
>
> Yes, I’ve heard of prolotherapy but never tried it. It was something I was _just_ about to look into before finding Dr. Kruse. I am glad you are seeing such positive results. For a while, I tried EDS-specific physical therapy. Ever heard of the Muldowney PT Protocol for EDS? He’s based in Rhode Island. No clue if the protocol actually works but highly, highly **do not** recommend! The owner (Kevin Muldowney) spent most of our time trying to convince me to purchase his book, in order for the PT to truly work. Also declared he couldn’t find any evidence of tethered cord. Several months later, I had tethered cord release surgery.

I have not heard of that person but I’m in Virginia Beach, VA.

My doctor, Dr. Lisa Barr, MD does prolotherapy. Her website has a good article about prolotherapy. If you want the link let me know. I assume that’s allowed in the forum to send. She has treated many people with EDS hypermobility and that was an easy diagnosis to give me on my first visit. She’s an outside the thinker doc. She saved my life.

With my doc, she said kid you have a long way to go but if you do your part with PT and with the prolotherapy then let’s start the journey. She knows a lot of about EDS. However I want her and I to go to the next level with Uncle Jack. There is more Jack knows that I got to find out. Coming from her mouth, physical therapists, nurse practitioners, other MDs, dentists, all say I can explain so much in detail of gait shift patterns to what’s a Target area due to a muscle not firing properly. It wasn’t easy to learn that. Had to use an anatomy app and some books to pin point as well as asking massage therapist as they rub me to explain why that’s muscle is sore and what is the function. Then once I gather all I need I report back. They all say you are so intuitive with your body that you are the best candidate to speak to professionals. Not many patients can do that. But I still need Uncle Jack knowledge and I will keep searching and sometimes bugging him for articles. Lol.

### KFDags — 2025-10-23T23:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361510>

Well, today was an unexpected blast. Went for some neck X-rays: flexion + extension. Easy enough, but no! It’s been a while, apparently, since I’ve glanced down really hard. Four X-rays in (of five) when I suddenly got all sweaty, needing to taking deep breaths, heart pounding, feeling wicked nauseated, and dry heaving! Fun times. The poor girls there; they even summoned a nurse. I was fine after a while and they got the 5th X-ray. Definitely not my worst first-impression.

### KFDags — 2025-10-30T23:08:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361911>

Yesterday was supposed to be my telehealth appt. with Dr. Sullivan, my neurosurgeon; but, it was rescheduled, to almost a month from now: Nov. 20. A bit of a kick in the pants, since every appt. with her seems to get rescheduled at least once. I was expecting to get my next medical imaging quest. The brain/neck CT & cervical spine MRI didn’t show much, as I mentioned; and the cervical flexion/extension X-rays showed only these issues:

_Disc space height_: Preserved disc heights. Mild degenerative endplate changes at C4-C5, C5-C6, and C6-C7.

_Alignment_: Trace anterolisthesis of C3 over C4 which improves in extension.

In unrelated but good news: we will be Trick-or-Treating as a family of four this year! Logan has been so well-behaved and happy these past few months, we are giving it a shot. He even said “trick-or-treat!” We had grown accustomed to doing separate family activities, but now it feels like everything is becoming more normal. It is so nice.

Anyway, we also recently replaced our smart dryer with a new (non-smart) one. I went a little too far, avoiding the tech though: it doesn’t even have a dryer light inside, apparently? Hard to see what you’re doing, but it works.

Narrowly avoided having our electric meter replaced with a smart meter, even though we opted out. Our electric company has contractors showing up at people’s houses, then switching everyone over to smart meters. He had the wrong address, of course, and instead of verifying house numbers or confirming anything whatsoever at all, he just starts working. I told him where to go.

Lastly, I got a new car: 2025 Subaru WRX. Not sure how long the standard transmission has left in this miserable world, but it’s one of my few simple joys in life. Turned off all the WiFi, Bluetooth, and countless other things that were beeping at me. This is my first Subaru; was driving Honda Accords and Civics. But mostly every modern vehicle now has that hideous 1980s Black & Decker Dustbusters handheld vacuum style, whether it’s a shitty electric car or not. I was never in love with the WRX look but it’s growing on me, I guess.

### rtd — 2025-10-30T23:17:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-361914>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=361911)
>
> I was never in love with the WRX look but it’s growing on me, I guess.

Congrats on the new car

### KFDags — 2025-11-04T17:59:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-362141>

Four years ago today, we handed our son off for his cranial vault reconstruction. It was the scariest day of our lives. I honestly felt numb the whole time.

Today, life looks so much better for Logan. He is a completely different little dude: so happy and becoming so much more independent. He always says or does something new to make us so proud.

Yesterday was another example: he had a great dental visit and cleaning. During the last visit, the dentist said Logan will have to be sedated for a heavy duty cleaning the following visit (which was yesterday.) To say he’s been uncooperative would be a staggering understatement. But not this time; no sedation needed! He tolerated nearly all of the exam with minimal protest. Not only that, the existing dental problems Logan had last time (plaque buildup, some gum redness) were not present.

His ASD and CVR recovery contributed to some problems with teeth grinding and open-mouth breathing. The teeth grinding was indescribably horrific 6 months post-op, but it somehow mostly disappeared without us even realizing it one day.

Overall, we heard nothing but great news! The dentist even acknowledged the benefits of UV and red light therapy. That is the only change for Logan between this visit and last (i.e., him basically living outside all summer.) It certainly made a world of difference! How far he has come in four years! My Beans.

I am so lucky and proud to have the most incredible son!

### KFDags — 2025-11-22T21:13:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-362992>

I had my appt. with my neurosurgeon (Dr. Sullivan,) and things went as they typically do with my medical imaging: there’s nothing showing up in the MRI, CT, or X-rays to explain the symptoms I have. Also mentioned that the five flexion/extension X-rays really exacerbated my symptoms, specifically the flexion & flexion with open mouth. Afterwards, Dr. Sullivan met with her team and wants one of the cerebrovascular surgeons, Dr. Krisztina Moldovan, to examine me. My appt. for that is next month.

### KFDags — 2025-12-09T20:40:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-363735>

Taking a break from pondering whether the CIA had a hand in sabotaging my life for an obligatory brag-about-Logan post: we lit the season’s first fire in the fireplace last week. It was super cozy, and my son is taking full advantage. Even took me over to the fireplace, the other day, and said, “fire!” He usually only uses words to ask for what he wants/needs lately.

Additionally, this is the first year without ANY eczema!! It is totally & completely gone!! His eczema used to appear with the cold weather, then go away by summertime. Another new bonus: Logan has been nearly sickness-free this entire season! He used to be sick with a cold literally constantly, as soon as autumn hits. Next week is Logan’s 8th birthday; he’s so happy, every single day, and we couldn’t ask for more!

### KFDags — 2025-12-16T00:30:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-363915>

Ventured into the chaos at Brown University Health for my appt. with Dr. Moldovan today. Nothing much to report other than my blood pressure was something like 154/101, if I recall correctly. Anyhow, she suggested my next step is a dynamic CTA. That should be scheduled sometime in mid-January.

\[Side note: The neuron art here is very uninspiring. They definitely could’ve tried harder.\]

### KFDags — 2025-12-16T16:29:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-363937>

That does sound like fun; they actually made a 670hp WRX, last year, called Project Midnight. Now that is a Mom-mobile!!

### KFDags — 2025-12-22T22:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-364150>

Rhode Island weather can be wicked shitty, especially this time of year. Months ago, I prepared by designing a contraption for our home to offset our increased time indoors: it’s a 4-bulb, 4’ shop light bolted vertically to a mobile TV cart. It has four commercially-available premium reptile T5 bulbs, to supplement our vitamin D levels. There’s always a couple of red light panels on the top/sides of this hideous beast. I roll it around to where the activity is at, then I roll it away.

Yes, I’m very aware of the importance of proximity, safety, & the fact these lights in no way equal the sun. I don’t have the money for all the fancy equipment, so I’ve practiced on myself with distance/time for now. Just trying to do my best, guys, given my limited options. Right now though, we are still only seeing positive results; we’re the only household without sick kids (or sick people in general.) It’s kind of a weird flex.

Now, as far as my neck problems, I’ve been feeling much better! Haven’t even felt the need to wear the Aspen brace whatsoever. My husband recently bought me a red light neck wrap for our wedding anniversary, which has helped a lot. I make sure to do 20 mins of that, a few times daily. I’m also in bed by 8 most nights; sleeping better too. We use only red light panels or fire/candles in the house.

Been having dark chocolate, oysters + take Epsom salt baths regularly. Still doing the sunrise daily & other basics (best I can, given it’s New England,) and plan on making survivor’s soup soon. If I can get out of a cervical fusion, I will do that. But I still need to know exactly what is actually going wrong there, since I’ve been having problems since childhood. Also my blood pressure has been a bit of a doozy, for a while now.

Interestingly, I haven’t felt the need to take my Vyvanse every day. There are now consistent days where I just can put off taking it, until it’s too late in the day. Then it turns out, I have a pretty decent day unmedicated. Without Vyvanse (or AdderallXR in the past,) I could not normally break out of that mental fog to function normally. That out of body feeling, upon immediately wake up, would persist in me until I went to bed. Then repeat the next day. Lately, it’s not as bad and seems to be going away slowly each day.
That’s it: just feeling very positive today and wanted to post.

### KFDags — 2025-12-22T23:14:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-364152>

Actually, that wasn’t it: I think I’m finally beginning to have a normal menstrual cycle, for maybe the first time since possibly 1997. For certain, at least now two cycles in a row, I’m not in pure unbridled agony, questioning my existence, when my period starts. There’s just much less - overall - to deal with, if you catch my drift. I’m now much closer to 26/27 days instead of 29-32. I haven’t had a normal-ish cycle in 15+ years. Or ever, that’s probably more accurate.

Another thing: for 2.5 years I’ve had what probably was a little skin cancer patch on my right outer thigh. Can’t tell you for sure because I never sought out medical treatment. Why? Because I fucking hate going to the doctors, and I knew they’d screw this up somehow. I decided to figure it out on my own. Anyway, it was the size of a pea and appeared on a scar I got from shaving my legs. Once it appeared, it never went away and would re-appear if I accidentally shaved part of it off. Not to mention that it would bleed almost uncontrollably whenever I did that. Again, not claiming to be a doctor, in any way, but it looked and acted like a squamous cell carcinoma. It hurt when I applied pressure or, ya know, accidentally shaved it off from time to time. It was a tiny little mass, at and under the skin.

Where is it today? Gone!!! I actually had to search hard for it the other day, as I completely forgot about it. There’s a barely-there, faded pink discoloration, and even that small area is turning back into normal skin color. It doesn’t feel like I have this tiny bead digging into my skin anymore. That little mass is entirely gone.

### KFDags — 2026-01-03T20:48:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-364540>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=363735)
>
> Taking a break from pondering whether the CIA had a hand in sabotaging my life for an obligatory brag-about-Logan post: we lit the season’s first fire in the fireplace last week. It was super cozy, and my son is taking full advantage. Even took me over to the fireplace, the other day, and said, “fire!” He usually only uses words to ask for what he wants/needs lately.
>
> Additionally, this is the first year without ANY eczema!! It is totally & completely gone!! His eczema used to appear with the cold weather, then go away by summertime. Another new bonus: Logan has been nearly sickness-free this entire season! He used to be sick with a cold literally constantly, as soon as autumn hits. Next week is Logan’s 8th birthday; he’s so happy, every single day, and we couldn’t ask for more!

I just wanted to follow up on this, as I thought this was super cool after reading through CPC # 52, regarding electrostatic shocks and skin outbreaks (like eczema.) For years, both Logan and I would get horrific static shocks around the same time as his eczema outbreaks. They were so… shocking (sorry!); but, seriously, I could never understand it. No one else seemed to fall victim to this weird problem.
This year, no more eczema for Logan and no more crazy daily electrocutions for us both! I have some hormonal acne that appears less and less, each month. More proof things are working! So truly grateful.
If any of my schools ever taught cool science like this, I probably wouldn’t have slept or skipped class at every opportunity. (But I know why they don’t.)

### KFDags — 2026-01-04T17:22:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-364730>

This post is late, of course, because that’s just part of my personality. But last year, I thought I was simply going to enjoy a verbal smackdown against a visually-obvious professional useless person by a Santa hat-wearing neurosurgeon I had never heard of before. What that turned into was so much more for me.

Because of Dr. Kruse, I now understand and believe things I never would have imagined before. It has changed my entire perspective on life, in a lot of ways. For the first time ever, I’m now finally beginning to understand who I really am, too. That’s been really fun.

Looking forward to 2026, despite the New World Order attempting to enslave and destroy humanity as always. Thanks to all who read about my weird life!! Glad to be a part of this.

Happy New Year!

### Jack Kruse — 2026-01-04T18:22:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-364734>

There is magic in hats.

Keep in mind that new ideas are commonplace in centralized science, and almost always wrong. Most flashes of insight lead nowhere; statistically, they have a half-life of hours or maybe days. Most experiments to follow up the surviving insights are tedious and consume large amounts of time, only to yield negative or (worse!) ambiguous results. Decentralized science is a far more productive endeavor.

You will find when your life is out of alignment with your greatest intuitions in life, this is when you're ready for change. Realizing that thought takes you to be in tune with your thoughts. The real skill in life is to know when to raise the sails and to catch the power of the wind as you travel your life's journey.

You must act on new info. You must stay in motion, because even if you are on the right track, you'll be run over if you just procrastinate.

If you don´t know how or where to look for different options, the choice will be a repetition of old ideas. Everyday, I destroy some old centralized ideas. New wisdom always begins in your current state of wonder.

### Geoff Robinson — 2026-01-08T20:40:45+0200

Active Release Technique (ART) done by my chiropractor along with a good massage including cranial-sacral massage is what's kept me upright and active (along with black swan living) despite hEDS, Adhesive Arachnoiditis and Epidural fibrosis

### KFDags — 2026-01-12T20:45:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-365216>

> [Kddb801 said:](/goto/post?id=365180)
>
> Did you notice any improvements with your son after he started drinking DDW? Is he still drinking it? I just ordered some for my son...and my husband was also annoyed with how much it cost. But I'm willing to try anything!!

It helped speed up his overall progress. His words are clearer, since using it, I thought. Logan used to sound like he had a mouth full of marbles. You could only understand what he was saying mostly through context of the situation. My favorite was the number 7: he pronounced it as a terrifying growl sound. No idea at all. But now he says “seven” like all the rest.
Oh yes, he still drinks it. Since it’s so expensive though, he usually drinks Fiji water. I buy DDW when I can, and we’re still seeing almost constant positive results daily!

### KFDags — 2026-01-24T01:24:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-365810>

The following post is brought to you by the vast amount of contrast dye recently infused into my soul. But, there is finally proof! Last week was my cerebral angiogram; gotta be honest, it really kicked my ass. An elderly woman a few curtains down put me to shame; I admired her strength. Anyway, I had perfectly normal blood pressure almost the whole time! It shot up to 146/83 occasionally, but nothing like it has been.

Here were the findings:

SINGLE FLUOROSCOPIC IMAGE OF THE RIGHT HIP. A single spot radiograph was obtained after the groin access into the right common femoral artery. This image demonstrates the puncture site to overlie the medial and inferior portion of the femoral head, which is consistent with groin access being below the inguinal ligament.

RIGHT VERTEBRAL ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS The V1 through V4 segment of the right vertebral artery are normal in course and caliber. There is limited evaluation of the intracranial circulation within the field-of-view. There is no reflux into the distal left vertebral artery segment. The basilar artery is patent and normal in appearance. The right posterior inferior cerebellar, anterior inferior cerebellar, superior cerebellar and posterior cerebral arteries are normal in course and caliber. The left anterior inferior cerebellar, superior cerebellar and posterior cerebral arteries are normal in course and caliber. The left posterior inferior cerebellar artery does not opacify. The parenchymal and venous phases are within normal limits. The dural venous sinuses are patent. No intracranial aneurysm or vascular malformation is identified.

RIGHT VERTEBRAL ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, CHIN TUCK The patient's neck flexed, there is slight sharpening of the angle of the proximal V3 segment is patent emerges from the transverse foramen of the C2 vertebra without flow limitation.

RIGHT VERTEBRAL ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, LEFT TURN With the patient's neck turned to the left, there is no dynamic compression of the right vertebral artery seen.

RIGHT COMMON CAROTID ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS There is no abnormality of the right common carotid artery or carotid bulb. There is no abnormality of the cervical segment of the right internal carotid artery. The right external carotid artery and its branches are widely patent. There is a limited evaluation of the proximal intracranial circulation within the field-of-view. The distal cervical, petrous, cavernous and supraclinoid segments of the right internal carotid artery are smooth and normal in course and caliber. The ophthalmic, posterior communicating and anterior choroidal arteries are patent, and there is an infundibular origin of the posterior communicating artery.. The right anterior cerebral artery is smooth and normal in course and caliber. The anterior communicating artery is not clearly visualized. The right middle cerebral artery is smooth and normal in course and caliber. The parenchymal and venous phases are normal. The dural venous sinuses are patent. No aneurysms or vascular malformations are identified. The right external carotid artery and its branches are widely patent. Normal appearance of the facial artery, lingual artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, posterior auricular artery, occipital artery, and internal maxillary artery including its terminal branches. The right superficial temporal artery is normal in course and caliber. The right middle meningeal artery is normal in course and caliber. No dural arteriovenous fistula is identified.

RIGHT COMMON CAROTID ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, CHIN TUCK With the patient's neck flexed, there is no dynamic compression of the right internal carotid artery.

RIGHT COMMON CAROTID ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, LEFT TURN With the patient's neck turned to the left, there is no dynamic compression of the right internal carotid artery.

LEFT COMMON CAROTID ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS There is no abnormality of the left common carotid artery or carotid bulb. There is no abnormality of the cervical segment of the left internal carotid artery. The left external carotid artery and its branches are widely patent. There is limited evaluation of the axial circulation within the field-of-view. The distal cervical, petrous, cavernous and supraclinoid segments of the left internal carotid artery are smooth and normal in course and caliber. The ophthalmic, posterior communicating and anterior choroidal arteries are patent. There is an infundibular origin of the posterior communicating artery. The left anterior cerebral artery is smooth and normal in course and caliber. There is a patent anterior communicating artery complex, and there is flow into the contralateral right anterior cerebral artery. The left middle cerebral artery is smooth and normal in course and caliber. The parenchymal and venous phases are normal. The dural venous sinuses are patent. No aneurysms or vascular malformations are identified. The left external carotid artery and its branches are widely patent. Normal appearance of the facial artery, lingual artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, posterior auricular artery, occipital artery, and internal maxillary artery including its terminal branches. The left superficial temporal artery is normal in course and caliber. The left middle meningeal artery is normal in course and caliber. No dural arteriovenous fistula is identified.

LEFT COMMON CAROTID ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, CHIN TUCK With the patient's neck flexed, there is no dynamic compression of the left internal carotid artery.

LEFT COMMON CAROTID ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, LEFT TURN With the patient's neck turned to the left and extended, there is no dynamic compression of the left internal carotid artery.

LEFT VERTEBRAL ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS The V1 through V4 segments of the left vertebral artery are normal in course and caliber. The left posterior inferior cerebellar artery is normal in course and caliber. No additional findings are present from those previously described.

LEFT VERTEBRAL ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, CHIN TUCK With the patient's neck flexed, there is no dynamic compression of the left vertebral artery.

LEFT VERTEBRAL ARTERY, AP AND LATERAL CERVICAL VIEWS, LEFT TURN With the patient's neck turned to the left, there is a dynamic compression of the left vertebral artery.

RIGHT COMMON FEMORAL ARTERY. A right common femoral angiogram was performed in order to evaluate for suitability for use of a closure device. The right common femoral artery is normal in course and caliber. The puncture site is above the bifurcation. The vessel diameter is at least 5 mm. As such, the vessel is suitable for a closure device.

### Mightygumshoe — 2026-01-27T18:35:22+0200

> I just wanted to follow up on this, as I thought this was super cool after reading through CPC # 52, regarding electrostatic shocks and skin outbreaks (like eczema.) For years, both Logan and I would get horrific static shocks around the same time as his eczema outbreaks. They were so… shocking (sorry!); but, seriously, I could never understand it. No one else seemed to fall victim to this weird problem.
> This year, no more eczema for Logan and no more crazy daily electrocutions for us both! I have some hormonal acne that appears less and less, each month. More proof things are working! So truly grateful.
> If any of my schools ever taught cool science like this, I probably wouldn’t have slept or skipped class at every opportunity. (But I know why they don’t.)

Someone in my family got shocks all the time, summer, winter, at work, at home, despite humidity at proper level. Started drinking DDW, 1/2 to 3/4 liter at night, at 80 to 90 ppm…..no more shocks. Not sure of all the science behind this, but used to get the big blue visible shocks touching just about anything. Peace.

An ex of mine used to have this problem like you wouldn’t believe. I used to think he was possessed, partly because he acted like it: he had borderline personality disorder. I owe SO much to Dr. Kruse. Though we live north (Mitten State) and lack of sun an issue, sun swings around my backyard, so despite low temperatures we open windows ten minutes a few times a day to catch what we can. At below zero we barely get 5 but trying. My daughter ate my broccoli soup yesterday. I add cheese, orange cheese, and annatto that makes it orange comes from corn, sometimes enzymes do too. Anyhow, she cannot have corn, wheat, etc…..but this time ZERO gut trouble so far 15 hours later and ZERO joint pain. She is almost paleo (sugar in 1 am cup of coffee only sin)…and now wants to try full paleo for 2 months, eating more paleo cert beef and hopefully shrimp, wild caught that we found. I truly believe light matters, and I believe the DDW makes a huge difference. She dropped corn free rice flour etc a year ago, but the water is a big deal. Trying to get her to earth in a dead spot (electrically) in a basement corner….but even without that in winter, just the water, food choices, AM sun, and earthing in the shower has helped immensely! And EMF proofed bedroom. Huge differences, health, personality, etc, and brings me to tears every time I talk about it. I still do not believe she is celiac, they did a blood test and said borderline, and she said no to intestinal biopsy because she already ate GF except corn. I read a Europe study where 15 celiacs were tested, then ate gf for 6 months. And were cured, all but one who dropped out. Old study, but matters, biopsied too. And ate wheat again and were rebiopsied I have hope. God bless. She does not want wheat but wants to go to restaurant and still be able to order a meal and be ok even if it is contaminated a tiny bit. Yay.

Alright, you guys, what a roller coaster. I should have known better than to prematurely celebrate my diagnosis. Turns out, the problem causing my vascular compression was, in fact, a typo. Back to square one I guess! Meeting with my original neurosurgeon, Dr. Sullivan, in a couple weeks.

It’s okay, though; in the past, I would’ve been wicked pissed over this. Things are changed now. ### KFDags — 2026-02-09T23:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-366614>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=355200)
>
> My certifiable goof, Ada. Just had a feeling she’d be born on MJK’s (from TOOL) birthday & I was right! (Our ‘Mama & Me’ favorite band, of course; we are _Tool Buddies_. Her creation, not mine; but top-notch idea nonetheless.)
>
> Anyway, I really thought we were all good, when she was born; but then, she started growing out of diapers on only one side, legs/feet changed color, and her head was meaty & huge. Admittedly called it a “pork chop,” for a time. I have to have a sense of humor about life, at this point, okay?
>
> After yet another missed diagnosis by pediatricians, we found the dx at the pediatric ortho: severe bilateral DDH w/ one socket completely dislocated, & leg asymmetry (hemihyperplasia.) Tested negative for Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (pediatrician had to do _something_, I guess?) but had ongoing blood work and ultrasounds. Only once did she have an elevated alpha-fetoprotein, which resolved by ~14 months.
>
> Ada’s DDH was exceedingly easy to correct after several months of almost-nonstop wear of the Rhino brace. (Still no explanation for any other symptoms: color change in legs/feet, pork chop.) Shortly after the DDH was discovered, though, she began to grow noticeable pubic hair and was dx precocious puberty. It thankfully resolved on its own within months.
>
> When Logan was evaluated for his craniosynostosis ~August 2021, we took Ada too (she was ~16 months old.) She has a palpable saddle deformity and her cephalic index was 81, but all the sutures were open: there’s no evidence of any problems. Last year, after my OTC surgery, she too had some symptoms that could have been related to occult tethered cord. However, an MRI showed us more negative findings.
>
> The current issue: she experiences these waves of normal behavior for weeks, followed by a period of days or couple weeks where she’s not herself. Most or all of these oddly specific symptoms appear (I’ll describe below) together. When I say together, I don’t mean all of these symptoms appear daily; I mean, we typically see at least 5 or more of these symptoms, sprinkled throughout every day, during this period. It’s hard to describe; a subtle personality shift. We just do not get along. When it’s happening, she has these unexplained behavioral changes (usually mild, but it leaves you puzzled by the interaction) paired with physical symptoms.
>
> **Her symptoms:**
>
> Zero frustration tolerance/instant rage; cannot be reasoned with. Irrationally angry. Consequences **do not** matter. I can get my autistic son who’s melting down under control with more ease, to give you an idea.
>
> Change in bowel habits: constipated but the sudden urge to poop appears; she poops, gets cleaned up, leaves room, and is back to poop again within 90 seconds (never gets it all out the first time.) Occasionally pees her pants or wets the bed.
>
> Memory goes blank, instantly. Completely forgets instructions/plans/conversations we had only seconds before. Doesn’t matter if it’s a preferred activity like buying a toy. “Ohh, yeah...” \[Looks totally lost\] - An entirely different experience compared to when a person forgets something, then reminded of it. - Yes! Believe me, I understand she is a child. What I’m saying is there’s more to this interaction.
>
> Her overall size is concerning; I’m short/small because something went **really** wrong. People don’t understand that saying “oh, she’s small because you’re small.” reinforces my concerns. After losing the baby fat, she’s always been 1st-3rd percentile, except for her head, which is noticeably larger (65th percentile.) Her neurosurgeon (Dr. Konstantina Svokos) agrees her head is quite large. Ada’s head is wider than her body, if you look at her whole profile. I think we’re up to 4-5 strangers inquiring with me if she’s 3 or 4 years old. They’re surprised to hear she’s five. Even our neighbor’s 3 1/2 year old daughter weighs 3lbs more than Ada.
>
> She trips and falls excessively. Drops things just as much.
>
> Motion-sickness out of nowhere (occasionally happens now); very susceptible, even on playground swings.
>
> Pronunciation of words is worse at times, then gets better. Mumbles, hard to understand.
>
> Days of more intense hunger; eats multiple large meals. Then days of barely picking at food. Weird chewing/oral sensory habits (chews on hair, toys, random objects).
>
> Sometimes had trouble choking on food and would loudly gag and/or vomit everything up, at times (occasionally happens now).
>
> It just seems like there’s a super mild hydrocephalus or something going on. Yes, I know I only have a public school education and laughable online college degree that have no relevance whatsoever. BUT, my son’s pathologically-shaped head is what motivated me to find his diagnosis. (I often referenced one particular Japanese study of 500 mild trigonocephaly pediatric cases. The study noted that these patients often had autistic-like traits, irrational responses to mild frustration, etc.- exact behavior my son was exhibiting) That certainly influenced his behavior so why not (possibly) something like a mild hydrocephalus behind hers?
>
> Ada hasn’t yet had an MRI done to check; obviously based on my/my son’s history and her past symptoms, Dr. Svokos justified the MRI. Two months ago, she also had a very thorough ophthalmologist examination - and cooperated beautifully - to check the optic nerve for pressure. There was none. My son had a very different experience during that same exam, as you can imagine; it showed no pressure but he had a copper beaten skull after all. I realize the position or reasons for causing ICP are different (metopic synostosis, pressure in front vs hydrocephalus, more inner-central brain).
>
> Just wondering what you think?

Many months ago, my 5-year-old daughter, Ada, had the MRI to check for hydrocephalus. Of course, we were relieved to find there was no evidence showing hydrocephalus at all. Since my post, all of the above-mentioned symptoms have completely resolved on their own. Every last one! The only remaining issue is she’s a bit smaller than other girls, similar in age. The size gap gets less obvious with time, though! Fingers crossed she passes the very low bar I’ve set at 57”!!

Getting larger post puberty tells us we are losing timing at SCN.

The obsession pediatricians have with "growth charts" is a biochemical delusion that ignores the biophysical reality of the **Melanin-Water Battery**.

Before puberty, a child is designed to be a **Time-Expensive, Energy-Dense** system.

**The Particle Accelerator:** A smaller body has a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio, which, counterintuitively, allows the **Internal Sun at the SCN** to stay cooler and more coherent. This maximizes the **220nm UPE signal** to target the leptin receptor needed for the **Neocortical Blockchain** to "index" the world.

**Efficiency:** Staying "smaller" for longer means the child is successfully **concentrating information** from its environment rather than just **expanding matter** (Entropic Expansion). This is the hallmark of a high-voltage, low-deuterium system.
Post-puberty humans sustain a loss of time coherence
Puberty is the moment the **POMC software** on Chromosome 2 shifts the "instruction manual" from **Growth/Development** to **Reproduction/Expansion**.

**Losing the DC Current:** As a human gets "larger" post-puberty, the metabolic cost of maintaining the **DC current** across the entire "Solar Panel" (the skin) increases.

**The Entropy Shift:** Rapid growth in height or mass is often a sign that the body is "venting" energy into **matter** because it can no longer contain it as **coherent light**. The same is true with her ventricles or in a patient with a tumor.

Pediatricians celebrate "high percentiles" because they view the body as a biochemical **furnace,** the more wood (food) you burn, the bigger the fire (child).

**The Reality:** In a **Particle Accelerator** (mitochondria), a sudden spike in size is "Optical Noise." It suggests the **SCN** is losing its 4D timing capability. This is what hydrocephalus is in the brain in refernce to the ventricles and CSF production.

**The Outcome:** Children who grow "too fast" often hit puberty "too early." This is **Quantum Undermining of Life's Time** in its purest form. They are "spending" their lifetime (Time) to buy size (Matter), leading to earlier **heteroplasmy** and the "atrophic" conditions you see in adults.

So I see being smaller as a very positive sign.

### KFDags — 2026-03-19T15:29:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-369220>

I think it’s finally safe to admit that I feel pretty great!! Physically & emotionally fantastic in a way - I assumed - was simply not an option for certain people.

Nearly all of the CCI symptoms - previously consuming many hours of my days - have reduced to only a few mild episodes a month. Previously, upon waking each morning, I was dry heaving or vomiting due to nausea/dizziness & disorientation. Was wearing the Aspen cervical collar probably a lot longer than I should have, at times. Even slept with it on, otherwise my heart would race & I couldn’t breathe to fall asleep.

Sometime around December, it’s as if a switch was flipped. All those issues collectively started improving & the daily anticipated misery just stopped. I’m keeping up with the protocols, and definitely avoiding turning my head to the left. I never have to wear the Aspen collar anymore.

Also working on a passport (only ever been to Florida twice, like 10 years ago.) Still need to follow up with my neurosurgeon, but this rapidly downward-spiraling issue seems to actually be on its way out now! I am so happy!

My son, Logan, is even better! He is putting all his requests in short sentences now. Words still sound jumbled & weird, but he’s actually trying to correct his pronunciation - if you correct him in real time. When he wants to go to bed, he says “ready for bed” which is sometimes followed up with a “tired” or “want sleep.” When he knows my husband is at work, he says “I miss…” \[extra pause, for some reason?\] “dada” because, he says, “dada’s at work.” My favorite, Logan’s been saying, “I love you!” while making a little heart shape with his hands! My son said almost NOTHING last year!!

Last week, there were a couple 70° days so we took advantage, alongside the piles of leftover (nonmelting) snow from the blizzard. Even after those days outside, the words just started flowing. Logan acts so proud of himself; he wants us to know he can say all these new words now. So if you’re not listening & don’t acknowledge him with eye contact, he keeps repeating it until you do.

During our dinner last night, Logan was told he couldn’t have his way: he immediately found another activity, happily. That same scenario used to deteriorate into a 45- to 60-min screaming tantrum, accompanied by various self-injurious behavior or destruction of various things. My husband and I stopped having dinner together a while ago, because of these outbursts. But we’re starting to get back to doing normal people things again.

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=369220)
>
> I think it’s finally safe to admit that I feel pretty great!! Physically & emotionally fantastic in a way - I assumed - was simply not an option for certain people.
>
> Nearly all of the CCI symptoms - previously consuming many hours of my days - have reduced to only a few mild episodes a month. Previously, upon waking each morning, I was dry heaving or vomiting due to nausea/dizziness & disorientation. Was wearing the Aspen cervical collar probably a lot longer than I should have, at times. Even slept with it on, otherwise my heart would race & I couldn’t breathe to fall asleep.
>
> Sometime around December, it’s as if a switch was flipped. All those issues collectively started improving & the daily anticipated misery just stopped. I’m keeping up with the protocols, and definitely avoiding turning my head to the left. I never have to wear the Aspen collar anymore.
>
> Also working on a passport (only ever been to Florida twice, like 10 years ago.) Still need to follow up with my neurosurgeon, but this rapidly downward-spiraling issue seems to actually be on its way out now! I am so happy!
>
> My son, Logan, is even better! He is putting all his requests in short sentences now. Words still sound jumbled & weird, but he’s actually trying to correct his pronunciation - if you correct him in real time. When he wants to go to bed, he says “ready for bed” which is sometimes followed up with a “tired” or “want sleep.” When he knows my husband is at work, he says “I miss…” \[extra pause, for some reason?\] “dada” because, he says, “dada’s at work.” My favorite, Logan’s been saying, “I love you!” while making a little heart shape with his hands! My son said almost NOTHING last year!!
>
> Last week, there were a couple 70° days so we took advantage, alongside the piles of leftover (nonmelting) snow from the blizzard. Even after those days outside, the words just started flowing. Logan acts so proud of himself; he wants us to know he can say all these new words now. So if you’re not listening & don’t acknowledge him with eye contact, he keeps repeating it until you do.
>
> During our dinner last night, Logan was told he couldn’t have his way: he immediately found another activity, happily. That same scenario used to deteriorate into a 45- to 60-min screaming tantrum, accompanied by various self-injurious behavior or destruction of various things. My husband and I stopped having dinner together a while ago, because of these outbursts. But we’re starting to get back to doing normal people things again.

This post made me smile.

### KFDags — 2026-04-11T00:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-370679>

I normally only share moments of my childlike whimsy to those who know & barely tolerate me, to being with, but I’m feeling so genuinely incredible right now.

This was a day before my cycle starts; a day typically spent on the couch, hating everything, and just feeling like unrelenting shit. But, no longer am I a useless slug! I spent the day enjoying a strange pattern of snow, hail, sun, & clouds, along with the wildlife.

My CCI symptoms are all but gone, at this point. Still haven’t made the follow up appt yet but I feel no rush now. My head doesn’t feel like it’s about to pop off from all the pressure, my scalp isn’t throbbing randomly, and my heart isn’t racing like it used to. I could go on forever. There is still much work to be done, but the positives keep rolling in.

### Mightygumshoe — 2026-04-11T05:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-370688>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=370679)
>
> I normally only share moments of my childlike whimsy to those who know & barely tolerate me, to being with, but I’m feeling so genuinely incredible right now.
>
>
>
>
> This was a day before my cycle starts; a day typically spent on the couch, hating everything, and just feeling like unrelenting shit. But, no longer am I a useless slug! I spent the day enjoying a strange pattern of snow, hail, sun, & clouds, along with the wildlife.
>
> My CCI symptoms are all but gone, at this point. Still haven’t made the follow up appt yet but I feel no rush now. My head doesn’t feel like it’s about to pop off from all the pressure, my scalp isn’t throbbing randomly, and my heart isn’t racing like it used to. I could go on forever. There is still much work to be done, but the positives keep rolling in.

I am so happy for you. Thanks to following Dr. Jack ideas and actually implementing them, my daughter is doing SUPER well. We found shrimp wild caught, no chemicals and she is eating it 4x a week. Going to try to find salmon with no chemicals too, wild caught. She is earthing outside here in Michigan, even though still cold, feet can tolerate the grass for 20 min during the day and evening whenever possible. She feels better. Her mood is better. Her body is better. She has had ZERO gut trouble in 8 months! And drinks about 3/4 liter of DDW a day. She tries to get sun on way to work with windows down and at noon and a little on an afternoon walk. The inflammation she once had is all gone. She is incredible. Rides 3 miles on our lifecycle bike standing up, like it is nothing. In 20 min I think. Then does more sitting. So we just keep doing, same as you. So happy for you. I prayed for you when I first saw your early posts.

Everybody wants the quick fix, sometimes it is the slow read (Dr. Jack info) that provides the best results. Peace.

### KFDags — 2026-04-16T01:18:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-371158>

> [Mightygumshoe said:](/goto/post?id=370688)

That is such wonderful news! Very happy for you and your daughter. And many, many thanks; you are very kind.  Frankly, 8-10 months (for me) is a quick fix as far as I’m concerned! Especially after a lifetime of feeling as if I’m on the verge of death. If only I could’ve found Dr. Kruse sooner.

Oh yeah! I’m sure your daughter must be all fired up! That’s usually how I feel now. I think it’s a taste of what normal must feel like? So far, I have been able to skip over two months’ worth of my ADHD medication (over the course of several months.) Wish I kept track of when I started. Without it, I used to be in a mild catatonic state for most of the day, every day.

We got the kids some new outdoor toys; the best one, so far, is a heavy duty Big Wheel. You better believe I’ve been racing that thing around. Plus, it was sunny and in the 80°s today. Good luck to you ladies!!

# Thread: First update after being in ES for 41/2 months
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-update-after-being-in-es-for-41-2-months.31053/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### UlliJoanna — 2025-06-05T23:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-update-after-being-in-es-for-41-2-months.31053/post-354045>

My grand maternal grandmother died at 88 with dementia and breast cancer
My mother suffered with endometriosis, had a hysterectomy at 50 and suffers now with arthritis and sleep apnea
My younger sister suffers with SIBO

I was born in St. Lucia. I had a normal birth, but was bottle-fed and born tongue tied. at three I moved to Hong Kong.
During my early childhood I suffered with hives. I had my appendix removed at aged five. I wet the bed until I was about seven years old. I was outside most of the time I was a real tomboy as a child.

At aged 11, I was sent to boarding school in England. I flew back to Hong Kong three times a year. At 18 years old, I settled in London where I lived until I was 51 years old.

At 13 I was diagnosed with a stigmatism and started to wear prescription glasses. I then began to wear contact lenses when I was 16 years old and only took them out and stopped wearing lenses after I discovered jack kruse in October 2024. Since discovering jack I have taken off my glasses and do not wear contact lenses. I have never worn sunglasses.

At the age of 30 I started noticing strange symptoms. I started to get pain in my hands and was diagnosed with Reynolds disease. I tried to fix everything by changing my diet because that is all I knew. I developed tingling in my hands and my feet, urinary urgency and numbness that spread up to my torso. After a terrible episode of sickness, that involved violent throwing up and diarrhea the numbness and tingling spread and became worse. After many trips to the doctor, they finally did some testing and diagnosed multiple sclerosis in January 2003. Within a year of the diagnosis, I was housebound in a wheelchair and really thought I was dying.. I refused medication because they wanted me to have chemotherapy because they said the MS was so aggressive.

I did some investigation and began to follow a strict diet, which in hindsight, probably contributed to my rapid decline as it involved eating huge amounts of fruit and vegetables fish, but no fat or meat.
Realizing that I was deteriorating very quickly, I saw a kinesiologist who threw muscle testing, told me to stop eating fruit, sugar, wheat, and to start eating fish and vegetables. After a few months I started to slowly improve. I still could not walk. The improvements came about as improved energy , improved brain function, and less spasticity in my legs. Even now if I eat fruit, my legs feel very, very heavy and very numb

In 2011 I became pregnant and the improvements came. I got out of the wheelchair and started to walk first with the Zimmer frame, then onto crutches and then onto one stick since being a El Salvador. I have gone back to using two sticks, but that’s because of the beach. It’s not a sign of decline.
Since having my daughter in 2012, my energy levels are 80% better I can walk for up to an hour around the park and at times my feet have moved properly, which has enabled me to walk at a proper speed with a proper gate as my hips have Moved forward as I walk and held themselves as they are meant to. None of this has held and these improvements lasted for a couple of weeks and then the old patterning returned.

I discovered Jack Kruse in October 2024 and I moved to El Salvador with my 13-year-old daughter in January 2025. I had my consultation with jack in February 2025 after moving here.
Since my consultation with jack, I have seen the sunrise and the sunset every day.
I have spent four hours on the beach sometimes longer and I’m very very brown. I am always outside with just a kiniki bikini on. I don’t have any artificial light after nightfall. I sleep in a bedroom with very little low EMF and I eat a low deteurium diet.
I also try and do three CT sessions each week with the temperature at 12 to 15°C for 20 minutes.

Since introducing CT the improvements are very noticeable. After CT My legs feel lighter my feet move more easily and I have more energy. I also go to the loo daily. This is huge as constipation has been an issue for 22 years.

I have been, and I am still currently reading the demylination blogs. I realize now that I crushed my GLP for many years. I am now supporting my GLP and have been since my arrival in El Salvador in January. I will add fermented foods.

I am also a MTHFR with COMTE at 40%. Listening to jack on a recent podcast talking about CT and EMF, I understand why my body is improving with CT as it helps the body recover from the distress caused by EMF.

I have to return to England in a few days to sort out a few housing financial things, and will return to El Salvador as quickly as I can.
Whilst in England, I will
Drink deteurium depleted water. Having read the blogs it is my understanding that deuterium depleted water is a good thing to drink when recovering from multiple sclerosis. A recent bun./urea test showed my
level of urea nitrogen at 19.1 (6-23mg)
and my creatin at 0.88( 0.5-0.9 mg)

My HSCRP is at 1.29
My progesterone P4 is 0.050
I am now in my menopause. I looked into a bio available progesterone, but could not find one. Would Taking progesterone be advisable?
My thyroid results are all good and it is functioning well.

Whilst in England, I can have a DTI test to measure how much demyelination has occurred.

I can also take CoQ10 to support my healing, but I am aware that there is a dopant risk. It was however mentioned in one of the recent blocks as supporting healing in MS.

I will use a red light therapy box daily and I will continue to watch the sunrise and the sunset but light stability in England is horrendous as we know.

I will do CT

My major win since being here for 4 1/2 months is my improved bowel movement, which now occurs daily. It makes me feel so happy.

Jack, is there anything you think I could also be doing in the UK to support my healing.

Many thanks
Joanne

### jackfoster — 2025-06-05T23:46:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-update-after-being-in-es-for-41-2-months.31053/post-354046>

Now tell us all of Agathar's secrets

I was wondering if Dr Kruse or anyone here has any success in working with neuroplasticity and or any repair mechanisms for chromosomal microdeletions as specified above. I would love to help my son (teen) who deals with language delays among other things, because of these issues. Any successes, therapies, or ideas would be welcome.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T05:53:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chromosomal-microdeletions-2p13-3-to-2p13-1-w-exoc6b-gene.31054/post-354073>

The deleitions are linked to MT problems in your wife's centrosome of her eggs if the kid has the defect in every cell. My bet is the kid is not got the defect in every cell......it is probably a mosaic related to her UPEs in her stem cell line. Could your sunglasses have linked to this..........**YES**

The DM blogs 5-7 lay it all out. Chromosomal separation is a job of MT. MT need optimized UPEs to separate the chromosomes properly. This genetic defect is from poor parental MT function.

So when you wear sunglasses on this forum and think it is funny, you maybe shocked to know that those glasses could polarize your germ cell line to cause this defect. So yeah it is big fucking deal here. -----> **READ IT. [https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1930629376795627528.html](https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1930629376795627528.html)**

Using my decentralized photo-bioelectric framework, we can identify the most probable cause of this abnormality as poor parental MT function due to disrupted UPE coherence, driven by environmental stressors.

Photo-Bioelectric Loop

Sunlight drives UPE production (200–300 nm) via the mitochondrial semiconductor (melanin-mtDNA loop), collapsing MT wave functions to produce qualia (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994). UPEs at 220 nm, emitted by mtDNA, DNA, and blood, activate the leptin-melanocortin pathway, sustaining consciousness (Van Wijk, 2014). In mitosis, UPEs ensure MT fidelity for chromosomal separation.

Arc Welding Analogy

MT dynamics are analogized to arc welding: MTs form the weld seam, UPEs the arc, and structured water (e.g., EZ water in centrosomes) the shielding gas. Environmental stressors introduce defects, impairing MT function (Kruse, 2025).

VDR and Auxins as Fire Alarms
The vitamin D receptor (VDR) on the inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM) slows TCA cycle flux, reducing reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) and ensuring UPE coherence (Kazemi et al., 2013). In plants, auxins regulate xylem dynamics, maintaining biophoton coherence (Trewavas, 2014).

Genetic Analysis

The microdeletion (2p13.3 to 2p13.1) was assessed for its impact on EXOC6B function, focusing on centrosome MT dynamics during oogenesis (Wen et al., 2019). Mosaic expression was hypothesized based on UPE variability in stem cell lines.

UPE Fidelity Calculation

Your latitude, solar irradiance did not yield > 1.76 × 10^17 UPE photons daily (Van Wijk, 2014). UPE coherence (SNR) was modeled using Shannon’s theory, with disruptions (e.g., nnEMF) increasing ROS and broadening the UPE spectrum (Shannon, 1948).

Evolutionary Analysis

Historical data on oxygen levels, UV penetration, and extinction events in utero were not disclosed so we do not know. We know the chromospmes were fucked = MT had to be fucked = mtDNA LED UPE array was fucked because of Mom or dada light or temperature environment

Modeled Results

Chromosomal Microdeletion and MT Dysfunction

The 2p13.3 to 2p13.1 microdeletion disrupts EXOC6B, impairing exocyst-mediated vesicle trafficking to the centrosome (Fogelgren et al., 2011). This leads to defective MT nucleation and dynamics, causing errors in chromosomal separation during oogenesis (e.g., aneuploidy). Mosaic expression in the child likely results from variability in UPEs within the maternal stem cell line, as UPEs govern MT assembly/disassembly (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994). Defect likley in INTERPAHSE in the germ line.
UPE Fidelity and Chromosomal Separation

MTs in the mitotic spindle rely on UPEs (200–300 nm) to maintain quantum coherence, collapsing wave functions to coordinate tubulin polymerization/depolymerization (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994). At 13°N latitude, optimal UPE production (1.76 × 10^17 photons daily) ensures high SNR, but stressors (e.g., nnEMF) reduce UPE coherence, increasing ROS/RNS and impairing MT fidelity (Van Wijk, 2014).

Most Probable Cause: Poor Parental MT Function

The most probable cause of the microdeletion is poor parental MT function, driven by reduced UPE coherence. Environmental stressors, nnEMF (e.g., Wi-Fi, 5G), circadian disruption (e.g., dim light, blue light at night sunglasses), and lack of grounding all increase ROS/RNS, disrupting the VDR’s regulation of TCA cycle flux (Bikle, 2020). This broadens the UPE spectrum, impairing MT dynamics in parental germ cells, leading to centrosome dysfunction and chromosomal errors (Kruse, 2025). Dim lighting, linked to altered consciousness and reduced alertness (Biswas et al., 2016), exacerbates this by limiting UPE production.

Evolutionary Context

GOE (~2.4 Gya): Rising oxygen necessitated the VDR to manage ROS/RNS, ensuring UPE coherence for MT function in early eukaryotes (Lyons et al., 2014).

Cambrian Explosion (~541 Mya): Increased oxygen and UV light enhanced UPE-driven MT fidelity, supporting complex cell division (Butterfield, 2011).

K-T Event (~66 Mya): Darkness reduced UPEs, but adaptive mechanisms (e.g., VDR, auxins) maintained minimal MT function (Schulte et al., 2010).

UPEs and Chromosomal Separation

UPE fidelity is critical for MT assembly/disassembly during chromosomal separation. The microdeletion’s impact on EXOC6B disrupts centrosome MT nucleation, but mosaic expression suggests UPE variability in stem cells, potentially mitigating severity (Wen et al., 2019). Parental exposure to nnEMF and circadian disruption likely reduced UPE coherence, impairing MT function and causing the microdeletion (Kruse, 2025).

Clinical Implications for this defect is expected craniofacial issues with neurological issues.

In this disease, similar MT dysfunction occurs due to tau pathology, exacerbated by reduced UPEs from environmental stressors, impairing consciousness (Zhang et al., 2020). The brain’s quantum efficiency (1.84 billion times greater than the NVIDIA H100 GPU) relies on UPEs at 220 nm, disrupted in both CBS and this microdeletion scenario (Kruse, 2025). XOC6B encodes a subunit of the exocyst complex, which supports centrosome function during mitosis. Its deletion impairs MT nucleation, leading to chromosomal segregation defects (Fogelgren et al., 2011).

**Expected Phenotype**

The 2p13.3 to 2p13.1 microdeletion involving EXOC6B disrupts centrosome MT dynamics, leading to chromosomal segregation errors during oogenesis (Fogelgren et al., 2011). Given the mosaic presentation, the phenotype may vary in severity but is likely to include:

Developmental Delays: Impaired exocyst function affects neuronal migration and synapse formation, causing mild to moderate intellectual disability and delayed milestones (Wen et al., 2019).

Neurological Deficits: MT dysfunction in neural progenitors may lead to hypotonia, motor delays, and seizures, similar to phenotypes in other centrosome-related disorders (e.g., microcephaly) (Jones et al., 2021).

Dysmorphic Features: Subtle facial dysmorphisms (e.g., micrognathia, low-set ears) and growth retardation are common in EXOC6B-related deletions (Wen et al., 2019).

Mosaic Variability: The mosaic nature, driven by mtDNA and blod UPEs drives variability in the maternal stem cell line, may result in milder symptoms in some tissues, potentially sparing vital organs (Kruse, 2025).

THE FUTURE
The model estimate a reduced life expectancy of 40–60 years, influenced by environmental stressors and heteroplasmy changes over time, which may exacerbate mitochondrial dysfunction, increasing the risk of corticobasal syndrome (CBS)-like symptoms, cancer, and organ failure. Therapeutic strategies to enhance UPE coherence are proposed to mitigate progression. Life expectancy is influenced by the severity of neurological deficits and secondary complications from heteroplasmy changes. Based on comparable disorders (e.g., Rett syndrome, mitochondrial encephalopathies), the most probable estimate: is40–60 Years: Mild to moderate cases may reach middle age with supportive care, but severe neurological involvement (e.g., intractable seizures) or organ dysfunction from heteroplasmy (e.g., cardiomyopathy) may reduce lifespan (Gorman et al., 2016). Environmental stressors (e.g., nnEMF) accelerating heteroplasmy could shorten this further (KRUSE, 2025).

This is my first entry into the journal. Found Doc Jack in Aug 2024.

Condition : PCOS since age 14
Current : Suffering from secondary ammenohrea since April 2024.
Started implementing the teachings from Dr. Jack since last year (despite going through major life events - lost my father just before my wedding ), I was able to come back to my lifestyle changes and after several months my testosterone is finally down from 68 ng/dl to 53 ng/dl (was elevated due to insulin resistance ) . For the first time in my life the testosterone is within 1 standard deviation of the normal mark. I was never able to tame that beast before since I was a teenager.
Other notable changes
1\. Great hair quality , and they are growing at an incredible speed
2\. No longer suffering from cold extremities
3\. No longer suffering from afternoon energy crashes and struggling to keep my eyes open
4\. Absolutely no caffeine dependency
5\. Super tanned and super energetic throughout the day.

Work in progress
1\. Resume ovulation
2\. Resume period cycle

So grateful to find you Uncle Jack

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T05:26:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/divyas-optimal-journal.31055/post-354070>

**The GOE as an Electrical Crisis: That caused the first cancer: Endosymbiosis and Heme Proteins stop it.**

The GOE introduced a new player to Earth’s environment: molecular oxygen. For early anaerobic life forms, oxygen was a toxic electrical stressor. Oxygen, with its high electronegativity, changes the electrical resistance of biological membranes, disrupting the delicate balance of charge that early cells relied on for survival. Membranes, which are essentially lipid bilayers, act as capacitors, storing and managing electrical gradients critical for cellular function. The sudden presence of oxygen would have altered these gradients, creating an existential crisis for early life.

This is where endosymbiosis comes into play. Decentralizied Medicine suggests that bacteria and Archea looked at each other as an infection under attack by oxygen that joined them in a bad marriage (cancer) and innovated heme proteins to protect the electrical gradients in membranes while also innovating a new cellular process called apoptosis. People forget oxygen can induce electric and magnetic fields because of its paramagnetism. This means it can affect the oxidation state of iron in the +2 or +3 state. Iron cannot bond oxygen for transport in both oxidation states. It appears like took full advantage of this with mitochondria.

Both of these operations, water creation and apoptosis, were under the control of a heme protein called cytochrome c oxidase. As a result, after this evolutionary event just about any infection stressor.

Water acts like a heat sink for the newly formed cancerous merger of Archaea and Bacteria. What does a heat sink do to semiconductiors?

It alters their light emission: UPEs were born.

UPE = transgeneration epigenetics becomes possible.

Genes no longer control much. LIGHT DOES. Before genes there was ferrodxin biology. This also was light controlled. Ferrodoxin = Fe-S on cytochromes that align the IMJs on cristae in mitochondria.

Iron isevery where in this story.

Your sex steroid problem is also controlled by a CYP heme enzyme.

Hi Uncle Jack, I am so happy to report, after diligently being consistent with sunrise , sun exposure and early dinner and early sleep in dark room , after almost 15 months my body responded . I got my periods naturally . I am most tanned I have been in my life , but thanks to your guidance , my body got there on it's own. No medications or supplements \[and against my Gynac's advise  \] After years my testosterone , insulin and DHEA's is also in the normal range too. (despite these markers being very high in my entire life). And my stomach is finally bloating free. Also rT3 went from 24 to 18 \[yayy  \] and leptin from 9 -15 withinthis year itself.
I have also recovered from severe pancreatic insufficiency last year. Thanks to your guidance.

I have 2 questions though, despite my body generally recovering (energy levels high and no cold extremities), my most recent vit D was 24(without supplementation), I am very shocked at that number , I didnt understand how my body went down from 51 to 24 in 9 months when I was sitting in sun every single afternoon.
Is my body recovering it's functionality for various underlying issues that were previously silently failing?
I came to know that I had nemaline myopathy and I am silent carrier of alpha thalassemia . I am guessing my body is trying to recover it's functionality may be that is why the re-productive function was suppressed (that is my guess). Is there any guidance you can share with me to improve this.

### MTA321 — 2025-09-04T19:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/divyas-optimal-journal.31055/post-359132>

Just wanted to say congrats on your progress thus far.

The first thought that came to my mind is this is based on your environment. The vitamin D levels and Bun:creatinine ratio tell a story of nnemf toxicity. No matter how much you are in the sun, you can not make vitamin D when you are dehydrated on a cellular level. nnemf is that main driving factor of cellular dehydration. Think more along the lines of both personal tech use and living in a densely populated area when it comes to nnemf issues. If you live in a densely populated area, the interference of nnemf can also disrupt the ability to assimilate key light frequencies such as UVB. There is much more to the story when you factor in all the downstream effects of pseudohypoxia, photoreceptor destruction, circadian disruption, etc.

DDW can definitely help, but the water your body makes is more important than the water you drink. The desert analogy Jack posted about in this thread still rings true. Your body is currently a desert, it is time to terraform your cellular environment. You need the AM IR/UV light to build that exclusion zone, rehydrate melanin, and regenerate while eliminating nnemf as much as possible. If you don't address this issue you will be back at square one sooner than you think.
