>
> Building biologist would tell you to sell the house, but I'm all for extreme measures to mitigate.

NFA1000 does have single axis, though I've been using three-axis for these measurements & not sure how single axis would be helpful here. Totally agree on taking measures most would consider extreme to improve the health of the living space. It's the most important aspect of a house to me, so I just look at it like money that others would spend pointlessly upgrading the brand of their car or their wardrobe because they think they're impressing people, or at nightclubs trying to meet the type of people who I have no interest in meeting. Even if I move, the next place is probably going to have problems too - of course some places have more and worse problems than others, but there are also things I like about this place from a health perspective (lots of sun, relatively low RF, relatively quiet, relatively affordable which leaves me more money to spend on other health-related things), and moving is no walk in the park financially or time-wise either. Plus on a society-wide scale, my view is that people have got to deal with their problems instead of just figuring out how to pass them on to others - especially unsuspecting others. We are all dependent on each other at the end of the day. The fact that I'm aware of this problem probably means I'm more educated on this particular important issue than whoever I would sell the house to, but it doesn't necessarily mean I'm a smarter or more valuable person than them in general. I feel like I have somewhat of an obligation to at least try to address the problem rather than just kick it down the road to the next sap, especially since they're unlikely to be aware of it. Imagine if I sold the house because I was afraid of the health impacts of the magnetic fields from the power lines and the guy who bought it from me had no idea what magnetic fields were but turned out to have a pregnant wife whose kid wound up dying of leukemia because I only cared about the immediate impact of my actions on myself. Is that the type of society we want to live in? Ultimately it's short-term thinking to behave that way in my view because as I noted earlier we're all dependent on each other and anything I do for my own benefit that comes at a dramatically higher detriment to others should be presumed to come back and bite me in the ass in some way eventually through its negative impact on the people I hurt. Even if it's not true in every case, society is a much better place if as many people as possible behave as if it is (and are pressured into doing so, rather than encouraged to behave in the opposite way).

### Mat.I — 2025-07-08T03:54:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-355972>

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355952)
>
> My understanding is that shielding of low-frequency AC magnetic fields is accomplished using materials such as Mu-metal that have high permeability to redirect the fields, which is in contrast to the techniques used to shield low-frequency AC electric fields and radiofrequency waves, which I believe is generally accomplished using materials that reflect or attenuate them. I am not sure I fully grasp the low-frequency AC magnetic field shielding mechanism of materials with high permeability and have some applied questions below involving three shielding scenarios hoping to increase my understanding (and also hoping this could be helpful to others as well). Answers to any of the below, even if not going through every piece of every question, would likely be super helpful.
>
> Say you have a main utility feeder powerline. The current going through this powerline generates AC magnetic fields in continuous circles with the powerline at the center of each circle, with the fields progressively decreasing in intensity with distance from the powerline. Now say you have a house 20 feet from this powerline that is exposed to the AC magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current.
>
> **(1) Powerline Cylinder Enclosure**: Would wrapping the portion of the powerline the current of which is giving off magnetic fields to which the house is exposed in a cylinder of high-permeability material, such as Mu-metal, eliminate or significantly reduce the exposure of the house to the fields?
> (A) If not:
> (i) Is it because AC magnetic fields at the house would be too far from the cylinder to be drawn into/along the cylinder?
> (a) If so, does this imply that magnetic fields are unlike solids/liquids/gasses in that enclosing them in a barrier (even one that has the electromagnetic property of high permeability) does not have any impact on the extent to which such fields travel through/outside the barrier?
> (ii) Or, alternatively, is it because the magnetic fields generated by a powerline would simply be too strong to be significantly mitigated by such a cylinder and would thus saturate the cylinder, with the remaining large majority of the magnetic fields escaping outside the cylinder and reaching the house?
> (a) If so, does this imply that if the cylinder were thick enough, enclosing the powerline within it would indeed eliminate the exposure of the house to the magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current?
> (iii) Or is such a cylinder shield unlikely to be successful or certain to be unsuccessful in blocking the magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current for some other reason?
>
> **(2) Attic Horizontal Sheet**: If the right-hand rule for determining the direction of magnetic field lines indicates that the fields generated by the powerline's current are "entering" the house from above, would putting a horizontal sheet of high-permeability material in the attic directly above a particular room eliminate or significantly reduce the exposure of that room to the fields generated by the powerline's current?
> (A) If not:
> (i) Is it because I'm misunderstanding the implications of the right-hand rule, and magnetic fields (or maybe just AC magnetic fields) don't, in fact, travel in a particular locational direction, per se?
> (a) If so, does this imply that such an attempt at shielding would fail because the magnetic fields from the powerline current would still be entering the room not only through the walls, but also through the floor?
> (ii) Or, alternatively, is it because while the magnetic fields from the powerline would be cut off from entering the room by the shielding sheet above the room, the fields would likely simply just "bulge" back into the room from the sides (through the walls, rather than through the ceiling) after traveling along the path of the sheet?
> (a) If so, does this imply that if the sheet extended far enough beyond the perimeter of the room in all directions, this "bulging" effect would be insufficient to cause the fields to reach the room, and thus that such shielding could in theory be effective?
> (iii) Or, alternatively, is it because the magnetic fields generated by a powerline would be too strong to significantly mitigate (even at a distance of 20 feet) and would thus saturate the sheet, with the remaining large majority of the magnetic fields then passing through it into the room?
> (a) If so, does this imply that if the sheet were thick enough, placing it above the room would indeed eliminate the exposure of the room to the magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current?
> (iv) Or is such a horizontal sheet shield unlikely to be successful or certain to be unsuccessful in blocking the magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current for some other reason?
>
> **(3) Buried Vertical Sheet**: Would burying a vertical sheet of high-permeability material in the front yard of the house with the same width of a given room of the house, which is as tall as such room is deep, and which is buried deeply enough to intersect the magnetic field lines from the powerline that pass through such room, eliminate or significantly reduce the exposure of that room to the fields generated by the powerline's current?
> (A) If not:
> (i) Is it because the intersection of the magnetic field lines by the vertical buried sheet occurs after the magnetic field lines have already passed through the room (from above, through the ceiling), per the right hand rule?
> (a) If so, does this imply that such an attempt at shielding using a buried vertical sheet of high-permeability material could succeed with respect to shielding a home across the street from the home at issue from the fields generated by the current going through the powerline at issue, because such fields are entering that home across the street from below (through the floor), per the right-hand rule, and thus could be intercepted by a buried sheet before so entering?
> (ii) Or, alternatively, is it because while the magnetic fields from the powerline would be diverted by being pulled forward away from the room by the buried shielding sheet, the fields would likely simply just "bulge" back into a path that takes them through the room by the time they entered the room?
> (a) If so, does this imply that if the sheet were extended to be tall and wide enough in all directions beyond the field lines of the fields that would otherwise pass through the room, this "bulging" effect would be insufficient to cause the fields to reach the room, and thus that such shielding could in theory be effective?
> (iii) Or, alternatively, is it because the magnetic fields generated by a powerline would be too strong to significantly mitigate (even at significant distance from the powerline) and would thus saturate the sheet, with the remaining large majority of the magnetic fields that pass into the room remaining unaffected?
> (a) If so, does this imply that if the sheet were thick enough, placing it in the proper location would indeed eliminate the exposure of the room to the magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current?
> (iv) Or is such a buried vertical sheet shield unlikely to be successful or certain to be unsuccessful in redirecting the magnetic fields generated by the powerline's current away from the room at issue for some other reason?

If you do not mind me asking - what is your background?
So we understand this issue:
\- have you got a services diagram? In Australia we have a service called dial before you dig that provides utility service diagrams. **This is extremely important as this alone will give you valuable insight on the type of cable/s + trunking used.**
\- So the issue is from the underground main? I assume you only have a single phase (or what the US refers to as split phase) connection to your house?
\- have you got any easements on your property for the underground main?? Where is the actual main?
\- is there a transformer near by?
\- how old is this area?? e.g. contemporary primary supply lines (modern XLPE's multi conductors) vs older say ~ 30 years ago (singles with separate conduits) - more distances between conductors = more mag field, due to inadequate (weaker) cancellation.. That is they add more rather than cancel... Ties back to Ampre's law and superstition..

If you provide more info - I will be able to give you my two cents...

That actually might be an idea because I think the standard max distance for utility easements here is 10 feet from the road, and if they are within 10 feet it would have to be just barely, so maybe I could call them out on that (if that is indeed the rule) and get them to move the main back farther away. Even just 10 feet farther back would cause a significant reduction in magnetic fields in the house and both yards, plus while they were re-doing it maybe they'd be kind enough to put the conductors closer together for even greater reduction thru cancellation (though perhaps not if I was only able to get them to move it by threatening to sue if they didn't).

### Mat.I — 2025-07-08T04:44:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-355977>

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355971)
>
> NFA1000 does have single axis, though I've been using three-axis for these measurements & not sure how single axis would be helpful here

the 3axis will give you the vector sum e.g. total = square root of x2 + y2 + z2.. The single axis can assist with tracking the direction and source identification etc.. Also, it can provide indirect insight into net currents as this is typically indicative of quite directional fields etc.. Mag fields are rotational (concentric loops), however at locations the field has a clear spatial direction

Professional success has been engineered in these fields to hinge mostly on strict, prompt & eager obedience to increasingly transparently malicious sets of written and unwritten rules designed to concentrate power in the hands of the few at the expense of the many. ### Mat.I — 2025-07-08T05:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-355984>

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355975)
>
> I have an undergraduate physics degree but then became a lawyer, so some (rusty) theoretical familiarity with electromagnetism, but no applied technical experience.

Good to know as you seem well versed and quite detailed on the theory. You would certainly give most so called building biologist a run for their money with the theory side alone...

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355975)
>
> I don't have my hands on a diagram yet but will look into getting one. We have a service called 811 here that's meant to be dialed before digging and they usually come and spraypaint the lawn to mark the location of utility lines, but I don't know if they provide diagrams - will look into it. I was hoping they could at least also tell me how deep the lines are buried in addition to marking where they are, but sounds like knowledge of the precise type of equipment they have down there could be useful here, too?

This is important as just based on a desktop assessment you could determine the likely cause. As you have dated the development circa 85 it is highly likely the underground main would be run in singles within separate conduits with distances between the lines (e.g. one for each phase A, B, C + N). This arrangement would cause very high fields.. Vs say more contemporary approach with multi conductor cable within one conduit..

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355975)
>
> Yes, the fields are coming from the underground main. The main branches off to a pad-mounted transformer, from which a line then runs (also underground) to my house, but this shorter line between the transformer and my house isn't the one from which the fields are emanating, it's the main before the transformer.

Noted. Yes, the secondaries downstream from the pad mount would feed a few houses. This makes sense as this would likely be ran in triplex or the like..

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355975)
>
> The main runs underground across my front yard parallel to the front wall of my house at a distance of about 20 feet from the front door. I assume the utility has a valid easement to put it there, but actually haven't looked into it. That actually might be an idea because I think the standard max distance for utility easements here is 10 feet from the road, and if they are within 10 feet it would have to be just barely, so maybe I could call them out on that (if that is indeed the rule) and get them to move the main back farther away. Even just 10 feet farther back would cause a significant reduction in magnetic fields in the house and both yards, plus while they were re-doing it maybe they'd be kind enough to put the conductors closer together for even greater reduction thru cancellation (though perhaps not if I was only able to get them to move it by threatening to sue if they didn't).

You need your property survey documents and compare to the actual cable path if the easement is detailed.. You would need the services marked and an accurate survey to compare with. Re: main cable configuration & type - I would reach out to the utility company if the 811 service you referred to does not provide this as this information would be required by contractors if carrying out work within that area.. They would hold the "as-built" drawings.. If confirmed the service is in fact ok and not outside the easement boundary - check if there are any planned maintenance or upgrades to the primary feeders.. As these cables may be approach end of service life.. Failing that if you wanted to fund a short section of upgrade you would need to jump through lots of hoops and would come at a cost..

Received the following map from the utility (Duke Energy Florida). Looks like they actually have a 120V line coming from the pad-mounted transformer in parallel with the 7200V main both going through my front yard. That 120V line also then appears to join up with the 7200V main after passing in front of the house one farther down the line from mine.

Regarding line replacement, they said their policy is not to replace anything until it's broken. When I asked how much it would be if I waned to pay for it, they quoted me $100,000 - $300,000. I replied asking how they arrived at that estimate but thus far they've ignored me.
Wondering if in light of the arrangement depicted below it might make sense to offer to pay for a transformer in front of the neighbor's house in an effort to get them to remove (or at least put out of service) the 120V line running across my front yard. My thinking is that perhaps that 120V line (which is slightly closer to my house than the 7200V main) is only there for that one neighbor's house, and thus if they had a transformer of their own coming off the main in front of their house, too, the utility wouldn't need to run a significant portion of their service line across my yard, which is what I'm guessing is currently happening. But I'm not sure if the 120V line could be a significant contributor to my readings or if it's more likely to be the 7200V main that's causing problems - I would think it's likely the latter not only because of the higher voltage but also because I didn't notice any significant drop-off in AC magnetic fields past that neighbor's house on the NFA1000 when walking down the street.

### Saichi — 2025-08-05T18:24:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357631>

As Mat already noted, it's the three phases in the distribution line 120 deg apart and whatever physical distance separating the phases causing the field.

I just noticed SLT significantly lowered the price on their g iron flex at some point if I'm remembering correctly used to be at least double the price. Looks like around 3k would be enough to shield your house for the shielding material of course not including a casing for it to prevent corrosion assuming necessary and labor costs to bury it along the distribution line.

### Saichi — 2025-08-05T19:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357641>

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=355962)
>
> It seems to depend significantly on the season, the time of day, and the weather that particular day, presumably since that largely dictates neighborhood power consumption. Today (relatively mild summer day) around 5:45 p.m.:
> \- Directly over the powerline: ~7mG
> \- Front door of the house (~20 feet away from the powerline): ~1.5mG
> \- Back door of the house (~40 feet away from the powerline): ~1.0mG
> \- Back yard twenty feet from the back door (~60 feet away from the powerline): ~0.75mG

Looking at your levels again respect to distance from distribution line I'm almost certain you have other issues causing the majority of fields within your home. Have you used a clamp meter to measure the current on your water pipes, ground rod and cable line? What does the NFA1000 measure when close to either?

I recommend not touching any metal carrying current without insulators since it's ionizing in effect and much worse than the fields themselves.

### TB14 — 2025-08-07T06:11:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357721>

> [Saichi said:](/goto/post?id=357641)
>
> Looking at your levels again respect to distance from distribution line I'm almost certain you have other issues causing the majority of fields within your home. Have you used a clamp meter to measure the current on your water pipes, ground rod and cable line? What does the NFA1000 measure when close to either?
>
> I recommend not touching any metal carrying current without insulators since it's ionizing in effect and much worse than the fields themselves.

The drop-off is steady and proportional given distance from the line, though. Like if I walk from one side of my house to the other, parellel to the line, the levels are basically the same. But if I move farther away from the line, from the front toward the back, the fields drop off. And the same is true in the yards. There's no increase within the house relative to the outside, either. I.e., the levels on the front porch and five feet in front of the front porch are dramatically higher than anywhere I've measured within the house.

### TB14 — 2025-08-07T06:14:35+0300

Looks like around 3k would be enough to shield your house for the shielding material of course not including a casing for it to prevent corrosion assuming necessary and labor costs to bury it along the distribution line.

One thing that occurred to me recently is that I assume blocking magnetic fields by blocking at the house (i.e., by putting plates in the attic) would cut off the Earth's DC magnetic field as well, which I think would probably be bad. I'm leaning away from shielding at the house at the moment, if I were to pursue shielding I think I would try to do it at some point away from the house, but not sure if a cylinder around each of the conductors or something like that would work from a physical perspective - perhaps it would, just need to think about it a bit more.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-07T06:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357724>

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=357587)
>
> Received the following map from the utility (Duke Energy Florida). Looks like they actually have a 120V line coming from the pad-mounted transformer in parallel with the 7200V main both going through my front yard. That 120V line also then appears to join up with the 7200V main after passing in front of the house one farther down the line from mine.
>
> Regarding line replacement, they said their policy is not to replace anything until it's broken. When I asked how much it would be if I waned to pay for it, they quoted me $100,000 - $300,000. I replied asking how they arrived at that estimate but thus far they've ignored me.
> Wondering if in light of the arrangement depicted below it might make sense to offer to pay for a transformer in front of the neighbor's house in an effort to get them to remove (or at least put out of service) the 120V line running across my front yard. My thinking is that perhaps that 120V line (which is slightly closer to my house than the 7200V main) is only there for that one neighbor's house, and thus if they had a transformer of their own coming off the main in front of their house, too, the utility wouldn't need to run a significant portion of their service line across my yard, which is what I'm guessing is currently happening. But I'm not sure if the 120V line could be a significant contributor to my readings or if it's more likely to be the 7200V main that's causing problems - I would think it's likely the latter not only because of the higher voltage but also because I didn't notice any significant drop-off in AC magnetic fields past that neighbor's house on the NFA1000 when walking down the street.

Is there a cable legend available that details the cable types?

### Saichi — 2025-08-07T08:24:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357727>

> [TB14 said:](/goto/post?id=357721)
>
> The drop-off is steady and proportional given distance from the line, though. Like if I walk from one side of my house to the other, parellel to the line, the levels are basically the same. But if I move farther away from the line, from the front toward the back, the fields drop off. And the same is true in the yards. There's no increase within the house relative to the outside, either. I.e., the levels on the front porch and five feet in front of the front porch are dramatically higher than anywhere I've measured within the house.

You measured a 79% drop in levels from power line to front door 20 feet and then only a 33% drop another 20 feet which doesn't conform to inverse square law. These were the measurements you took with the main service breaker off correct, just your DC home that doesn't have one right? The photo of the page I posted above clearly states can have just a 0.1 mG drop in field from front to back porch but even in that situation the source can't be purely an external power line but parallel neutral or neutral ground faults occurring whether within or without the home's wiring's obviously the question.

I just noticed you mentioned before the house is made of concrete which conducts some current moreso in heat/humidity of Florida. Even if your home were wired correctly, I have no idea if the concrete's conductivity would make decoupling from objectionable current coming from outside the house's wiring a possibility and whether it's enough to create the mag levels you currently have.

I have only experienced a slow drop in levels from parallel neutrals within my home. This has me curious enough I'll attempt to take readings with a ladder, tape measure, and Alpha Labs meter from the overhead power line outside tomorrow with respect to distance.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-07T09:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357730>

> [Saichi said:](/goto/post?id=357727)
>
> You measured a 79% drop in levels from power line to front door 20 feet and then only a 33% drop another 20 feet which doesn't conform to inverse square law.

Maybe for point sources/smaller sections yes, however, possible separated HV singles (long line sources) buried would decay differently. The day to day variability is likely due to the neighbourhood load.

### TB14 — 2025-08-07T09:37:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357731>

Will ask the utility for the cable legend. Another complicating factor might be the proximity of the secondary to one or more of the primary conductors. But yes, the reduction in the strength of the fields absolutely does significantly decelerate with distance from the lines. But that is true outside as well as in. For example if the field is 7 mG directly over the powerlines at a given time, then probably two feet away from them, it's already down to ~4 mG. Another ten feet and it's only down to like ~2 mG. Towards the back of the back yard, I can walk like 100 feet in the direction away from the powerline and the levels will only go from ~0.5 mG to ~0.4 mG. There is a fairly large lake at the edge of the property's back yard, and given (a) the steepness of the drop-off in the rate of decline and (b) the magnitude of the fields even at the edge of the lake on my property, I've found myself wondering if the impact zone where the fields would be measurable from the lines in my front yard actually stretches all the way to the other side of the lake (probably a quarter mile from the source)

### Saichi — 2025-08-07T17:23:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357752>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=357730)
>
> Maybe for point sources/smaller sections yes, however, possible separated HV singles (long line sources) buried would decay differently. The day to day variability is likely due to the neighbourhood load.

I just did some quick measurements outside without a ladder, my neighbor's property is where the overhead HV lines end and I measured a 0.1 drop (0.7 to 0.6) bringing my meter down just little over two feet from head to stomach level underneath where the transformer is.

I took same head to stomach measurements under only the 240V line next to my house and it was 0.6 to 0.5 which isn't significantly different than the HV lines. The HV lines are a few feet higher than the residential line of course.

### S Hudson — 2025-08-07T21:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-357801>

The number of new builds in the housing developments near me that are being placed under existing power lines is staggering.

### TB14 — 2025-12-04T19:49:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physics-questions-shielding-magnetic-fields-from-powerlines.31260/post-363590>

Update on this, and seemingly good news of some sort - the AC magnetic fields in my home in Ocala have decreased many-fold since I measured this summer. I believe this is most likely either because it's now winter and people are using A/C less, meaning this would be a much better situation in the winter here than in the summer, or, even more optimistically, because the power company did something in response to my request/complaint. As I sit here on my couch mid-day, AC magnetic fields read 0.03 mG on the NFA1000 - the same meter in the same spot at the same time of day almost always read over 1.00 mG six months ago, during summer.

In terms of my interactions with the power company - after I got in touch with them, they sent a guy out to the house with an AC gaussmeter. Nice guy, though clearly working for the utility - he turned my microwave on and held the gaussmeter like a foot away from it to get a "comparison" value to "compare" against the ambient fields pervading the entire home from the buried powerline(s) in the front yard. He hypothesized that the problem was related to some sort of back-up lower-voltage line (not the service drop to my house, but a reserve line of some sort, present in case the main one failed) that he thought might be somehow interacting with the higher-voltage primary lines. A few months elapsed with no activity from the power company after this visit, so I reached out to them again, and some time thereafter, I heard from someone who was in the house that utility employees arrived in two trucks and spent some time around the pad-mounted transformer. I came down to the house for the first time since August earlier this week, and every time I've measured, it's been dramatically lower. So either the power company did something, which I lean towards being the most likely scenario given the apparent activity by the power company after my complaint at what I assume would be the right spot to bring about such changes, or it's just ~20- - 35- fold less intense during winter due to lack of A/C use, which is also plausible to me given the intensity of Florida summer heat and people keeping their HVACs entirely off here during winter, which I would expect to create a big disparity, although maybe not 30+-fold (my power bill certainly doesn't drop 30-fold in the winter...).

# Thread: Hi. New to Gold. I have questions about access to Vermont 2018, Bootcamps.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-new-to-gold-i-have-questions-about-access-to-vermont-2018-bootcamps.31262/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DonnaMarie444 — 2025-07-08T02:33:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-new-to-gold-i-have-questions-about-access-to-vermont-2018-bootcamps.31262/post-355967>

Hi, I’m DonnaMarie from Western MA. So after spending a couple months at Bronze level on Patreon, then a month at Silver, I took the leap to Gold in May. What a great move, I’m loving the Webinars/PowWows/Blogs. They’ve been such a great help. After doing a lot of searching and navigating, I still have a few technical questions.

1-My Optimal Klub says Gold, but I noticed that I'm showing on the forum as a Silver member. Am I missing out on something or does that not really matter?

2-On my Optimal Dashboard there is a Bootcamp section, but it says I'm not allowed access to it. Are Bootcamps available for purchase only, and if so, is there a list to look at?

3-I did a search for the Vermont 2018 talk and found a link Dr. Kruse had posted in February 2025. The link took me to Patreon where it said I needed to be a paid member to view the video. I had cancelled my Patreon Bronze when I became a Gold member. Is there another way to see it, and all the other links that also send me to Patreon, or do I still need to pay for Patreon?

Thanks for any help. DonnaMarie

### MTA321 — 2025-07-08T16:13:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-new-to-gold-i-have-questions-about-access-to-vermont-2018-bootcamps.31262/post-356004>

> [DonnaMarie444 said:](/goto/post?id=355967)
>
> Hi, I’m DonnaMarie from Western MA. So after spending a couple months at Bronze level on Patreon, then a month at Silver, I took the leap to Gold in May. What a great move, I’m loving the Webinars/PowWows/Blogs. They’ve been such a great help. After doing a lot of searching and navigating, I still have a few technical questions.
>
> 1-My Optimal Klub says Gold, but I noticed that I'm showing on the forum as a Silver member. Am I missing out on something or does that not really matter?
>
> 2-On my Optimal Dashboard there is a Bootcamp section, but it says I'm not allowed access to it. Are Bootcamps available for purchase only, and if so, is there a list to look at?
>
> 3-I did a search for the Vermont 2018 talk and found a link Dr. Kruse had posted in February 2025. The link took me to Patreon where it said I needed to be a paid member to view the video. I had cancelled my Patreon Bronze when I became a Gold member. Is there another way to see it, and all the other links that also send me to Patreon, or do I still need to pay for Patreon?
>
> Thanks for any help. DonnaMarie

1\. If you have access to AskJack then there isn't much difference. Also, if you have Facebook, there could be an additional group for Gold you can have access to. Start a conversation with support here on the forum and they should get to you eventually.

2.Bootcamps are purchased separate from gold as far as I am aware.

3\. See No. 2 - for Vermont 2018. My understanding is Jack paid to have this session filmed/edited so that is why there is a cost. Gold members get a steep discount if you search for the title on the optimal klub portal; there you will see an option to purchase. I've watched it and it is good content but nothing you can't learn from the blogs and podcasts since he recorded it back in 2018.

### JenniferM — 2025-07-08T18:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-new-to-gold-i-have-questions-about-access-to-vermont-2018-bootcamps.31262/post-356009>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-new-to-gold-i-have-questions-about-access-to-vermont-2018-bootcamps.31262/post-356698>

After you go to your dashboard, click on “blogs”, then search for Vermont 2018. The 1st option is $100. If you scroll down there is an option for gold and platinum members for $20.

Hope that helps. I just listed to it and found it helpful even though I’ve been a member reading blogs for 5 years.

### Szen — 2025-07-21T03:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-new-to-gold-i-have-questions-about-access-to-vermont-2018-bootcamps.31262/post-356701>

# Thread: Ryan's path
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ryan M — 2025-07-08T05:13:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-355978>

I started reading blogs by the doctor (Dr. Kruse) in 2014, after many of my fellow Functional Patterns practitioners were recommending his work. I implemented it here and there over the next couple years, never very seriously. I used the airplane protocol, I bought a cheap pair of red glasses that I wore at night doing shift work, I would take occasional cold baths, that was about it.

In 2019, I changed jobs so I would no longer have shift work, but was now in an office most of the time. About one year after starting this job, I started feeling terrible intermittently. I would feel good for about one week and then crappy for about one week. I could barely find a correlation. At the time, I was following the advice of Ben Greenfield and other influencers. I was eating one meal a day, and often exercising once or twice daily for several years. (I would love to know specially what I destroyed doing this, because as you will read, I suffered greatly )

I tried everything Ben sells and suggested and got worse and worse.

In 2020, my Dad passed unexpectedly.

In October 2020, I started experiencing bouts of anxiety. I went back to 3 meals per day at this time, and still did every other day fasts of 16-20 hours often. By December 2020, this had morphed into a frequent throat tightness and chest issues by February 2021. These included heart palpitations, chest pain in different areas, arm numbness, headaches at times, anxiety continued...

I went to a Yale Hospital Cardiologist who performed all the tests: multiple Ekgs, 24 hour holter monitor, multiple stress ekgs, multiple echos, stress echo, etc. I insisted on a calcium scan and it was 0. The cardiologist was eventually frustrated that he could not prescribe a beta blocker nor BP lowering medication and literally left the room in huff saying he has no idea what was wrong.

Exercise never a problem, the pain occurred at rest.

History: Right bundle branch block, only observed on EKG (since 16), excellent exercise capacity on stress tests, however the first echo, in early 2021 showed 50% EF. This had me worried for a long time.

A sought a naturopathic cardiologist (Decker Weiss).via telehealth who started me on taurine, Restorative functions cardiac calm (blend if herbs) and Magnesium. He said he may have to start on these forever. They seemed to help.

Second echo was up to 63% EF.

My primary naturopath since 2017 was getting more and more into the teachings of Jack Kruse at this time. But, based on my symptoms, suggested I test for Lyme. That came back positive.
I tried several different things with my naturopath including methylene Blue, B1, and others. My symptoms persisted.

Blood work consistently showed low white blood cell count since about the time of symptoms, along with low ceruloplasmin, copper and zinc all while eating lots of organ meats. One blood test in 2017 showed normal range wbc count, which was my only baseline.

04/2022
Vibrant labs Lyme and co-infections. A couple positives.

Had a health coach appointment with Dr. Wolfson's clinic in Arizona which came with the lab test. They suggested low does 150mcg, 3 times daily of Lizyme (lithium) for heart issues. Did not help.

I would still go for 5-6 days feeling great followed by a couple days of feeling very low energy, brain fog, achy, etc.

I ended up seeing a Lyme literate doctor who put me on herbs, at $800 per month. I did that for about 8 months until I was starting to feel better.

During the same time period, I was listening to my naturopath who was encouraging more of the light controls, grounding and as much sun as possible here in Connecticut. I'm not sure if it was the herbs or the protocols that were helping me improve, but I suspect the latter.

These are not nearly all the doctors I saw or the protocols I tried that failed. I wasted a shit load of money and struggled for years with my family and two young kids. It's emotional to think about, but I'm pretty good now.

i cold plunge almost daily year round. I have decreased exercise to 3-4/times per week in the summer and 1-2 times per week in the winter. I only exercise in my garage or outside. Red lights only after dark. My whole family is on board. My kids do minimal screens only with red lights on and blue blockers. We all get as dark as we can in the summer (not my wife).

I have not really been able to fast anymore, I feel like I broke something doing it excessively. My neuropath explained how the melanin in my heart was likely degraded so I've learned to balance exercise. I do my best to eat seasonally (my having isn't really with that). I'm extreme with these teachings. I know I need more sun because I know how I feel when I get it. I know winters will always be a struggle for me up here.

I went to ES in March and am buying a condo there for the future. My wife is not currently on board, but I'll try to rent it until she is, or until I need it.

I'm Irish/Italian and my wife is light skin, blue eyes, blonde hair, Scandinavian. She is not a fan of the sun.

I would love someone to shed light on what they believe I damaged with my excessive exercise and fasting. My only symptom that continues is still occasional chest pain which I can not determine if it is heart burn or not (still). Zinc carnosine taken with meals seems to help it. I'll try to update with anything I missed...

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-08T05:26:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-355980>

apoptosis of the the electrical system in the heart = heteroplasmy and you tolerated it via compensation but it took your reserve away.

Bigger issue is what you said about uncoupled wife.

That will sink you.

### Ryan M — 2025-07-08T21:13:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-356024>

A couple things I missed: I herniated S1/L5 in June 2023. No trauma-related injury, just came on gradually after exercise and playing basketball. I understand that is a melanin issue also.

My wife took one Moderna. My kids and I took none. The kids are otherwise fully vaxxed. My wife is none months pregnant with our third and I've decided no vaccines is must likely and I'll figure out schooling later.

What you said sunk in years ago. I'm hoping she can change before I have to without her.

My wife tolerates the sun and is tanner than she has ever been in her shoulders and chest (while wearing makeup and sunglasses in the sun, which is out of my control right now). Vanity still controls her. Most of her is not tan.

My son's are savages. I need to make sure my unborn daughter is.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T01:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-356034>

Wife took Moderna then so did the whole family.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T01:02:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-356035>

High chance the new baby is GMO.

### Ryan M — 2025-09-11T20:40:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-359382>

My 9 year old son broke his clavicle at jiu jitsu last night. We went to Yale New Haven where they did several X rays.

They referred us to a pediatric orthopedic doctor for follow up X rays to make sure it's healing properly. Perhaps I'm too cyclical, but are these additional X-rays necessary? If it's healing slightly off, I would hard pressed to go the route of surgery, so what's the point?

### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-22T17:13:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-361408>

Taurine has a very dark side that food gurus never speak on. Yes, it promotes bile flow and digestion… But it also binds to bile and forms taurocholate; a bile acid that feeds toxic bacteria. → More taurocholate = more H₂S (mitochondrial inhibitor) → More endotoxin → More Candida overgrowth and more SIBO risks If taurine makes you feel worse… this is why.

It’s not that taurine is bad, but in the wrong gut environment due to poor light choices on your skin, it can backfire hard. Fix your skin clocks to fix you gut problems before you flood it with bile.

### Ryan M — 2026-01-21T17:21:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-365689>

I had this additional blood work done in June 2022 showing low serum CoQ10. I've never supplemented with it besides on airplanes based on the airplane protocol.

### Mat.I — 2026-01-21T23:59:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-365702>

> [Ryan M said:](/goto/post?id=365689)
>
> I had this additional blood work done in June 2022 showing low serum CoQ10. I've never supplemented with it besides on airplanes based on the airplane protocol.

Mito are under stress likely due to light mismatches and nnemf.

### Ryan M — 2026-02-09T13:56:32+0200

Does anyone know if Dr. Kruse is still doing in-person consults in ES?

# Thread: Ocean Water and Sunlight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Alain Dwight — 2025-07-08T05:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/post-355986>

Sunlight is good and being in the ocean is good, but is there a trade off? Does being in the Ocean water interfere with the body's ability to pick up the proper signals from the sun?

### Mat.I — 2025-07-08T05:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/post-355988>

> [Alain Dwight said:](/goto/post?id=355986)
>
> Sunlight is good and being in the ocean is good, but is there a trade off? Does being in the Ocean water interfere with the body's ability to pick up the proper signals from the sun?

I would have thought diving would, however normally swimming or being in the shallows would not? Depends on n=1 context I think. As if someone wanted to maximise UV exposure (large area) & POMC etc you would ensure you are balancing in water vs out I guess or staying in the shallows.

### AlbertD — 2025-07-08T05:58:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/post-355989>

The eccrine glands in our feet (sweat glands) unique to humans are designed to absorb electron-rich water from the sea and excrete sodium. This influx of electrons can help hydrate the discs of our spine, which has no blood supply, yet must remain hydrated to function properly.

Our feet are literally a built-in reverse osmosis system.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-07-08T07:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/post-355991>

> [Alain Dwight said:](/goto/post?id=355986)
>
> Sunlight is good and being in the ocean is good, but is there a trade off? Does being in the Ocean water interfere with the body's ability to pick up the proper signals from the sun?

Interesting thoughts.
We humans can be in water for a length of time (swimming) but under the water (deep) for a limited amount of time.
Me thinks the energetic properties of the water (grounding), without ANY nnEMF would be a net benefit.
What are the penetration levels of NIR & UV in shallow water?

### Mat.I — 2025-07-08T07:58:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/post-355992>

UV and IR (water is a heat sink) would take the hardest hit.. The optical window of water is highly in the vis spectrum and sits ~ 450nm to 550nm hence the colour of the ocean and certain marine life adapting to it (eyes are tuned to it). You can calculate this with online calculators or do the math manually via the beer-lambert law.

### Saichi — 2025-07-08T17:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocean-water-and-sunlight.31265/post-356007>

Sunbathing and ocean bathing should both be done in safe spaces.

# Thread: Chicken's with Marek's disease --- what to do?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chickens-with-mareks-disease-what-to-do.31266/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### eve001 — 2025-07-08T13:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chickens-with-mareks-disease-what-to-do.31266/post-355994>

Last week I noticed my mum had a chick with trouble walking. Not yet mature. Then another one from another race -- a bigger one -- also had a wing hanging. One of this bread I saw with a mouse in her beak and her sisters running after her. These are still quite young also. 3 months or so.

Either the mouse was poisoned and both ate from it, or they have marek's?

Unfortunately they put the chicks together away in a seperate compartment WITHOUT propper daylight.
We think it might be marek's but are not sure.

I now opened the door so they habve daylight and put a red heat lamp there too.

However if it is marek best is maybe to kill them to protect the other chickens?

One of them was bought elsewhere the little one is own breed. own breed = def no vaccines etc.

# Thread: Seeking advice for toddler enamel hypoplasia (brown spots)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/>

### jespada — 2025-07-08T21:07:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356022>

My son is 1.6 years old and has been diagnosed with **enamel hypoplasia** by his dentist (he has brown spots on his two upper front teeth). He is a normal toddler, under no medication, and we don’t use fluoride (at least not knowingly).

The dentist has advised **topical application of fluoride toothpaste** on those teeth.

However, in the June 2025 Q&A, Dr. Jack Kruse mentioned:

-   **Enamel production:** Enamel is made by **ameloblasts**, which die once the tooth erupts, so enamel cannot regenerate.
-   The structural integrity of enamel depends on its **electromagnetic bonding to dentin**, maintained by nerves and blood supply.
-   **Enamel absorbs deep UV light**, and UV light used by dentists (e.g. to harden bonding materials) is a useful “biohack” to strengthen teeth.
-   **100 nm UV light** (deep UV) is considered beneficial for enamel.

We already follow many of Jack’s recommendations as a family (sunrise exposure, grounding, low EMF environment, dark nights, etc.).

### **My questions:**​

1.  Has anyone here dealt with enamel hypoplasia in toddlers naturally?
2.  Would **UV light exposure** (e.g. dentist curing light) be beneficial in this context?
3.  Are there **non-fluoride approaches** to improve enamel integrity in hypoplasia cases?
4.  Any additional advice to support **oral development and remineralization** at this stage?

Thank you so much for your insights and experiences

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356027>

It consists of collagen fibrils, proteoglycans, and mesenchymal cells, includingdental pulp stem cells (hDPSCs), which support the formation and maintenance of enamel, dentin, and pulp.

Transgenerational weaknesses in the tooth bud’s stroma, potentially inherited through epigenetic orenvironmental influences, can predispose teeth to structural vulnerabilities, leading to enamel pits and fissures. Below, I’ll clarify how stromal weaknesses contribute to photobioelectric failure, integrating a decentralized framework focusing on human tooth development.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:38:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356028>

Central Node: Tooth (Enamel, Dentin, Pulp)
Subcomponents: Odontoblasts, Dentinal Tubules, Dental Pulp Stem Cells (hDPSCs)
Left Branch of slide: Protective Mechanisms

Parotid/Submandibular Glands:
Outputs: Saliva (minerals, buffering), Parotid Hormone (Leonora’s flow)

Mitochondrial ATP drives secretion.

**Stromal Role in Tooth Development**

During odontogenesis, the tooth bud forms from interactions between ectodermal epithelial cells and mesenchymal stromal cells derived from the neural crest. The stroma provides a scaffold for odontoblasts, which secrete dentin, and supports ameloblasts, which form enamel. The dental papilla, a stromal component, gives rise to the pulp and contains hDPSCs, which maintain a tooth's regenerative potential. The stromal layer’s integrity, stabilized by collagen and proteoglycans, is critical for:

1.  Mechanical Support: The stroma anchors odontoblasts and maintains dentinal tubule structure, facilitating dentinal fluid flow, which delivers calcium, phosphate, and protective substances to enamel.
2.  Bioelectric Signaling: The stroma, connected to the CNS via the trigeminal nerve, supports bioelectric potentials (Becker’s current) that regulate cellular activity and mineralization.
3.  Nutrient Supply: Stromal vascularization ensures nutrient delivery to developing and mature teeth, supporting mitochondrial ATP production in odontoblasts and salivary glands.

Transgenerational weaknesses, from the Mother or father germ line or potentially from maternal nutrition, stress, or environmental exposures (e.g., nnEMF, light stress, high-carb diets), may impair stromal collagen density, proteoglycan stability, or hDPSC functionality. This mirrors stromal thinning in keratoconus, where weakened collagen matrices lead to corneal fragility.

In teeth, a compromised stroma results in brittle enamel prone to pits and fissures, as the biomechanical foundation fails to support ameloblast function or resist trauma.

Photobioelectric Failure in the Stroma

Photobioelectric failure refers to disruptions in the bioelectric and biochemical processes driven by light and electromagnetic influences, exacerbated by stromal weaknesses. Here’s how it unfolds:

1.  Protective Mechanisms Disrupted:
2.  Dentinal Flow Impairment: Dentinal fluid, driven by bioelectric potentials and mitochondrial ATP, transports minerals through dentinal tubules to remineralize enamel. A weakened stroma, due to fragmented collagen or reduced proteoglycan content, disrupts tubule architecture, stagnating flow and starving enamel of calcium and phosphate.
3.  Salivary Gland Dysfunction: The parotid and submandibular glands, regulated by mitochondrial ATP and parotid hormone (Leonora’s flow), secrete saliva rich in minerals and buffering agents. Stromal weaknesses may impair neural signaling via the trigeminal system, reducing gland output. High-carb diets suppress parotid hormone through the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, further diminishing salivary flow.

Dentinal Flow (Leonora):

-   Fluid movement through tubules
-   Delivers calcium, phosphate, protective substances
-   Bioelectric potential (Becker’s current)
-   Red Light (PBM, 630–850 nm):
-   Enhances mitochondrial ATP, hDPSC differentiation
-   Increases salivary flow, dentinal flow
-   Supports pulp regeneration

-   Right Branch: Disruptive Factors
-   High-Carb Diet:
-   Suppresses parotid hormone via hypothalamic-pituitary axis
-   Induces mitochondrial stress, inflammation
    -   Blue Light (380–550 nm):
        -   Increases ROS, lowers melatonin/DHA/melanopsin disrupts mitochondrial function
        -   Impairs salivary and dentinal flow
        -   Reduces dopamine, melatonin, photoreceptor repair and circadian signaling
    -   nnEMF (1G–5G):
        -   Disrupts mitochondrial electron transport, water structure, increases glucose and insulin
        -   Impairs bioelectric potentials, creating dentinal flow
-   Bottom Node: Outcomes
    -   Healthy Tooth: Maintained enamel/dentin, vital pulp, regenerated dentin (via hDPSCs)
    -   Decay/Abscess: Demineralization, stagnant flow, pulp necrosis
-   Arrows:
    -   Red light → Glands, Odontoblasts, hDPSCs (enhances function)
    -   Blue light/nnEMF → Glands, Odontoblasts (impairs function)
    -   High-carb diet → Parotid suppression → Reduced flow
    -   Dentinal flow <--> Becker’s current (bioelectric synergy)

The sun is how you improve it because the stem cells in the kid are fired do the parents habits and choices that influenced the stem cells that the child came from.

Healthy Tooth: A robust stroma supports enamel and dentin integrity, vital pulp, and hDPSC-driven regeneration, maintaining resistance to trauma.

Decay and Pits/perio disease: A weakened stroma leads to stagnant dentinal flow, demineralization, and enamel pitting, increasing susceptibility to biomechanical failure and caries. Severe cases result in pulp necrosis or abscess or destruction of Sharpey's fibers.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:57:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356031>

1.6 years old with this problem = parents redox is bad.

Tooth Bud Development Timeline

Tooth bud development in humans begins during the 6th to 8th week of embryogenesis (late first trimester). Specifically:

6th week: The oral epithelium thickens to form the dental lamina, an ectodermal band along the future dental arches.

7th–8th week: The dental lamina invaginates into the underlying mesenchyme, forming tooth buds for the primary (deciduous) teeth. These buds mark the initiation of odontogenesis, progressing through the bud, cap, and bell stages.

Embryological Origin of Tooth Bud Stroma

The stroma of the tooth bud, which forms the dental papilla and eventually the dental pulp and supportive connective tissue, is derived from cranial neural crest cells (ectomesenchyme). Here’s the breakdown:

Neural Crest Origin: The stroma arises from ectomesenchymal cells, a subset of neural crest cells that migrate from the neural folds to the first and second branchial arches during the 3rd–4th week of embryogenesis. These cells are unique in combining ectodermal (neural crest) and mesenchymal properties.

Composition: The tooth bud stroma consists of:
Mesenchymal cells: These differentiate into odontoblasts (which form dentin), dental pulp stem cells (hDPSCs), and other pulp cells.
Collagen fibrils: Primarily type I and III collagen, providing structural support.
Proteoglycans and extracellular matrix (ECM): These stabilize the stromal architecture and regulate cell signaling.
Interaction with Epithelium: The stroma (ectomesenchyme) interacts with the ectodermal enamel organ, driving reciprocal signaling that shapes tooth morphogenesis. The dental papilla, a stromal condensation, forms the pulp and dentin, while the dental follicle (another stromal derivative) gives rise to the periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone.

The Time line of the kids diagnosis = really a embryological parent issue.

Innervation of the First and Second Branchial Arches During Embryogenesis

The first and second branchial arches (also called pharyngeal arches) develop during the 3rd to 4th week of embryogenesis and are innervated by specific cranial nerves. The innervation is established as the arches form, driven by the migration of neural crest cells and the development of cranial nerve ganglia. Neural crest cells are like the Mother board of redox for these branchial cleft so pitting enamel makes me worry about many other things derived in these two clefts.

First Branchial Arch (Mandibular Arch)

Innervation: Trigeminal nerve (Cranial Nerve V), specifically the mandibular branch (V3).

The trigeminal nerve provides sensory innervation to the derivatives of the first arch, including the mandible, lower jaw muscles, and oral mucosa, as well as motor innervation to the muscles of mastication (e.g., masseter, temporalis, pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior digastric, tensor tympani, and tensor veli palatini.

Embryological Origin: The trigeminal nerve arises from the trigeminal ganglion, which forms from neural crest cells and neural tube-derived neurons in the hindbrain (rhombomeres 2 and 3).

Timing of Innervation: how soon the germ line redox problem caused this effect.

3rd week: Neural crest cells begin migrating into the first branchial arch, and the trigeminal ganglion starts forming.
4th week: The trigeminal nerve extends axons into the first arch, establishing sensory and motor innervation as the arch differentiates into mesenchymal stroma and musculature. By the end of the 4th week, the mandibular branch (V3) is actively innervating the arch, coinciding with the formation of the mandibular prominence and Meckel’s cartilage.

Second Branchial Arch (Hyoid Arch)

Innervation: Facial nerve (Cranial Nerve VII).

The facial nerve provides sensory innervation to the taste buds of the anterior tongue (via the chorda tympani, a branch of CN VII) and motor innervation to the muscles of facial expression (e.g., orbicularis oris, buccinator), stapedius, stylohyoid, and posterior digastric.

Embryological Origin: The facial nerve originates from the facial ganglion (geniculate ganglion) and motor neurons in the hindbrain (rhombomere 4).

Neural crest cells also contribute to the sensory ganglia in the head and neck.

Timing of Innervation:

3rd week: Neural crest cells populate the second arch, and the facial nerve begins to form as its ganglion develops.

4th week: The facial nerve extends axons into the second arch, establishing innervation as the arch forms the hyoid bone and associated musculature. By the end of the 4th week, CN VII innervation is established, supporting the differentiation of second arch structures like the hyoid apparatus and facial muscles.

Tooth Bud Context

The first and second branchial arches contribute to tooth development, with the first arch forming the mandible, maxilla, and tooth bud stroma (ectomesenchyme), and the second arch contributing to supporting structures like the hyoid bone which the tongue connects to. The trigeminal nerve (V3) is particularly critical for the sensory innervation of the tooth buds, as it connects the developing dental pulp and stroma to the central nervous system (CNS) via the trigeminal ganglion. This innervation begins in the 4th week as the dental lamina starts to form (around 6 weeks), with nerve fibers extending into the tooth buds during the bud stage (7th–8th weeks) to support sensory and photo- bioelectric signaling.

# Thread: Fixing myself to understand how to ultimately breed better racehorses
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fixing-myself-to-understand-how-to-ultimately-breed-better-racehorses.31276/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KStuebs — 2025-07-10T21:47:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fixing-myself-to-understand-how-to-ultimately-breed-better-racehorses.31276/post-356121>

I have Graves' Disease. I was diagnosed in 2007. It has been controlled with trace mineral balance and diet. The information I'm finding here is finally answering a lot of question marks in my mind that apply not just to me, but to my immediate phenotype family members.

I also am a thoroughbred bloodstock consultant/agent. Figuring things out is a passion of mine. I've even researched and written a book as a ghost author about a sensitive topic that finally answered a ton of questions in a highly charged area of political America. Figuring out the Rubik's cube was something I completed at age 12. Figuring out the thoroughbred genetic puzzle to breed top racehorses has been my passion since age 18. I have discovered undeniable patterns in breeding that have allowed my to apply that knowledge for clients to help them breed winners of two Breeders' Cups, multiple Grade 1 races, and many runners with seven-figure earnings.

The new passion is going to be to apply the quantum bio-physics from Dr. Kruse to this art. Horse racing is simply about which horse is the fastest, strongest, healthiest. Thinking about all Dr. Kruse discusses, clearly mitochondrial physics is at play. The things I'm learning have yielded questions that could help learn and direct better breeding and management of racehorses.

For example, heteroplasmy vs. homoplasmy is likely of serious consequence in choosing sires for dams in racing. Clearly there are families that consistently produce top runners (one mare recently producing three Derby horses, and one winner). So many questions come to mind like, does the mitochondria of an embryo become hybridized, damaged, or repaired during gestation. I would assume it does, but looking at the consistencies of some mares over others, what could be happening and when? Also, some stallions make amazing sires, yet offer nothing to the mitochondrial equation, (unless they actually offer some rare influence). Some sires make great broodmare sires, but poor quality sires of runners suggesting that they have strong influence passing on their X chromosome. The patterns of lineage in great horses are consistent, but unexplained. Can these sorts of patterns be an indication of some sort of mitochondrial homoplasmy returning to an offspring, or simple DNA physical genetics? Of course the light equation is something that is a variable more easily controlled that the genetic roll of the dice in breeding two thoroughbreds for a desired result. For example evidence suggests that proper light exposure may be an antidote for respiratory bleeders, with the European yards having far more light in the stalls and yards, versus their American counterparts. Then Dr. K's information discussed that dopamine determines muscle quality, and this has to be yet another serious consideration to the breeding and husbandry choice.

Besides fixing my own health, thoroughbred mating is my passion. I will hopefully develop a proprietary software to collect and apply new knowledge and science. It's just going to take a lot of time and research. BUT, I'm always up for a big challenge!

# Thread: HOW I HEALED KERATOCONUS IN TWO SAVAGES
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-healed-keratoconus-in-two-savages.31277/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-10T22:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-healed-keratoconus-in-two-savages.31277/post-356127>

A new blog post is available today on keratoconus. I promise that what you'll read here is not available anywhere else on this disease, which ophthalmologists still struggle to understand and solve. I used light to do it.

In this blog, you'll see how I cured two of my patients of this disease.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-61-124956764](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-61-124956764)

How did I come up with new ideas to solve Keratoconus? It was through my studies on narcolepsy in 2005. The discovery that an orexin receptor mutation causes the sleep disorder canine narcolepsy in Doberman Pinschers indicated a significant role for this system in sleep regulation. Narcolepsy is a REM sleep disorder. Additionally, genetic knockout mice lacking the orexin gene were also reported to exhibit narcolepsy.

In humans, narcolepsy is associated with a specific variant of the human leukocyte antigen (HLA) complex.

A lack of either red light or UV light is believed to be a contributing factor to the onset of narcolepsy. Does the same principle apply to eye diseases and collagen vascular diseases?

Today, all of them begin with poor light signaling in the prism of the retina. Light changes the charge density of the retina. The proof is found in genome-wide analysis studies that show that, in addition to the HLA variant, people with narcolepsy also exhibit a specific genetic mutation in the T-cell receptor alpha locus. I spoke about the HLA complex in the brain-gut series when I explained how the chimp brain evolved into the homo sapien brain using the HLA complex. Orexins helped fuel the transition. They are active in this eye disease, too.

### JenniferM — 2025-07-10T23:00:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-healed-keratoconus-in-two-savages.31277/post-356131>

My brother dated a lady with narcolepsy. He never mentioned her disability so when I met her and she just fell out asleep I thought she was on drugs. Her car looks like a junkyard car and she keeps on driving. So dangerous.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-10T23:27:09+0300

### Kendall Toerner — 2025-07-11T12:47:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spreading-ev-danger.31281/post-356156>

I published a short book telling my story about health issues accrued from my Tesla (and EMF in general), with the intention of spreading physical copies throughout the world

[Amazon.com](https://a.co/d/flpmhcs)

I made it 80 pages so it is cheap to print ($3 each for author copies)

I want to find people interested in spreading copies around their area, or during their travels, to help prevent EVs from becoming ubiquitous (and as a result making it impossible to rent a gas car, or get a non-EV Uber, or eventually even find gas for a reasonable cost)

You can DM me your address and I will share the total cost $3/copy + small shipping cost)

I'm starting with 300 copies myself and spreading them around my favorite places - Iceland and Costa Rica. In particular I need people in Scandanavia since they are in the most imminant danger from EV effects and adoption rate

### DLO — 2025-07-11T17:33:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spreading-ev-danger.31281/post-356169>

Whats the book called

### AlbertW — 2025-07-11T17:55:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spreading-ev-danger.31281/post-356170>

### The Health Dangers of Electric Vehicles: A Health Journey From An Ex-Tesla Owner​

### AlbertW — 2025-07-13T06:58:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spreading-ev-danger.31281/post-356242>

I read your book in Kindle format. While Jack’s content is still the go-to for the deepest technical details, I think you did a great job laying out all the most important points in an easily understandable manner.

My main complaint is that I was hoping to get some details on typical car construction and what components to pay attention to in terms of contributing the most in EMF magnitude

I’m curious what car you drive now. I was just in my Tesla today and realized there is massive amounts of RF coming from the rear view mirror area. In the book you talk about ac magnetic fields a lot. Perhaps you can avoid some of it with a gas car, but I’ve seen measurements of very high M fields even in gas cars because there are plenty of wiring runs where neutral isn’t run parallel to hot for 100% of the length. All modern cars have tons of electronics regardless of whether it is EV. I’ve thus seen that the Teslas actually tend to face pretty decent AC M fields compared to many gas cars, counterintuitively.

The RF part I would also assume is not specific to EVs, as plenty of cars are connected to cell networks, WiFi, Bluetooth, etc.

Is the only solution to buy a very old car with minimal electronics? What did you end up doing?

### AlbertW — 2025-07-13T17:32:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spreading-ev-danger.31281/post-356253>

I turned off WiFi. I don’t have any Bluetooth devices paired. Tesla also has a cell connection though and I’m not sure how to turn that off. I’ve seen some people online saying they needed to open the car up to remove some of the antennas.

All modern cars which have WiFi and BlueTooth are EMF hell holes. Think of it as having 4 or 5 cell phones on in the car with you.
Some ICE vehicles are worse than some EV’s because of their design and placement of the alternator, which can put high magnetic and electric field into the cabin In addition to the RF.

I would be surprised if you can turn all the WiFi/Bluetooth off in a Tesla - it what helps them work.
Always test drive a car with EMF meters BEFORE purchase.

### AlbertW — 2025-07-14T05:24:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spreading-ev-danger.31281/post-356265>

Is RF worse or the high magnetic fields? When I look at online measurements of gas cars, plenty of gasoline cars have AC magnetic fields well above 10 mG in many areas of the car, which is crazy high. I measured my Tesla today and it was generally in the 0.5-1 mG range, maxxing out at 1.5 mG. This seems incredibly low for any car, let alone EV.

Of course it’s best to get a car that’s low in every kind of nnEMF, but it seems it’s hard enough to find even a gasoline car with low AC magnetic field.

[https://www.ahappyhabitat.com/cars.html](https://www.ahappyhabitat.com/cars.html)

I wonder if it’s easier to modify an electric car to remove the biggest RF offenders or to modify a gasoline car to remove the AC magnetic fields.

Here’s a video showing low AC magnetic fields from Ford F150 Lightning (EV). It’s unfortunate they produce low LF EMF but typically can’t have their RF emissions shut off.

I recently purchased a new EV after test driving most brands and I was concerned about all the windows blocking uv and infra-red light and the extra large blue light toxic computer screen. Adding more electricity into my life is a worry but all modern cars are using more automated functions to the point they need an electrician as well as a mechanic to service them.
We are just in the car to go from a to b so we spend more time at home or work and kids are at school where there are electrical circuits, multiple powered devices and wifi servers equivalent to a mini cell tower. No matter where we are there are radio waves passing through our bodies.
Modern cars are 75% safer than older cars according to my insurance company but most cars have airbags or can be fitted with them. The driver is the most important safety asset if they are paying full attention and eyes on the road and with experience develop good habits and skills.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-11T20:49:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-got-sleep-apnea.31289/post-356188>

Check out this paper published in June of 2025. It fully supports my decentralized photo-bioelectric thesis in Decentralized Medicine #46 blog post. The paper reveals that peroxisomes act as "sinks" for mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (ROS) via membrane contacts, a process mediated by proteins like ACBD5 and PTPIP51, which increases during oxidative stress to maintain mitochondrial health.

This supports my idea that the GOE (~2.4–2.1 billion years ago), when rising oxygen from cyanobacteria shifted redox dynamics, drove the evolution of such adaptive mechanisms to manage oxygen’s "Holocaust" effects. The ROS transfer likely emerged as a response to intensified mitochondrial ROS production, to create UPEs to alter physiology paralleling my thesis that nature embedded resilience by enhancing function via hypoxia (breath holds) and UV-A light (via neuropsin and melanin), into cellular design. [https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adn2804](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adn2804)

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-11T20:59:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-got-sleep-apnea.31289/post-356189>

This finding strengthens my UPE hypothesis, as the redox signaling at these contacts could involve biophoton emissions during electron transitions, a process your thesis links to light-mediated communication. The GOE’s ozone layer formation, reducing UV-B/C while preserving UV-A, co-evolved with this redox balance, suggesting light-oxygen synergy need to be explore further by centralized science who remain clueless.

Additionally, the paper’s focus on oxidative stress aligns with my claims that intermittent hypoxia shifts redox states, were what optimized peroxisomal ROS uptake and mitochondrial function. This is a mechanism testable in my resilience model. While the paper lacks direct GOE evidence and focuses on mammals, it provides a modern cellular lens to infer ancient adaptations by first principle thinking, reinforcing my narrative that breath control and UV exposure unlock GOE-honed survival strategies for regeneration. I have integrated this as a GOE-evolved safeguard linked to the melanin reniovation of mammals because it amplifies light and breath, to deepen my thesis’s mechanistic and therapeutic implications.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/melanin-rx-for-80782201](https://www.patreon.com/posts/melanin-rx-for-80782201)
